pages.title: Back EMF and Winding Turns
pages.text:
# Back EMF and Winding Turns

Back EMF is the voltage a motor generates by itself as it rotates, like a generator. This voltage acts against the direction in which the driver is trying to push current into the winding, so current injection for torque becomes harder as speed rises.

A simple brushed DC motor model shows this relationship most directly.

$$
V \approx iR + Kb\omega
$$

Here, $V$ is the voltage applied by the driver, $iR$ is the voltage consumed by winding resistance, and $Kb\omega$ is the back EMF produced by rotational speed $\omega$. As the motor spins faster, $Kb\omega$ grows, leaving less voltage headroom for forcing current into the winding. This is why the motor operating region is not a simple maximum-torque by maximum-rpm rectangle; available torque falls at high speed.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 8; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 125-155.
# Why Back EMF Appears

A motor and a generator can be understood as the same electromagnetic structure used in opposite directions.
- As a motor: current through a coil creates force in the magnet field, so the rotor turns.
- As a generator: a coil moving through a magnet field produces voltage across the coil.

In a real spinning motor, both effects happen at the same time. The driver applies voltage to make the motor turn, while the rotating motor simultaneously generates voltage like a generator. Because this self-generated voltage appears opposite the driver voltage and resists current buildup, it is called "back" EMF.

For a 48 V driver:
- If back EMF is 10 V at low speed, the remaining voltage headroom can overcome winding resistance and inductance to build current.
- If back EMF is 45 V at high speed, only about 3 V remains, so it is difficult to force in the same torque-producing current.
- When back EMF approaches the driver voltage, the motor is near its no-load maximum speed and cannot produce large torque.

For this reason, a motor's "maximum rpm" is not an absolute property of the bare motor alone. It is the result of the winding, driver voltage, control method, and current limit together.
# Why More Winding Turns Increase the Back-EMF Constant

It helps to imagine one coil turn producing a small voltage as it moves through the magnet field. When the coil has many turns, the voltage generated by each turn adds in series.

For example, if one turn produces 0.1 V at a given speed:

| Winding turns | Generated voltage at the same speed |
|---:|---:|
| 1 turn | 0.1 V |
| 10 turns | about 1 V |
| 100 turns | about 10 V |

More turns therefore produce more back EMF at the same rotational speed. The constant that represents this relationship is the back-EMF constant, usually written $Ke$ or $Kb$.

$$
E{back} = Ke\omega
$$

High-turn windings therefore have larger $Ke$ and reach the voltage limit earlier for the same driver voltage. The resulting maximum speed is lower.
# The Torque Constant Increases for the Same Reason

More winding turns do not only increase back EMF. A current of 1 A flows through more active conductor segments, and the force produced by each segment in the magnet field adds together. This also increases the torque constant $Kt$.

$$
\tau = Kt I
$$

A high-turn winding produces more torque per ampere. The cost is that its back-EMF constant also rises, reducing voltage headroom at high speed. A low-turn winding is the opposite: it produces less torque per ampere, but it can reach higher speed at the same voltage.

| Winding choice | Low-speed torque view | High-speed voltage view | Driver match |
|---|---|---|---|
| More turns | Larger $Kt$, so less current is needed for torque | Larger $Ke$, so back EMF blocks high-speed operation earlier | Higher voltage, lower current |
| Fewer turns | Smaller $Kt$, so more current is needed for the same torque | Smaller $Ke$, so higher speed is possible | Lower voltage, higher current |

The winding explanation in Lecture 1 can be read this way. A high-turn, thin-wire winding matches a higher-voltage driver; a low-turn, thick-wire winding matches a higher-current driver. This means motor winding configuration is not a way to improve every physical limit of the motor for free. It is a way to match the same motor structure to the driver's voltage and current limits.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 20; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-174.
# Key Takeaways

Winding turn count is not a knob that independently improves both torque and speed. For the same motor structure, increasing turn count means:
- $Kt$ increases, so torque per ampere increases.
- $Ke$ also increases, so back EMF is larger at the same speed.
- The winding tends to become longer and thinner, increasing resistance.
- Maximum speed falls for the same driver voltage.

Reducing turn count moves in the opposite direction:
- $Kt$ decreases, so more current is needed for the same torque.
- $Ke$ decreases, so higher speed is possible at the same voltage.
- The winding tends to become shorter and thicker, reducing resistance.

When selecting a frameless motor, it is therefore misleading to look only at "how many watts" or "maximum rpm." The first questions should be the torque, mass, inertia, reduction ratio, and whether that combination can fit within the driver's voltage and current limits. This selection logic connects directly to actuator-performance-metrics.

pages.title: Backdrivability
pages.text:
# Backdrivability

Backdrivability is the ability to drive an actuator backward from the output side. In robot actuator discussions, the word is useful but ambiguous.

Lecture 4 explicitly separates three meanings:

Figure: Lecture 4 separates backdrivability into backward efficiency, Coulomb friction/reverse-drive starting torque, and reflected inertia. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.
# Meaning 1: Backward Efficiency

Forward efficiency measures how much input power becomes output power. Backward efficiency measures how well output-side mechanical power transmits back toward the motor.

These can differ. The lecture uses screw/bottle-cap intuition: turning the cap can move it axially, but pushing axially may not spin it backward. A mechanism can have nonzero forward efficiency and nearly zero backward efficiency.
# Meaning 2: Reverse-Drive Starting Torque

Reverse-drive starting torque is the output torque needed to start motion from rest. This is close to a Coulomb/static-friction idea: the output shaft may stay stuck until the applied external torque exceeds the static-friction threshold.

It depends on preload, tooth contact, bearings, seals, lubrication, and assembly. It is not the same as backward efficiency under motion, although both are friction-related.

For QDD/proprioceptive control, this meaning is often the most important: if friction torque is small enough, the motor current can be used as a good proxy for output torque.
# Meaning 3: Reflected Inertia

Sometimes people include output-reflected rotor inertia in "backdrivability." Lecture 4 recommends keeping it separate.

Reflected inertia is:

$$
J{out}=N^2Jr
$$

It affects impact response and dynamic motion, but it is not friction. This wiki uses backdrivability mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior and discusses inertia separately.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239 and 317-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.
# QDD Interpretation

The lecture clarifies a common misconception. QDD does not fundamentally mean "the reduction ratio must be small." It means the actuator should preserve the control idea of direct drive: output torque can be inferred without a dedicated torque sensor because friction and unmodeled transmission effects are small.

Low reduction ratio is a common way to achieve that, but not the only theoretical way. A high-ratio reducer with excellent low-friction design could still behave like a QDD actuator. In practice, high ratio makes this harder, so designers often impose a realistic reduction-ratio limit.
# Related

- transmission-transparency

pages.title: BLDC Electronic Commutation
pages.text:
# BLDC Electronic Commutation

Electronic commutation means switching stator winding currents with electronics instead of using mechanical brushes and a commutator.
# Brush-Type DC Motor vs BLDC

In a brush-type DC motor:
- brushes make sliding electrical contact
- a mechanical commutator switches which rotor coils receive current
- brush wear, dust, friction, electrical noise, and maintenance are real costs

In a BLDC motor:
- the windings are typically on the stator
- the rotor usually carries permanent magnets
- a motor driver switches phase currents electronically
- rotor position sensing or estimation tells the driver when to switch

Figure: BLDC rotor magnets and sensor IC arrangement. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 1.

Honeywell describes BLDC motors as brush-type motors "turned inside out": power goes to stationary windings, while the permanent-magnet rotor rotates.
# Six-Step Intuition

In a common three-phase six-step BLDC drive:
- two phases are energized at a time
- one phase is left off/floating
- the active phase pair creates a stator magnetic field
- the driver changes the active pair as the rotor moves, using rotor-position feedback or estimation
- the rotor magnets keep moving toward the next stator field orientation

Beginner analogy: the controller keeps creating the next magnetic "target" slightly ahead of the rotor, so the rotor keeps chasing it.
# What the Driver Needs

The motor driver needs rotor position or rotor sector information to energize the correct winding. It can get that from:
- Hall effect sensors
- encoders
- sensorless estimation from electrical signals

Figure: simplified three-phase BLDC driver circuit using six power transistors and Hall-effect sensor IC feedback. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 4.

This is the control-facing reason why motor-actuator-structure says BLDC/SPMSM-style motors need a driver and commutation logic. For the broader distinction between six-step, trapezoidal, sinusoidal, and FOC language, see pmsm-control-waveform-language.
# Relation to Winding

Electronic commutation determines when current goes through each phase. Winding design determines the motor's voltage/current matching and electromagnetic constraints. See bldc-pmsm-spmsm for the naming distinction between BLDC/PMSM/SPMSM.
# Related

- hall-effect-position-sensing
- bldc-pmsm-spmsm
- pmsm-control-waveform-language
- motor-actuator-structure

pages.title: BLDC Electronic Commutation — ChatGPT Notes
pages.text:
# BLDC Electronic Commutation — ChatGPT Notes

Source: sources/chatgpt-shares/BLDC Electronic Commutation - ChatGPT notes.md - user-provided shared ChatGPT conversation about BLDC commutation and Hall sensors.
# Main Questions Covered
- What is an electronic commutation circuit?
- What are DC and AC?
- How can a BLDC controller continuously switch windings?
- What is winding?
- What are BLDC motor sensor ICs?
- What are brushes and commutators?
- How does the controller know rotor position?
- Why does a BLDC rotor usually use permanent magnets rather than electromagnets?
- What is a Hall sensor?
- How do Hall signals map to coil switching?
# Useful Explanation Captured

The final ChatGPT explanation frames BLDC six-step commutation as:
- Hall sensor signals identify rotor sector.
- Controller chooses which two of three phases are energized.
- One phase is positive, one negative, and one off in a common six-step scheme.
- The energized stator field is moved ahead of the rotor.
- The rotor permanent magnets keep "chasing" the next stator field position.

This is a useful beginner analogy for bldc-electronic-commutation, but should be treated as a learning note rather than an authoritative source.

For the broader split between six-step, trapezoidal, sinusoidal, and FOC language, use pmsm-control-waveform-language. For the field intuition behind "the rotor chases the stator field," use three-phase-rotating-magnetic-field.

pages.title: BLDC, PMSM, and SPMSM Terminology
pages.text:
# BLDC, PMSM, and SPMSM Terminology

Use this page only as the naming map. It answers what each label names, which labels are topology labels, and how to read loose BLDC language in robot-actuator notes.

The compact rule is:
- PMSM names the permanent-magnet synchronous machine family.
- SPMSM and IPMSM name rotor topologies inside that family.
- BLDC is a loose product/control label, not a clean physical topology class.

In this wiki, if a source says "BLDC motor" but the hardware is a brushless permanent-magnet actuator with an inverter drive, the safe default is to read it as an SPMSM unless the source is explicitly discussing six-step commutation, trapezoidal back EMF, Hall-sector control, or product naming.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 36-42; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - transcript.md, lines 486-494.
# Term Map

| Term | Names what | Does not specify | Safe reading in this wiki |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| BLDC | A brushless permanent-magnet motor label used in products, drive notes, and beginner explanations | Rotor magnet placement, exact torque mechanism, exact phase-current waveform | Usually casual language for a surface-PM brushless motor plus electronic commutation; do not infer topology from the label alone |
| PMSM | The permanent-magnet synchronous machine family | Surface vs interior magnets | Umbrella term for SPMSM and IPMSM |
| SPMSM | Surface-mounted rotor magnets | Drive waveform | Common robot-actuator topology; often the hardware behind casual "BLDC" naming |
| IPMSM | Interior rotor magnets | Drive waveform | Interior magnets plus saliency; can produce both magnet torque and reluctance torque |

Strict topology view:

text
PMSM
├── SPMSM (surface-mounted rotor magnets)
└── IPMSM (interior rotor magnets)

BLDC is omitted from the strict tree because it mixes product history, back-EMF assumptions, and commutation language. It can be useful in a driver discussion, but it is not the clean label to use for rotor geometry.

Outrunner and inrunner language is a different axis again. It describes whether the outer bell or the inner shaft is the rotating part, not whether the motor is BLDC or PMSM, and not whether the magnet topology is SPMSM or IPMSM. Use outrunner-vs-inrunner-bldc-motors when the source is really comparing outer-rotor versus inner-rotor packaging, especially in drone propeller selection.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner BLDC Motor vs Inner Rotor BLDC Motor.md, mechanical-structure comparison; sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, structural-differences section.
# Textbook Taxonomy Check

The Electric Motor Control textbook shows why the naming is confusing. In its classification figure, "Brushless DC" appears near the DC/AC boundary while the permanent-magnet synchronous branch appears under AC motors; the same figure also annotates the permanent-magnet synchronous branch with "BLAC motor." This supports the wiki rule: BLDC, BLAC, and PMSM language often mixes waveform/control naming with physical topology.

Figure: motor classification tree showing brushless DC near the DC/AC boundary and permanent-magnet synchronous machines under AC motors. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2, Fig. 1.1.

Chapter 4 then treats SPMSM, IPMSM, PM-assisted SynRM, and SynRM as rotor/topology categories on a spectrum from magnet torque to reluctance torque. That is the topology language to prefer when discussing d-q torque, saliency, or actuator selection.

Figure: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM arranged from reluctance-torque-dominant to magnet-torque-dominant. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 3.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 12-14.
# Why BLDC Feels Like DC

From the user's power-terminal viewpoint, a BLDC actuator often looks like a DC product:

text
DC battery or DC bus
 -> inverter / motor driver
 -> switched three-phase phase currents
 -> rotating stator field
 -> permanent-magnet rotor follows

That does not mean the motor windings simply receive constant DC. The DC bus is the energy source before the inverter; the phase currents and stator field are actively switched or modulated by the driver. See three-phase-rotating-magnetic-field for the field intuition and pmsm-control-waveform-language for six-step, sinusoidal, and FOC naming.

The clean mental model is:

text
BLDC: how the product or drive is often described
PMSM: what kind of synchronous PM machine it is
SPMSM/IPMSM: where the rotor magnets are placed
# Practical Reading Rules
- Use SPMSM/IPMSM when the source is about rotor geometry, torque generation, d-q models, saliency, or actuator selection.
- Use BLDC when the source is deliberately talking about six-step or block commutation, trapezoidal back EMF, Hall-sector feedback, or casual product naming.
- Use outrunner/inrunner when the source is about rotor packaging, motor shape, torque radius, rotor inertia, cooling path, or propeller/ducted-fan fit.
- Do not infer waveform from topology or topology from waveform label alone. The same physical motor can be driven with six-step/block commutation or sinusoidal/FOC control if the driver and feedback support it.
- When the source moves from naming into MTPA, negative d-axis current, or wide constant-power speed range, switch to pmsm-topology-control-consequences instead of keeping that discussion on this taxonomy page.

> WARNING
> "BLDC is DC" and "PMSM is AC" are useful shortcuts, but they are not clean taxonomy. A brushless robot motor still needs an inverter/driver, rotor-position information or estimation, and commutation or current-control logic.
# Unified Lecture Reading

The lectures are not making different claims; they are layering the same naming correction at different abstraction levels.

| Lecture | Role in the unified picture |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | Actuator-level intuition: the robot motor people casually call BLDC is better read as SPMSM because the driver needs rotor position to commutate current and make torque in the desired direction. |
| 2 | Formal taxonomy and model: SPMSM is the surface-magnet topology used in robotics, IPMSM adds saliency and reluctance torque, and SynRM has no magnets. |
| 4 | Design-language correction: the same motor is more accurately SPMSM, while BLDC has become an overloaded industry/paper label that can hide the actual topology. |

Put together, this wiki should read BLDC as a loose control/product label and SPMSM as the physical rotor topology. Use BLDC only when the source is explicitly talking about six-step commutation, trapezoidal back EMF, Hall-sector sensing, or casual naming.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - transcript.md, line 395; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 36-42, 132-141, 179, 189; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - transcript.md, lines 486-494.

pages.title: Coulomb and Static Friction
pages.text:
# Coulomb and Static Friction

In backdrivability discussions, the Coulomb/static-friction idea means that a joint can resist motion even before inertia becomes the main issue. A small external torque may not move the output shaft at all; the joint only starts moving after the applied torque exceeds a friction threshold.

For a robot joint pushed from the output side:
- a weak push produces no motion
- a stronger push may still be held by static friction
- above a threshold, the joint breaks free and starts moving
- after motion begins, friction continues to oppose the direction of travel

That threshold is closely related to reverse-drive starting torque or breakaway torque.
# Static Friction

Static friction is the friction available while the joint is still at rest. It does not have one fixed direction by itself; it reacts against the attempted motion up to a maximum value.

A simple output-side model is:

$$
|\tau{\text{external}}| < \tau{\text{static,max}}
$$

If this is true, the joint can remain stuck. To start reverse-driving the joint:

$$
|\tau{\text{external}}| > \tau{\text{static,max}}
$$

This is why a reducer can feel "sticky": nothing happens at first, then it suddenly starts moving once the static-friction threshold is exceeded.
# Coulomb Friction

Coulomb friction is the simple model often used after sliding or rotation has begun. It treats friction torque as roughly constant in magnitude and opposite the direction of motion:

$$
\tau{\text{friction}} \approx -\tauc \, \operatorname{sign}(\dot{q})
$$

Here $q$ is joint position, $\dot{q}$ is joint velocity, and $\operatorname{sign}(\dot{q})$ keeps only the direction of the velocity:

$$
\operatorname{sign}(\dot{q}) =
\begin{cases}
+1 & \dot{q} > 0 \\
0 & \dot{q} = 0 \\
-1 & \dot{q} < 0
\end{cases}
$$

So if the joint moves in the positive direction, Coulomb friction acts in the negative direction. If the joint moves in the negative direction, Coulomb friction acts in the positive direction. The important simplification is that the friction magnitude is about $\tauc$; it is not mainly proportional to speed.
# Not Viscous Friction, Not Reflected Inertia

Coulomb/static friction can feel similar to other forms of passive impedance, but the causes differ:
- Static friction: the joint will not start moving until a threshold torque is exceeded.
- Coulomb friction: once moving, the joint continues to feel a roughly constant opposing torque.
- Viscous friction: the opposing torque grows with speed, often modeled as $\tau{\text{viscous}}=-b\dot{q}$.
- Reflected inertia: the joint can move, but it resists acceleration because motor or transmission inertia is reflected to the output.

This distinction matters because backdrivability is often used loosely. For actuator design, it is clearer to separate friction/reverse-drive behavior from reflected inertia.
# Why It Matters for QDD

In QDD and proprioceptive force control, low Coulomb/static friction helps motor current remain a useful proxy for output torque. If internal friction is large, part of the motor torque is spent overcoming the reducer instead of producing external contact force. The controller then sees a less direct relationship between motor current and output torque.

Lecture 4 uses backdrivability terminology to separate backward efficiency, Coulomb-friction/reverse-drive starting torque, and reflected inertia. In that framing, the Coulomb/static-friction idea explains why a joint may need a noticeable output torque before it starts moving backward at all.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239 and 317-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.
# Web References
- Engineering LibreTexts, "Dry Friction," https://eng.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/MechanicalEngineering/EngineeringStatics%3AOpenandInteractive%28BakerandHaynes%29/09%3AFriction/9.01%3AFriction/9.1.01%3ADryFriction, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.
- "An improved Coulomb friction model for servo mechanisms," https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12788085/, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.
- Springs Fastener, "Breakaway Torque," https://www.springsfastener.com/resources/glossary/breakaway-torque, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.
- maxon Support, "Gears: Self-locking or back-drivability," https://support.maxongroup.com/hc/en-us/articles/5881942527132-Gears-Self-locking-or-back-drivability, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.

pages.title: DC Motor Control
pages.text:
# DC Motor Control

DC motors are a useful minimal model for understanding motor-operating-region, back-EMF, current control, and PWM drives before moving to three-phase PMSM control. The same structure appears repeatedly in robot actuator notes: voltage creates current, current creates torque, motion creates back-EMF, and the driver must respect voltage and current limits.
# Dynamic Model

The textbook models a DC motor drive with four coupled equations:

$$
Va = Ra ia + La\frac{d ia}{dt} + ea
$$

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

$$
Te = kT\phif ia
$$

$$
Te = J\frac{d\omegam}{dt} + B\omegam + TL
$$

Here $Va$ is armature voltage, $ia$ is armature current, $Ra$ and $La$ are armature resistance and inductance, $ea$ is back-EMF, $\phif$ is field flux, $\omegam$ is mechanical angular speed, $J$ is inertia, $B$ is viscous friction, and $TL$ is load torque. In the textbook's SI convention, $kT=ke$.

Figure: equivalent circuit for the armature equation and moving-conductor back-EMF. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 5, Figs. 2.5-2.6.

This is why a DC motor feels like the scalar version of a PMSM q-axis model. Back-EMF subtracts from the voltage available for current, and current is the torque-producing quantity.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-6.
# Speed-Torque Curve

At steady state, set $dia/dt=0$ and $d\omegam/dt=0$. Neglecting viscous friction, the textbook gives:

$$
\omegam = \frac{Va}{k\phif} - \frac{Ra}{(k\phif)^2}TL
$$

The slope is negative: as load torque increases, the armature current and resistive voltage drop increase, leaving less voltage margin for back-EMF and speed. This is the DC-motor version of the high-speed torque limit in motor-operating-region.
# Speed Control Regions

Below base speed, a DC motor normally uses armature voltage control. The field flux is held constant, and speed changes roughly linearly with armature voltage. Torque is controlled by armature current, so the same current can produce the same torque across this region.

Above base speed, the terminal voltage has reached its limit. A wound-field DC motor can reduce field flux to keep back-EMF manageable and extend speed. This is field-flux control or field weakening. Torque per ampere falls as flux is reduced, so the high-speed region is approximately a constant-power region rather than a constant-torque region. A permanent-magnet DC motor cannot use this field-winding method.

Figure: DC motor constant-torque and constant-power capability curve. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 11, Fig. 2.11.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 7-11.
# Cascaded Control Structure

A motor control system commonly nests loops from fastest to slowest:
- current loop inside the drive
- speed loop around current
- position loop around speed

The current loop must have the widest bandwidth because torque depends directly on current. The speed and position loops can be slower because mechanical states cannot change as quickly as electrical current.

For a DC current loop with back-EMF feedforward and pole-zero cancellation, the textbook gives:

$$
K{pc}=La\omega{cc}
$$

$$
K{ic}=Ra\omega{cc}
$$

where $\omega{cc}$ is the desired current-control bandwidth. The gains depend on the motor's armature inductance and resistance, so the same numeric controller gains do not transfer cleanly between motors.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 19 and 31-34.
# Bandwidth and Anti-Windup

The current-loop bandwidth is limited by switching frequency and current sampling. The textbook rule of thumb is up to about one-tenth of switching frequency when current is sampled twice per switching period, and lower when sampled once per period. It also recommends keeping the bandwidth below about one twenty-fifth of the sampling frequency in the once-per-period case.

Integrator windup appears when a PI controller keeps integrating error while the voltage command is already saturated. Back-calculation anti-windup feeds the difference between the pre-limit and post-limit voltage command back into the integrator path. The textbook gives the common anti-windup gain choice:

$$
Ka = \frac{1}{K{pc}}
$$

This is the DC-motor version of the same saturation problem handled in motor-current-control for PMSM drives.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 34-36.
# H-Bridge PWM

A four-quadrant chopper, or H-bridge, lets a DC motor operate in forward/reverse motoring and braking. The textbook describes two PWM schemes:
- Bipolar switching: diagonal switch pairs alternate, so the motor voltage jumps between $+V{dc}$ and $-V{dc}$. This gives smooth average-voltage control but larger current ripple.
- Unipolar switching: the two bridge legs are modulated separately, so the motor sees $0$ and $+V{dc}$ or $0$ and $-V{dc}$. This reduces current and torque ripple, but the average-voltage sign transition can be less smooth.

Figure: H-bridge circuit for four-quadrant DC motor operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 45, Fig. 2.47.

Figure: unipolar H-bridge switching waveforms and current ripple comparison. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 47, Figs. 2.50-2.51.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 45-47.

pages.title: Design Intentions and Contributions
pages.text:
# Design Intentions and Contributions

In robot hardware, a design intention is the reason a concrete pre-build decision exists. A contribution is the retrospective claim made after the robot works. The two are related, but confusing them makes early hardware design feel more certain than it really is.

Design intentions answer questions like:
- Where should this bolt, bearing, actuator, wire, or service access path go?
- How modular, manufacturable, and serviceable does the design need to be?
- What simple mechanism choices will keep control, modeling, and assembly tractable?

Contributions answer questions like:
- What can this robot now demonstrate?
- What performance region did the hardware open?
- What academic story can be defended with experiments?

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 37-59.
# Why the Distinction Matters

Requirement selection and Pareto-front reasoning can make actuator design look like a clean optimization problem. Mechanism development is messier. Many high-impact decisions are local and practical: hole placement, connector access, part count, assembly order, machining feasibility, wiring route, and whether one actuator family can be reused.

Those decisions may not be publishable by themselves, but they decide whether the robot becomes a testable platform. The RAIPAL lecture argues that hardware engineers spend most of their time on these design intentions, while contribution language often appears only after the system works.
# RAIPAL Example

RAIPAL's major design intentions were control transparency, engineering simplicity, and streamlined symmetry:
- use QDD-like, transparent actuation for easier control and lower sim-to-real gap
- avoid heavily coupled mechanisms that make the dynamics hard to model
- reuse actuator and electronics subcomponents where possible
- keep the platform compatible with high-bandwidth motor drivers and RL-oriented rollout workflows

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 5; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 63-75.

At the mechanism level, "make it light and not broken" was essential but not enough as an academic contribution. The speaker therefore describes adding at least one mechanism-level hook and biasing small design choices toward dynamic capability where design freedom allowed. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 6; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 81-103.
# Contributions Often Emerge Late

RAIPAL's retrospective contribution framing shifted toward "human-level dynamic range." The lecture connects this to the lower-than-expected forearm gear ratio and successful dynamic demonstrations, but it presents that framing as something found after competent engineering, not as a fully visible claim at the blank-page stage. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 29-31; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 96-147.

That does not mean contribution planning is useless. It means early contribution pressure should not force a complicated mechanism before basic engineering quality exists. A robust platform with transparent actuation, accurate digital interface, and credible demonstrations gives more opportunities for contribution framing than an over-specialized mechanism that cannot be built or tested.
# Practical Use

When starting a robot hardware design, write intentions in engineering language first:
- "The controller should see near-linear, low-coupling dynamics."
- "The same actuator package should repeat where possible."
- "The drawing should communicate which surfaces and fits are critical."
- "The URDF mass and rotor inertia should match the physical assembly closely enough for RL."

Then let contribution candidates remain hypotheses until iteration, production, and demonstrations reveal what the hardware can actually do.

pages.title: Difference Between BLDC Motor and PMSM Motor
pages.text:
# Difference Between BLDC Motor and PMSM Motor

Source: sources/web-clippings/Difference Between BLDC Motor and PMSM Motor.md - GeeksforGeeks clipping, "Difference Between BLDC Motor and PMSM Motor."
# Main Points from Source
- BLDC = brushless direct current motor.
- PMSM = permanent magnet synchronous motor.
- Both use a permanent-magnet rotor and stator windings.
- BLDC is described as using electronic commutation from a DC supply.
- PMSM is described as using AC rotating magnetic field/synchronous operation.
- The source compares BLDC and PMSM by torque, efficiency, torque ripple, control complexity, cost, and applications.
# Useful Takeaways
- The practical distinction often appears in control and waveform language:
- BLDC is commonly associated with electronic commutation and trapezoidal/six-step drive.
- PMSM/SPMSM topology terminology is commonly associated with sinusoidal current and field-oriented/vector control, but the control labels belong on pmsm-control-waveform-language.
- Both require an electronic drive/inverter/controller in real robot actuator usage.
- PMSM/SPMSM terminology is usually more precise when discussing motor topology, while BLDC is commonly used as casual industry/hobby terminology.

> WARNING
> This clipping frames BLDC as "DC motor" and PMSM as "AC synchronous motor." That is common beginner-facing wording, but it can be misleading. In practice, many motors called BLDC are also permanent-magnet synchronous machines driven by an inverter; the important distinction is often drive waveform/control strategy and back-EMF shape rather than a completely separate physical category.

pages.title: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
pages.text:
# ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian

This lecture introduces the Jacobian as the derivative of the forward kinematic map and then develops its main uses: velocity mapping, singularity analysis, manipulability, force mapping, and space/body Jacobians.
# Main Flow

The lecture starts from forward kinematics $x=f(\theta)$ and differentiates it:

$$
\dot{x}=\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}\dot{\theta}.
$$

The matrix $\partial f/\partial \theta$ is the Jacobian. A two-link planar arm is used to show the concrete matrix form and the interpretation of Jacobian columns as end-effector velocity directions.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 2-5.
# Velocity, Manipulability, And Singularities

The lecture maps bounded joint velocities through $J(\theta)$ to obtain bounded end-effector velocity sets. A square joint-velocity limit becomes a parallelogram in the two-link example, while a disk becomes a manipulability ellipsoid.

A flat ellipsoid indicates a posture close to singularity: some end-effector directions are unavailable or require large joint velocities. For the two-link example, $\theta2=0$ is explicitly identified as singular.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 5-7.
# Force Mapping

The lecture derives the joint-torque relation from conservation of power:

$$
\tau=J^T(\theta)F.
$$

It also shows the reciprocal view: with joint-torque limits and invertible $J^T$, one can plot the end-effector forces the robot can counteract using $(J^T)^{-1}\tau$.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 8-9.
# PoE Space And Body Jacobians

The second half of the lecture derives the space Jacobian from product-of-exponentials forward kinematics:

$$
T(\theta)=e^{[S1]\theta1}e^{[S2]\theta2}\cdots e^{[Sn]\thetan}M.
$$

Using adjoint maps, each space-Jacobian column is the corresponding screw vector expressed in fixed-frame coordinates after accounting for earlier joints. The lecture then derives the body Jacobian and relates the two by:

$$
Jb(\theta)=\operatorname{Ad}{T{bs}}Js(\theta),
$$

$$
Js(\theta)=\operatorname{Ad}{T{sb}}Jb(\theta).
$$

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 10-23.

- jacobian-singularities
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping
- space-and-body-jacobians
- jacobian-inverse-kinematics

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Electric Motors

Chapter 1 is the textbook foundation for motor vocabulary used throughout the robot hardware wiki. It connects machine taxonomy, magnetic energy conversion, load dynamics, four-quadrant operation, and drive-system architecture. Read it as background for motor-actuator-structure, bldc-pmsm-spmsm, motional-emf-and-flux-linkage, magnetic-reluctance-torque, and motor-operating-region.
# Scope

The chapter makes five durable points:
- Electric motors are prime movers that convert electrical energy into mechanical torque.
- Motor taxonomy is not perfectly clean: brushless DC is listed near DC motors because of its DC-like drive characteristics, but its configuration is close to a permanent-magnet synchronous AC motor.
- Useful torque normally comes from the interaction between two magnetic fields, although reluctance motors exploit the interaction between a field and a magnetic material.
- Motor selection is coupled to load inertia, load torque type, transmission ratio, and operating quadrant.
- A drive system is motor plus source, converter, controller, sensors, and mechanical load, not the bare electromagnetic machine alone.

Figure: textbook motor classification tree showing DC, AC, brushless DC, synchronous PM, induction, reluctance, and related subtypes. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2, Fig. 1.1.
# Motor Structure and Torque Production

The chapter starts from the two basic motor parts: the stator is stationary, the rotor moves, and the air gap lets the rotor spin while carrying magnetic flux between the two sides. Stator and rotor each have magnetic material and electrical conductors or magnets. Current in conductors makes magnetic fields; permanent magnets can also supply field flux.

The simplest torque story is alignment. One magnetic field on the stator and one on the rotor pull toward an energetically favorable relative angle. In a brushed DC motor, brushes and the commutator keep the rotor current distribution effectively fixed in space even while the physical rotor turns. In an AC motor, the stator field rotates, and the rotor field must rotate with it to keep producing torque.

Reluctance torque is a different but related mechanism: the rotor moves toward the position that reduces magnetic reluctance. The chapter treats this as usually lower torque than two-field interaction, but this idea becomes important later for SynRM, IPMSM, and salient-pole machines.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 3-7.
# Magnetic Energy, Flux Linkage, and Reluctance

Chapter 1 introduces the magnetic quantities that reappear in motor modeling:

$$
\lambda = N\phi
$$

$$
L = \frac{\lambda}{i}
$$

$$
L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}}
$$

Here $\lambda$ is flux linkage, $N$ is turns, $\phi$ is magnetic flux, $L$ is inductance, $i$ is current, and $\mathcal{R}$ is magnetic reluctance. The key design intuition is that a longer air gap increases reluctance and lowers inductance, so the same current produces less flux linkage.

The magnetization curve is also important. In the unsaturated region, flux is roughly proportional to current and inductance is approximately constant. Near and above the knee of the curve, added current produces diminishing flux. Since motor torque depends on flux and current, saturation is where simple linear motor models begin to over-predict torque.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 9-12.
# Mechanical Load Model

The mechanical side is modeled with Newton's law. For a rotating load with constant inertia:

$$
TM - TL = J\frac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

The chapter then adds viscous friction and load torque:

$$
TM = J\frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + TL
$$

This equation is the textbook version of the robot-actuator idea that motor torque must cover acceleration torque, friction, and external load torque. The same section classifies common load types:
- constant torque loads such as elevators and conveyors
- constant power loads where $TL \propto \frac{1}{\omega}$
- fan/pump loads where $TL \propto \omega^2$

For a translational mass driven by a pulley radius $r$, the load reflects to the motor shaft as:

$$
Je = Mr^2
$$

For gears, reflected inertia and friction scale with the square of the gear ratio. This is the textbook reason reducer ratio affects actuator feel and acceleration so strongly.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 27-32.
# Operating Quadrants

The chapter defines motoring and generating by the direction of torque relative to speed. If torque and speed have the same sign, energy flows from the electrical side into the mechanical load. If torque opposes speed, the motor is braking or generating, so mechanical energy is returned to or dissipated by the electrical side.

This gives the four-quadrant map used by drive engineers:
- Quadrant 1: forward motoring, $+T$, $+\omega$
- Quadrant 2: forward braking/generating, $-T$, $+\omega$
- Quadrant 3: reverse motoring, $-T$, $-\omega$
- Quadrant 4: reverse braking/generating, $+T$, $-\omega$

For robot hardware, this matters because a joint motor is not only an accelerator. It also has to brake, hold, recover energy, or dissipate energy safely depending on the mechanical task.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 32-34.
# Drive-System View

Chapter 1 ends by framing a motor drive as a system: source, converter, controller, motor, sensors, and load. This is the most useful bridge to robot actuators. The power electronic converter adapts the available source to the motor, the controller chooses current/voltage/frequency commands, and sensors provide current, speed, or position feedback.

Figure: source, power electronic converter, digital controller, sensors, motor, and mechanical load as one motor-drive system. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 35, Fig. 1.32.

The chapter also gives practical component tradeoffs. DC motors are simpler to control but have brush/commutator maintenance issues. AC motors are generally smaller, lighter, higher power density, and more maintenance-free, but high-performance AC drives need more complex converters and control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 35-37.
# Wiki Integration
- Next chapter: chapter-02-dc-motor-control turns the basic motor-drive picture into the scalar DC motor model and current/speed control loop.
- motor-actuator-structure uses this chapter for the motor-drive-system block view.
- bldc-pmsm-spmsm uses the classification tree as evidence that "BLDC" mixes topology and drive/control language.
- magnetic-reluctance-torque uses the chapter's field/material torque explanation as the entry point for reluctance torque.
- motional-emf-and-flux-linkage uses the flux-linkage and inductance definitions.
- torque-mass-inertia-selection and motor-operating-region connect the load-dynamics and four-quadrant material to robot actuator selection.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 2: Control of Direct Current Motors

Chapter 2 uses the DC motor as a compact model for motor dynamics and control. It is the cleanest textbook source for armature voltage, back-EMF, torque constant, current-loop design, anti-windup, speed-loop nesting, and H-bridge PWM. Read it before dc-motor-control, motor-operating-region, motor-current-control, and motor-driver-inverter.
# Scope

The chapter builds from machine model to controller implementation:
- brushed DC motor construction and commutation
- armature circuit, back-EMF, torque, and load equations
- speed-torque curve and voltage/field speed control
- constant-torque and constant-power operating regions
- cascaded current, speed, and position control
- PI current-controller gain selection and bandwidth limits
- anti-windup for voltage saturation
- four-quadrant H-bridge PWM with bipolar and unipolar switching
# DC Motor Model

The chapter models a DC motor drive with four coupled equations:

$$
Va = Ra ia + La\frac{d ia}{dt} + ea
$$

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

$$
Te = kT\phif ia
$$

$$
Te = J\frac{d\omegam}{dt} + B\omegam + TL
$$

In the textbook's SI convention, $kT=ke$. This is the scalar model behind the intuition that voltage creates current, current creates torque, and speed creates back-EMF that reduces the voltage left to drive current.

Figure: DC motor equivalent circuit and moving-conductor back-EMF sketch. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 5, Figs. 2.5-2.6.

The torque equation is simple because the rotor current conductors are kept perpendicular to the stator flux by commutation. That is why the chapter says DC motors inherently have a maximum-torque-per-ampere characteristic.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-6.
# Steady-State Speed Control

In steady state, with friction neglected, the speed-torque relation is:

$$
\omegam = \frac{Va}{k\phif} - \frac{Ra}{(k\phif)^2}TL
$$

This gives the standard downward-sloping speed-torque curve. Increasing load torque requires more armature current, which increases resistive voltage drop and lowers available speed.

Two speed-control methods follow directly:
- Armature voltage control: hold field flux constant and vary $Va$. This is the normal below-base-speed method and produces a constant-torque region.
- Field flux control: hold terminal voltage at its limit and reduce field flux. This extends speed above base speed, but torque per amp falls, speed regulation worsens, and the response is slow because the field winding has large inductance.

Permanent-magnet DC motors cannot use field-flux control through a field winding, so they cannot use this textbook field-weakening method.

Figure: DC motor constant-torque and constant-power capability curve. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 11, Fig. 2.11.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 7-11.
# Cascaded Control

The chapter's control architecture is current inside speed inside position. Torque is expensive to measure directly, but under constant field flux it is proportional to current, so current control is treated as torque control.

The current loop must have the widest bandwidth. The speed loop should be slower than current control, and position should be slower than speed. This is the same nesting used later in PMSM drives: fast electrical control inside slower mechanical control.

The chapter also contrasts open-loop and closed-loop control. Open-loop control depends on a good model and cannot reject back-EMF or load-torque disturbances reliably. High-performance motor drives therefore use feedback for current, speed, or position.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 18-24.
# PI Current Controller

The chapter designs a PI current loop around the armature $R$-$L$ plant. Back-EMF acts as a disturbance; for low-inertia servo motors, the text recommends feedforward compensation using speed information so the current loop sees a simpler $R$-$L$ circuit.

With pole-zero cancellation and a desired current-loop bandwidth $\omega{cc}$:

$$
K{pc}=La\omega{cc}
$$

$$
K{ic}=Ra\omega{cc}
$$

The gains depend on motor parameters. A motor with larger inductance needs a larger proportional gain for the same current bandwidth.

Bandwidth is constrained by converter switching frequency and current sampling. The textbook rule of thumb is up to about one-tenth of switching frequency when sampling twice per switching period, up to about one-twentieth when sampling once per switching period, and preferably below about one twenty-fifth of sampling frequency in the once-per-period case.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 29-34.
# Anti-Windup

The current controller outputs a voltage reference, but the motor and converter impose voltage limits. If the PI output saturates, the integrator can keep accumulating error even though the plant cannot receive the requested voltage. The chapter describes this as integral windup, which causes overshoot and long settling.

The standard fix is back-calculation anti-windup: feed the difference between the unlimited controller output and the saturated output back into the integrator path. The textbook gives the common gain choice:

$$
Ka = \frac{1}{K{pc}}
$$

This maps directly to PMSM current control, where d-q voltage commands also saturate against the DC bus.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 35-36.
# H-Bridge PWM

The chapter's four-quadrant converter is the H-bridge. Four switching devices let the drive apply positive or negative voltage across the armature, enabling forward/reverse motoring and braking.

Figure: H-bridge circuit for four-quadrant DC motor operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 45, Fig. 2.47.

The chapter compares two PWM methods:
- Bipolar switching: diagonal pairs switch together, so the armature voltage alternates between $+V{dc}$ and $-V{dc}$. The average voltage changes smoothly across zero, but current ripple is large because the voltage swing is wide.
- Unipolar switching: the two bridge legs are modulated separately, so the motor sees $0$ and $+V{dc}$ or $0$ and $-V{dc}$. Current and torque ripple are smaller, but very small average voltages near sign changes can be harder to realize smoothly.

Figure: unipolar H-bridge switching waveforms and current ripple comparison. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 47, Figs. 2.50-2.51.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 45-47.
# Wiki Integration
- Previous/next: chapter-01-fundamentals supplies the motor-drive-system foundation, and chapter-03-ac-motors carries the operating-region and rotating-field ideas into AC machines.
- dc-motor-control holds the durable model, operating regions, current-loop design, and H-bridge notes.
- motor-operating-region uses the DC capability curve as the intuitive scalar case.
- motor-current-control uses the PI, feedforward, bandwidth, and anti-windup structure as the bridge to PMSM d-q current control.
- motor-driver-inverter uses the H-bridge as the simpler two-leg version of inverter switching.
- resistive-braking connects to the braking/generating quadrants.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 3: Alternating-Current Motors

Chapter 3 explains steady-state AC motor behavior, with most of the chapter devoted to induction motors and a final section on synchronous motors. It is useful background for induction-motor-basics, electrical-angle, motor-operating-region, bldc-pmsm-spmsm, and magnetic-reluctance-torque.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- induction-motor structure: stator, squirrel-cage rotor, wound rotor
- rotating magnetic field and synchronous speed
- slip, rotor frequency, equivalent circuit, and power flow
- torque-speed behavior, rotor resistance effects, and speed control
- induction motor constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions
- synchronous motor steady-state model, torque angle, saliency, and starting
# Induction Motor Principle

An induction motor creates torque without externally powering the rotor. Balanced three-phase stator currents produce a rotating air-gap field. That field cuts the rotor conductors, induces EMF, drives rotor current through the shorted rotor circuit, and the rotor current interacting with the field produces force.

The field speed is the synchronous speed:

$$
ns=\frac{120f}{P}\quad\text{r/min}
$$

where $f$ is stator electrical frequency and $P$ is pole count. The rotor must run below this speed for motoring torque. If the rotor caught the rotating field exactly, there would be no changing flux in the rotor, no induced rotor voltage, no rotor current, and no induction torque.

Slip is:

$$
s = \frac{ns-n}{ns}
$$

A typical full-load slip range stated in the chapter is about 1-5%.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 5-10 and 15-16.
# Structure and Winding Notes

The stator uses three-phase windings displaced by $120^\circ$ electrical. Chapter 3 notes that induction motor stator phases are usually connected in delta for low supply voltage and wye for high supply voltage. Distributed windings are used to approximate a sinusoidal air-gap mmf and reduce torque ripple compared with concentrated windings.

The most common rotor is the squirrel-cage rotor, where bars are short-circuited by end rings. The chapter states that about 95% of induction motors use this rotor because it is simple and rugged. Wound-rotor machines expose rotor windings through slip rings, allowing external rotor resistance to shape starting and torque-speed behavior.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 3-5.
# Equivalent Circuit and Power Flow

The steady-state equivalent circuit separates stator resistance/leakage, magnetizing branch, core loss, and rotor terms reflected to the stator. Only the mutual or magnetizing flux crosses the air gap and participates in energy conversion; leakage flux links only its own winding.

The air gap makes induction motors different from transformers. The chapter states that transformer magnetizing current is typically 1-5% of rated current, while induction motor magnetizing current is about 30-50% of rated current.

The rotor frequency is the slip frequency:

$$
fr = sfs
$$

The induced rotor voltage is also proportional to slip, so it is largest at standstill and goes to zero at synchronous speed.

In power-flow terms, the rotor copper loss and mechanical output split the air-gap power:

$$
Pr=sP{ag}
$$

$$
P{mech}=(1-s)P{ag}
$$

That is the central efficiency intuition: lower slip sends less air-gap power into rotor copper loss and more into mechanical output.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 11-26.
# Speed Control and Operating Regions

The basic speed-control lever is stator frequency because synchronous speed is proportional to frequency. Below base speed, the chapter uses constant $V/f$ control. Since induced voltage is proportional to frequency times flux, keeping voltage roughly proportional to frequency keeps air-gap flux approximately constant.

Above base speed, voltage cannot keep increasing, so flux weakens. The induction motor operating range divides into:
- constant-torque region: voltage and frequency rise together, flux and current stay approximately constant
- constant-power region: voltage and current are limited; flux drops with speed and slip frequency is increased to hold current
- breakdown-torque region: slip frequency reaches its stable maximum, current falls, and torque drops faster

Figure: induction motor constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions under variable-frequency operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 45, Fig. 3.47.

This is the AC-machine analogue of the DC motor's voltage-control and field-weakening regions.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 39-49.
# Synchronous Motors

The synchronous motor section contrasts induction machines with machines whose rotor field locks to the stator rotating field. A synchronous motor has a three-phase stator, like an induction motor, but the rotor field comes from DC field excitation or permanent magnets. It develops steady torque when rotor speed equals synchronous speed.

For a cylindrical rotor with stator resistance neglected, the chapter gives the classic power-angle relation:

$$
P = 3\frac{VsEf}{Xs}\sin\delta
$$

$$
T = 3\frac{VsEf}{\omegas Xs}\sin\delta
$$

The angle $\delta$ is the power angle or torque angle between stator voltage and excitation voltage. It increases with load and is analogous to slip as the load-dependent variable, but the rotor still runs at synchronous speed.

For salient-pole machines, the rotor has different d- and q-axis reluctances, so the torque has both electromagnetic and reluctance components:

$$
T =
3\frac{VsEf}{\omegas Xd}\sin\delta
- 3\frac{Vs^2(Xd-Xq)}{2\omegasXdXq}\sin 2\delta
$$

This is the steady-state version of the magnet-torque plus reluctance-torque split used later in IPMSM/SynRM discussion.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 49-57.
# Starting Synchronous Motors

The chapter notes that synchronous motors are not self-starting from a fixed 50/60 Hz supply because they produce steady torque only once synchronized. Traditional starting methods include an external motor or damper windings that let the machine start as an induction motor. PMSM variable-speed drives avoid this by using a PWM inverter to start at reduced frequency with rotor-position information.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 57-58.
# Wiki Integration
- Previous/next: chapter-02-dc-motor-control gives the scalar control baseline, while chapter-04-ac-motor-modeling turns the AC rotating-field picture into d-q equations.
- induction-motor-basics keeps the durable rotating-field, slip, equivalent-circuit, power-flow, and operating-region material.
- electrical-angle uses the synchronous-speed and pole-count relationship.
- motor-operating-region uses the induction motor capability curve as the AC motor counterpart to the DC curve.
- magnetic-reluctance-torque uses the salient-pole synchronous motor equations to connect reluctance torque to d-q saliency.
- bldc-pmsm-spmsm uses the PMSM/synchronous discussion as context for permanent-magnet robot motors.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 4: Modeling of AC Motors and Reference Frame

Chapter 4 is the textbook counterpart to the lecture d-q derivations. It starts from abc phase variables with time-varying inductances, then uses reference-frame transformations to produce constant-coefficient d-q models for induction motors and PMSMs. Read it with abc-to-dq-transform-derivation, spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, spmsm-torque-equation, and induction-motor-basics.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- abc voltage and flux-linkage equations for induction motors
- why stator-rotor mutual inductance varies with rotor angle
- PMSM structure, magnet materials, SPMSM/IPMSM topology differences, and saliency
- Clarke and Park transformations into stationary and rotating d-q frames
- induction-motor d-q voltage, flux-linkage, and torque equations
- PMSM d-q voltage, flux-linkage, equivalent-circuit, and torque equations
# Why Reference Frames Are Needed

In abc coordinates, a three-phase AC motor has phase windings fixed in the stator and rotor windings or magnets moving relative to them. For an induction motor, the mutual-inductance matrix between stator and rotor windings depends on rotor angle $\thetar$. Those coefficients change with time whenever the rotor moves.

That is the modeling problem: the physical circuit is symmetric, but the equations in raw phase variables are full of sinusoidal, time-varying coefficients. Reference-frame transformation rewrites the same electrical state in d-q-n coordinates so the main mutual-inductance terms become constant in a selected rotating frame.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 3-11.
# PMSM Structure and Saliency

Chapter 4 also gives a practical PMSM taxonomy. Synchronous motors can be viewed as a spectrum from pure reluctance torque to pure magnet torque: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM.

For PMSM hardware, the chapter distinguishes:
- distributed vs concentrated stator windings
- radial-flux vs axial-flux layouts
- surface-mounted permanent magnet synchronous motors (SPMSM)
- interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSM), including parallel and spoke/perpendicular topologies
- inner-rotor vs outer-rotor construction

SPMSMs have magnets on the rotor surface, so the effective air gap includes magnet thickness and the d- and q-axis inductances are approximately equal. IPMSMs bury magnets inside the rotor, usually giving magnetic saliency and stronger flux-weakening capability. The chapter also notes the practical magnet tradeoff: NdFeB has high remanence and coercivity but is temperature sensitive, so thermal limits and demagnetization risk constrain maximum current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 12-18.
# Clarke and Park Transformations

The chapter uses a magnitude-invariant transform with coefficient $\frac{2}{3}$. In the stationary reference frame, the abc variables are transformed into d-q-n variables. For a balanced three-phase system with no neutral current, the n-axis variable is zero.

Then a rotation matrix maps stationary d-q variables into a rotating frame:

$$
fd^e = fd^s\cos\theta + fq^s\sin\theta
$$

$$
fq^e = -fd^s\sin\theta + fq^s\cos\theta
$$

The chapter labels the stationary transform as Clarke's transformation and the stationary-to-rotating transform as Park's transformation.

Figure: transformations between abc, stationary d-q, and arbitrary rotating reference frames. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 29, Fig. 4.22.

The important convention warning is that magnitude-invariant and power-invariant transforms do not produce the same numerical d-q values or power expressions. The book uses magnitude-invariant scaling for motor variables.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 25-29.
# Induction Motor d-q Model

After transformation, the induction motor stator voltage equations in an arbitrary frame rotating at angular speed $\omega$ are:

$$
v{ds}^{\omega}=Rs i{ds}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{ds}^{\omega}}{dt}-\omega\lambda{qs}^{\omega}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{\omega}=Rs i{qs}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{qs}^{\omega}}{dt}+\omega\lambda{ds}^{\omega}
$$

The rotor equations use the relative speed between the chosen frame and rotor:

$$
v{dr}^{\omega}=Rr i{dr}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{dr}^{\omega}}{dt}-(\omega-\omegar)\lambda{qr}^{\omega}
$$

$$
v{qr}^{\omega}=Rr i{qr}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{qr}^{\omega}}{dt}+(\omega-\omegar)\lambda{dr}^{\omega}
$$

For a squirrel-cage rotor, rotor voltages are zero because the rotor bars are shorted. The d- and q-axis flux linkages become magnetically decoupled and use constant inductances:

$$
\lambda{ds}^{\omega}=Ls i{ds}^{\omega}+Lm i{dr}^{\omega}
$$

$$
\lambda{qs}^{\omega}=Ls i{qs}^{\omega}+Lm i{qr}^{\omega}
$$

The chapter's key modeling payoff is explicit: transformation removes time-varying mutual inductances due to stator-rotor relative motion and decouples d and q flux linkages.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 37-42.
# PMSM d-q Model

The PMSM voltage equations reuse the stator d-q structure:

$$
v{ds}^{\omega}=Rs i{ds}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{ds}^{\omega}}{dt}-\omega\lambda{qs}^{\omega}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{\omega}=Rs i{qs}^{\omega}+\frac{d\lambda{qs}^{\omega}}{dt}+\omega\lambda{ds}^{\omega}
$$

The PMSM difference is in flux linkage. For an IPMSM in the rotor frame:

$$
\lambdad^r=Ld id^r+\phif
$$

$$
\lambdaq^r=Lq iq^r
$$

For an ideal SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the saliency term largely disappears and torque is mainly controlled by q-axis current.

Figure: IPMSM d-axis and q-axis equivalent circuits in the rotor reference frame. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 48, Fig. 4.27.

The torque expression separates magnet torque and reluctance torque:

$$
Te=\frac{3}{2}\frac{P}{2}\left(\phif iq^r+(Ld-Lq)id^r iq^r\right)
$$

The exact sign of the reluctance term depends on the book's d-q axis and current sign convention, so use this page as a convention cross-check before mixing formulas with lecture notes or software libraries.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 46-49.
# Wiki Integration
- Previous/next: chapter-03-ac-motors supplies the steady-state AC motor picture, and chapter-05-vector-control uses these d-q models for field-oriented control.
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation uses this chapter as the textbook cross-check for Clarke/Park transformation structure.
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations uses the PMSM rotor-frame voltage and flux-linkage form.
- spmsm-torque-equation uses the magnet-torque and reluctance-torque split.
- spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions connects to the chapter's idealized no-saturation, sinusoidal-winding assumptions.
- induction-motor-basics uses the induction d-q model as the dynamic-model continuation of Chapter 3.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 5: Vector Control of AC Motors
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 5: Vector Control of AC Motors

Chapter 5 is the field-oriented control chapter. It explains how AC motor control can be made to behave like separately excited dc-motor-control by choosing a rotating reference frame where one axis controls flux and the orthogonal axis controls torque. Read it after abc-to-dq-transform-derivation, induction-motor-basics, spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, and spmsm-torque-equation.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- the torque-control conditions that make a separately excited DC motor simple to control
- direct and indirect rotor-flux-oriented control of induction motors
- rotor flux angle generation and slip-frequency calculation
- detuning from incorrect rotor time constant or rotor resistance
- induction-motor flux estimation by voltage model, current model, and combined model
- PMSM vector control for SPMSM and IPMSM machines
- maximum torque per ampere (MTPA) ideas for magnet and reluctance torque
# DC Motor Analogy

The chapter starts by using the separately excited DC motor as the control model. Its field flux and armature current are naturally orthogonal because the commutator keeps the armature magnetomotive force at 90 electrical degrees from the field. With fixed field flux, torque is proportional to armature current:

$$
T = k |\phif| |ia| \approx k' |ia|
$$

Vector control tries to create the same structure inside an AC motor. A rotating d-q frame is aligned with a chosen flux vector, the d-axis current is used as flux-producing current, and q-axis current is used as torque-producing current. This makes current commands meaningful for fast torque control instead of only voltage-frequency control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 2-5.
# Induction Motor Vector Control

For an induction motor, the most common alignment is rotor-flux orientation. If the d-axis is aligned to the rotor flux, then the q-axis rotor flux becomes zero and the stator current components separate into flux and torque roles.

In the rotor-flux frame, the d-axis current determines rotor flux through a first-order lag:

$$
\lambda{dr}^{e}=\frac{Lm}{1+Tr p}i{ds}^{e}
$$

where $Tr=Lr/Rr$ is the rotor time constant. In steady state, this becomes:

$$
\lambda{dr}^{e}=Lm i{ds}^{e}
$$

With rotor flux held constant, torque becomes proportional to q-axis current, so the controller can command $i{qs}^{e}$ as the torque channel.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 6-12.
# Direct and Indirect Flux Orientation

Direct vector control estimates or measures the rotor flux vector and then calculates the flux angle:

$$
\thetae=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda{qr}^{s}}{\lambda{dr}^{s}}\right)
$$

Indirect vector control instead generates the flux angle from rotor speed and slip speed:

$$
\thetae = \int \omegae dt = \int (\omegar+\omega{sl}) dt
$$

With constant rotor flux, the slip frequency is:

$$
\omega{sl}=\frac{1}{Tr}\frac{i{qs}^{e}}{i{ds}^{e}}
$$

Indirect control is easier to implement and can work well at low speed, but it depends on rotor speed feedback and a correct rotor time constant. Direct control avoids the explicit slip equation but depends on flux estimation quality.

Figure: induction motor vector-control system with speed controller, flux controller, current controller, coordinate transformation, and flux estimator. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, page 32, Fig. 5.22.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 13-22.
# Detuning Risk

The main weakness of indirect induction-motor vector control is detuning. The slip-frequency equation uses $Tr=Lr/Rr$, and rotor resistance changes with temperature. If $Tr$ is wrong, the commanded synchronous frame does not stay aligned with the true rotor flux.

That misalignment breaks the intended d-axis/q-axis decoupling. The result is lower torque per ampere, poorer transient torque response, and flux error. The chapter notes that a 10 C rotor temperature rise can raise rotor resistance by roughly 4%, so practical drives need either conservative tuning or online compensation.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 23-26.
# Flux Estimation

The chapter compares three induction-motor flux-estimation families.

Voltage-model estimation integrates stator back EMF to estimate stator flux and then derives rotor flux. It is mostly independent of rotor parameters, but low speed is difficult because voltage drops, inverter nonidealities, offsets, and noise become comparable to the actual back EMF.

Current-model estimation uses measured currents, rotor speed, and the rotor circuit equations. It is better at low speed, but it depends strongly on rotor resistance and can become oscillatory or inaccurate at higher speed.

Combined estimation blends the two. The current model dominates at low speed, the voltage model dominates at high speed, and the transition region is designed so the estimator avoids the worst weakness of each model.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 27-34.
# PMSM Vector Control

For PMSMs, rotor flux orientation is structurally easier because rotor position gives the magnet-flux angle directly. That is why PMSM FOC is tightly connected to position sensing: startup needs absolute rotor position, or an incremental encoder plus an initial-position detection routine. See hall-effect-position-sensing for the simpler commutation-sensor context.

For an SPMSM with $Ld \approx Lq$, torque is:

$$
Te=\frac{P}{2}\frac{3}{2}\phif i{qs}^{r}
$$

The MTPA condition is simply:

$$
i{ds}^{r}=0
$$

so q-axis current directly sets torque.

For an IPMSM, saliency adds magnetic-reluctance-torque:

$$
Te=\frac{P}{2}\frac{3}{2}\left[\phif iq^r+(Ld-Lq)id^r iq^r\right]
$$

For common IPMSM designs with $Lq>Ld$, negative d-axis current can make reluctance torque assist magnet torque. The practical MTPA command therefore depends on the motor's inductance map, magnet flux, saturation, cross-saturation, and temperature. This is the Chapter 5 half of the topology-control bridge captured in pmsm-topology-control-consequences; Chapter 8 continues the same negative d-axis-current idea into high-speed voltage limits and flux weakening.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 35-44.
# Wiki Integration
- motor-current-control is the current-loop layer that makes vector-current commands track fast enough for torque control.
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation and spmsm-dq-voltage-equations provide the modeling basis for the rotating frames used here.
- spmsm-torque-equation and magnetic-reluctance-torque explain the PMSM torque terms behind SPMSM and IPMSM MTPA.
- pmsm-topology-control-consequences connects SPMSM/IPMSM MTPA to high-speed flux weakening.
- motor-operating-region continues the story into voltage-limited and flux-weakening operation.
