pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 6: Current Regulators for AC Motors
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 6: Current Regulators for AC Motors

Chapter 6 is the current-control layer underneath vector control. It compares hysteresis control, carrier-based current control, stationary-frame regulators, synchronous-frame d-q regulators, feedforward decoupling, and complex-vector regulators. It is the textbook companion to motor-current-control, motor-driver-inverter, and space-vector-pwm.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- hysteresis current regulation
- ramp-comparison current regulation
- stationary-frame and synchronous-frame d-q current regulators
- synchronous-frame PI gain selection
- feedforward voltage commands for induction motors and PMSMs
- complex-vector current regulator structure
# What the Current Regulator Does

In a vector-controlled drive, the outer speed or torque controller produces current references. The current regulator compares those references with measured phase currents, transforms the error if needed, and commands inverter voltage. Its quality determines how quickly torque can respond and how much of the voltage limit remains usable at high speed.

Because a floating-neutral three-phase motor has $ia+ib+ic=0$, only two current variables are independent. That is why the chapter quickly moves from phase currents to d-q regulation.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 2-4.
# Hysteresis and Ramp Comparison

Hysteresis current control is a bang-bang regulator. When current rises above or below the hysteresis band, the inverter switch state changes. It is simple and has strong transient response, but switching frequency varies with operating point, current slope, DC-link voltage, and motor back EMF. That makes acoustic noise, thermal design, and EMI/filter design harder.

Ramp-comparison regulation compares current error with a triangular carrier. It gives a controlled switching frequency, which is easier for inverter design. The weakness is steady-state magnitude and phase error when regulating sinusoidal AC currents. The problem becomes worse as frequency and back EMF rise because the regulator has less voltage headroom and more phase lag.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 4-7.
# Synchronous-Frame PI Regulation

The chapter's practical center is synchronous-frame current regulation. If d-q currents are transformed into a frame rotating with the commanded electrical angle, sinusoidal steady-state phase currents become DC quantities. A PI controller can then eliminate steady-state error in the synchronous frame.

For a general R-L load in the synchronous frame:

$$
v{ds}^{e}=R i{ds}^{e}+L\frac{d i{ds}^{e}}{dt}-\omegae L i{qs}^{e}+e{ds}^{e}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{e}=R i{qs}^{e}+L\frac{d i{qs}^{e}}{dt}+\omegae L i{ds}^{e}+e{qs}^{e}
$$

The cross-coupling terms, $-\omegae L iq$ and $+\omegae L id$, show why plain independent PI loops become inadequate at high speed unless decoupling/feedforward is added.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 8-12.
# PI Gain Selection

The chapter gives a simple gain-selection rule for synchronous-frame PI current loops:

$$
Kp=L\omegac
$$

$$
Ki=R\omegac
$$

where $\omegac$ is the desired current-loop bandwidth. For an induction motor in rotor-flux-oriented control, the effective current-loop inductance is the leakage inductance term $\sigma Ls$, and the effective resistance includes stator resistance plus the reflected rotor contribution:

$$
R \approx Rs + Rr\frac{Lm^2}{Lr^2}
$$

These formulas are useful starting points, not a substitute for checking voltage saturation, sampling delay, PWM delay, measurement filtering, and inverter nonlinearities from motor-driver-nonidealities.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 12-14.
# Feedforward Control

Feedforward adds the expected motor voltage terms before the PI output is sent to the PWM modulator. The PI loop then corrects modeling error instead of supplying the full back-EMF and cross-coupling voltage.

For a PMSM in the rotor frame, the feedforward terms are:

$$
v{ds,ff}^{r}=-\omegar Lq i{qs}^{r}
$$

$$
v{qs,ff}^{r}=\omegar Ld i{ds}^{r}+\omegar\phif
$$

This directly matches the cross-coupling and magnet back-EMF terms in spmsm-dq-voltage-equations. Feedforward improves high-speed current control, but only if the motor parameters, speed estimate, and voltage model are good enough.

Figure: synchronous-frame PI current regulator with feedforward terms and PWM inverter interface. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, page 16, Fig. 6.14.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 15-17.
# Wiki Integration
- motor-current-control should use this chapter as the textbook basis for synchronous-frame PI, gain rules, feedforward, and anti-windup context.
- motor-driver-inverter and space-vector-pwm provide the voltage-actuation layer that the current regulator commands.
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations explains why the PMSM feedforward terms have speed-dependent d-q coupling.
- motor-current-sensing is the measurement side that constrains the regulator's usable bandwidth.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 7: Pulse-Width Modulation Inverters
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 7: Pulse-Width Modulation Inverters

Chapter 7 is the inverter and PWM chapter. It starts from voltage-source inverter switching states, then builds sinusoidal PWM, third-harmonic injection PWM, space-vector PWM, discontinuous PWM, overmodulation, dead-time effects, and current-measurement constraints. It is the textbook expansion of motor-driver-inverter, space-vector-pwm, motor-driver-nonidealities, and motor-current-sensing.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- voltage-source and current-source inverter categories
- single-phase and three-phase inverter switching states
- square-wave operation and switching functions
- sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)
- third-harmonic injection PWM (THIPWM)
- space-vector PWM (SVPWM)
- discontinuous and offset-voltage PWM methods
- overmodulation and six-step operation
- dead time and dead-time compensation
- current measurement timing in PWM drives
# Inverter Basics

The chapter focuses on the voltage-source inverter because it is the standard motor-drive structure: a DC link supplies three half-bridges, and each inverter leg connects a motor terminal to either the positive or negative DC bus. The switches in each leg must be complementary with dead time to avoid shoot-through.

Switching devices are chosen by voltage, current, and switching-frequency needs. The chapter frames GTO, IGCT, IGBT, and MOSFET devices as different points in that trade space. For robot-scale drives, the same topology appears with MOSFETs or IGBTs depending on power level.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 2-17.
# PWM Goals

PWM chooses switch timings so the motor sees a desired fundamental voltage while high-frequency harmonics are pushed to ranges the motor inductance can attenuate. The control criteria are:
- usable linear voltage range
- current ripple and torque ripple
- harmonic distortion
- switching loss
- motor loss and acoustic noise
- implementation cost and sampling constraints

The chapter defines modulation index as:

$$
MI=\frac{V{1,peak}}{V{dc}/2}
$$

In six-step operation, the maximum fundamental corresponds to:

$$
MI=\frac{4}{\pi}=1.273
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 18-25.
# SPWM and Third-Harmonic Injection

Sinusoidal PWM compares three sinusoidal references with a triangular carrier. It is linear when the reference peak does not exceed the carrier peak:

$$
0 \le MI \le 1
$$

Its maximum linear fundamental is $V{dc}/2$, which is 78.5% of the six-step maximum.

Third-harmonic injection adds a common third-harmonic component to the three phase references. For a floating-neutral three-phase load, this common-mode component does not appear in the line-to-line voltages, but it reduces the reference peak and increases the available fundamental voltage. The common textbook choice gives about 15.5% more linear voltage than SPWM.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 26-36.
# SVPWM

SVPWM represents inverter output as a space vector in the stationary d-q plane. The three-phase two-level inverter has six active vectors and two zero vectors. The active vectors form a hexagon, and each PWM period synthesizes the commanded voltage vector using the two adjacent active vectors plus zero-vector time:

$$
V^ Ts = Vn T1 + V{n+1}T2
$$

$$
T0 = Ts-(T1+T2)
$$

Compared with SPWM, SVPWM uses the DC-link voltage better, giving the same 15.5% linear voltage increase associated with common-mode injection. It also gives a useful framework for reducing current ripple, torque ripple, and switching loss.

Figure: output voltage vectors for a three-phase voltage-source inverter in the stationary d-q plane. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 37, Fig. 7.38.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-50.
# Offset and Discontinuous PWM

The chapter also explains PWM through offset-voltage injection. Adding the same offset voltage to all three phase-voltage references does not change the motor's line-to-line voltage in a floating-neutral system, but it changes pole-voltage duty cycles. This viewpoint unifies SPWM, third-harmonic injection, and SVPWM as different common-mode choices.

Discontinuous PWM intentionally clamps one phase to a DC rail for part of the electrical cycle. That reduces switching events and inverter switching loss, but it changes current ripple and common-mode voltage behavior. It is a practical efficiency tool rather than a different motor-control objective.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 50-62.
# Overmodulation

Overmodulation starts when the commanded voltage exceeds the linear PWM range. SPWM reaches this point at 78.5% of the six-step fundamental; conventional SVPWM reaches it at 90.7%. Beyond that point, the relation between command and fundamental voltage is nonlinear, low-order harmonics rise, and d-q current regulators lose their clean voltage-actuator assumption.

The chapter separates dynamic overmodulation, where a transient current loop briefly needs more voltage, from steady-state overmodulation, where the drive intentionally operates near six-step to extend speed. This distinction matters for motor-operating-region because steady-state high-speed operation trades current quality for voltage utilization.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 63-69.
# Dead Time

Dead time prevents both switches in one leg from conducting simultaneously. The cost is an average output-voltage error whose sign depends on current direction:

$$
V{err}=t{dead}V{dc}f{sw}
$$

The error causes fundamental voltage loss, low-order current harmonics, torque pulsation, and extra motor loss. It is especially visible at low speed, where the desired voltage is small. Compensation estimates the sign and magnitude of current-dependent voltage error and adds a corrective voltage command.

Figure: inverter leg voltage error caused by dead time for positive and negative current directions. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 69, Fig. 7.80.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 69-76.
# Wiki Integration
- space-vector-pwm should use this chapter for the voltage-vector, zero-vector, and modulation-index grounding.
- motor-driver-inverter maps the hardware topology behind the PWM methods.
- motor-driver-nonidealities should use the dead-time section as the textbook source for current-direction-dependent voltage error.
- motor-current-control and motor-current-sensing depend on this chapter's PWM timing and voltage-limit constraints.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 8: High-Speed Operation of AC Motors
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 8: High-Speed Operation of AC Motors

Chapter 8 explains field-weakening and flux-weakening operation after the inverter reaches its voltage limit. It turns the voltage and current constraints from motor-operating-region into concrete command rules for induction motors, IPMSMs, and SPMSMs, and it supplies the high-speed half of pmsm-topology-control-consequences.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- why motor back EMF forces field weakening above base speed
- induction-motor flux weakening under voltage and current limits
- maximum-torque field-weakening control for induction motors
- PMSM flux weakening by negative d-axis current
- IPMSM voltage/current limit geometry and maximum speed
- SPMSM high-speed constraints
# Why Field Weakening Is Needed

Back EMF rises with speed. For a DC motor it is proportional to flux and mechanical speed, and for an AC motor it is proportional to flux and electrical frequency:

$$
E{DC} \propto \phi\omegam
$$

$$
E{AC} \propto \phi f
$$

Below base speed, the drive can raise voltage as speed rises. Above base speed, the inverter voltage is saturated by the DC link and PWM method, so the drive must reduce effective flux if it wants to keep increasing speed.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 2-5.
# Induction Motor Field Weakening

In rotor-flux-oriented induction-motor control, d-axis stator current controls rotor flux and q-axis current controls torque. The simple field-weakening idea is to reduce flux inversely with speed:

$$
i{ds}^{e}=\frac{\omega{base}}{\omegar}I{d,rated}
$$

The chapter shows why this is not enough by itself. A high-speed motor is constrained by both the current circle and voltage ellipse:

$$
i{ds}^{2}+i{qs}^{2}\le I{smax}^{2}
$$

$$
v{ds}^{2}+v{qs}^{2}\le V{smax}^{2}
$$

For SVPWM, the chapter uses $V{smax}=V{dc}/\sqrt{3}$ as the theoretical voltage limit; for SPWM it uses $V{dc}/2$. In practice, it recommends keeping the command below the theoretical limit so voltage drops, dead time, and control stability do not consume all margin.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 5-12.
# Maximum-Torque Field Weakening

Under rotor-flux orientation, induction-motor torque can be written in the simple form:

$$
Te=k i{ds}^{e}i{qs}^{e}
$$

The maximum-torque field-weakening problem is therefore a constrained optimization: choose the d-axis and q-axis currents that maximize $id iq$ while staying inside both the voltage ellipse and current circle. As speed rises, the voltage ellipse shrinks, forcing lower flux-producing current and eventually lower torque.

The useful practical point is that flux weakening is not just "reduce d current." The optimal point depends on speed, DC-link voltage, leakage inductance, current limit, and the desired torque response.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 12-16.
# PMSM Flux Weakening

In a PMSM, magnet flux cannot be directly reduced. Instead, the controller commands negative d-axis current so stator-produced d-axis flux opposes the permanent-magnet flux:

$$
\lambda{ds}^{r}=Ld i{ds}^{r}+\phif
$$

This is why saliency and d-axis inductance matter for high-speed PMSM operation. Larger $Ld$ gives negative d-axis current more ability to reduce effective flux, but the same current also consumes current-limit budget and creates demagnetization/thermal constraints.

Figure: PMSM flux weakening by stator-current flux opposing permanent-magnet flux. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, page 18, Fig. 8.16.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-19.
# IPMSM Voltage and Current Limits

For an IPMSM, the steady-state rotor-frame voltage equations simplify to:

$$
v{ds}^{r}=-\omegar Lq i{qs}^{r}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{r}=\omegar(Ld i{ds}^{r}+\phif)
$$

The current limit remains a circle in the $id$-$iq$ plane. The voltage limit becomes an ellipse centered at:

$$
\left(-\frac{\phif}{Ld},0\right)
$$

As speed increases, the voltage ellipse shrinks. The feasible command region is the intersection of the current circle and voltage ellipse. This geometry connects MTPA at lower speed, flux weakening above base speed, and maximum torque per voltage (MTPV) behavior at higher speed.

Figure: voltage-limit ellipse and current-limit circle for PMSM high-speed operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, page 22, Fig. 8.20.

If $\phif > Ld I{smax}$, the chapter gives a finite maximum speed:

$$
\omega{r,max}=\frac{V{smax}}{\phif-Ld I{smax}}
$$

If $\phif < Ld I{smax}$, the ideal electrical model does not impose the same finite speed ceiling, though mechanical strength, losses, voltage margin, and control limits still matter.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 20-27.
# SPMSM High-Speed Operation

For an SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the voltage-limit boundary is closer to a circle than an ellipse. Flux weakening is still possible by commanding negative d-axis current, but the effect is limited because surface-magnet motors usually have small inductance and limited saliency. That is why IPMSM designs are often preferred when wide constant-power speed range is important.

The chapter's main SPMSM warning is the same practical one: high-speed commands must satisfy current limit, voltage limit, thermal limit, demagnetization limit, and PWM voltage utilization at the same time.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 27-31.
# Wiki Integration
- motor-operating-region is the main concept page for the current-circle and voltage-limit interpretation.
- pmsm-topology-control-consequences connects this high-speed geometry back to SPMSM/IPMSM MTPA and topology choice.
- space-vector-pwm determines the practical $V{smax}$ available to the motor.
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations and spmsm-torque-equation provide the PMSM equations used for flux weakening.
- magnetic-reluctance-torque explains why IPMSM saliency changes both MTPA and high-speed behavior.

pages.title: Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 9: Speed Estimation and Sensorless Control
pages.text:
# Electric Motor Control (2017), Chapter 9: Speed Estimation and Sensorless Control

Chapter 9 covers the feedback side of AC motor drives: position sensors, encoder-based speed estimation, and sensorless control. It links directly to vector-control startup, motor-current-control, spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, and inverter nonidealities from motor-driver-nonidealities.
# Scope

The chapter covers:
- resolver and rotary encoder position sensors
- M, T, and M/T speed-estimation methods for incremental encoders
- limits of discrete average-speed estimation
- sensorless control by motor model and back EMF
- sensorless control by motor saliency or injected signal
- practical sensorless-control performance limits
# Position Sensors

The chapter distinguishes resolver and encoder use in AC motor drives. A resolver is an absolute, transformer-like rotor-position sensor with sine/cosine outputs. It is robust and suitable for PMSM startup because rotor position is available immediately.

An incremental encoder gives relative position through A/B quadrature pulses and often one Z-index pulse per revolution. It is common for speed and position feedback, but absolute electrical angle must be established by homing, index detection, or an initial-position routine.

Figure: induction motor and PMSM drive systems using encoder or resolver position feedback. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, page 10, Fig. 9.11.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 2-5.
# M Method

The M method counts encoder pulses during a fixed sampling interval $Tc$. If $m$ pulses are counted and the encoder has PPR pulses per revolution:

$$
\omegam=\frac{2\pi}{Tc}\frac{m}{PPR}
$$

$$
N=\frac{60}{Tc}\frac{m}{PPR}
$$

Its benefit is a constant sample time and simple implementation. Its weakness is low-speed quantization: the count may change by only one or a few pulses per sample, so speed resolution is poor. It is better suited to higher speed.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 5-6.
# T Method

The T method measures the time required for a fixed pulse displacement. If the measured time is $mcT{clock}$:

$$
\omegam=\frac{2\pi}{PPR\;mcT{clock}}
$$

It has better low-speed resolution because long pulse intervals can be measured precisely. Its weakness is high-speed operation, where the measured interval becomes very short and requires a fast clock. The sample interval is also variable, which complicates control-loop timing.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 6-8.
# M/T Method

The M/T method combines pulse counting with pulse-period timing. It counts pulses over a fixed interval and extends the timing to the last detected pulse edge:

$$
\omegam=\frac{2\pi}{PPR}\frac{m1}{Tc+m2T2}
$$

$$
N=\frac{60}{PPR}\frac{m1}{Tc+m2T2}
$$

This improves accuracy over a wider speed range, but implementation is more complex and the effective update interval can still vary at low speed. The chapter also emphasizes that all three encoder methods estimate average speed over a finite interval, not instantaneous speed. High-bandwidth servo drives often need observers or filtering around the raw estimate.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 8-10.
# Sensorless Control Families

The chapter divides sensorless control into two broad families.

Model-based methods estimate rotor flux or position from measured voltage and current. This includes back-EMF methods, observer-based estimators, and MRAS-based estimators. They work best at medium and high speed, where back EMF is large enough to observe reliably. At low speed and zero speed, the signal becomes too small and inverter voltage errors become dominant.

Motor-characteristics methods exploit saliency, slotting, saturation, or eccentricity. High-frequency signal injection is the main practical example. It can estimate position near zero speed, but it adds signal-processing complexity, acoustic noise, torque ripple, and extra loss.

Figure: sensorless control categories based on motor model or motor characteristics. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, page 11, Fig. 9.12.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-14.
# Practical Performance Limits

The chapter gives a useful drive-selection perspective: position-sensor vector control has much better speed accuracy and wider speed range than sensorless vector control, and sensorless vector control is still much better than scalar V/f control. It gives representative values of about +/-0.01% speed accuracy and 1:1000 range for sensored vector control, about +/-0.5% and 1:150 for sensorless vector control, and about +/-1% to +/-2% for V/f control.

For model-based sensorless control, the hard region is low speed. The chapter notes that below roughly 10% rated speed, back-EMF-based sensorless performance is generally unsatisfactory without additional methods. For induction machines, stable low-speed operation may be limited to only a few hertz. Practical systems often combine high-frequency injection at low speed with back-EMF/model observers at medium and high speed.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 12-16.
# Wiki Integration
- motor-current-control depends on speed and position feedback quality for high-bandwidth vector control.
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations explains why PMSM sensorless observers rely on voltage, current, speed, and flux-linkage models.
- motor-driver-nonidealities is critical for sensorless control because dead time and voltage error corrupt low-speed back-EMF estimates.
- hall-effect-position-sensing provides a simpler BLDC commutation-sensor contrast to resolver/encoder feedback.

pages.title: Electrical Angle
pages.text:
# Electrical Angle

Electrical angle measures position in the motor's magnetic/electrical cycle, not just the physical shaft angle.

Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Hall-effect commutation placement discussion and Fig. 7.

Mechanical angle is the actual rotor rotation: one shaft revolution is 360 mechanical degrees.

Electrical angle counts how far the rotor magnetic field has advanced relative to the stator phase pattern. A motor with $p$ pole pairs goes through $p$ electrical cycles per one mechanical revolution:

$$
\theta{electrical} = p\theta{mechanical}
$$

Equivalently, with pole count $P$:

$$
\thetae=\frac{P}{2}\thetam
$$

$$
\theta{mechanical} = \frac{\theta{electrical}}{p}
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 10.

Examples:
- 2-pole motor = 1 pole pair: 30 electrical degrees = 30 mechanical degrees.
- 8-pole motor = 4 pole pairs: 30 electrical degrees = 7.5 mechanical degrees.
- 8-pole motor = 4 pole pairs: one full 360-degree shaft revolution = 1440 electrical degrees.

This matters for Hall sensor placement because commutation depends on magnetic/electrical phase, not only physical package angle. Two motors may need the same Hall-sensor spacing in electrical degrees but different mechanical spacing if their pole counts differ.
# Related

- hall-effect-position-sensing

pages.title: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
pages.text:
# Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors

Hall effect sensors detect magnetic field and turn rotor magnetic position into electrical signals for the motor controller/driver.
# Why BLDC Motors Need Position Information

A BLDC driver must know which stator phases to energize. If it switches too early or too late, torque ripple, inefficient operation, poor startup, or incorrect commutation can result.

Hall sensors provide a simple way to answer:

> Where is the permanent-magnet rotor right now?
# Hall Effect Basics

The Hall effect: when a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor is exposed to a magnetic field, a voltage appears perpendicular to the current and field direction.

In practical BLDC sensor ICs, that effect is packaged as a digital position sensor. When a rotor magnet pole passes near the sensor, the sensor output changes state.
# How It Works in a BLDC Motor

Typical flow:

1. Permanent magnets on the rotor pass near Hall sensor ICs.
2. Sensor outputs change based on magnetic polarity/position.
3. The logic/controller interprets the sensor pattern as rotor sector.
4. The driver switches the power transistors.
5. Current flows through the correct stator windings.
6. The stator field pulls the rotor forward.

Figure: three Hall-effect sensor outputs over electrical angle in a BLDC motor example. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 3.

Honeywell's application note describes three Hall-effect sensor IC outputs being used as shaft-position encoder signals for a logic circuit and a three-phase driver.
# Sensor Placement

The Hall sensor ICs are usually mounted on the stationary side of the motor assembly, such as the stator, housing, or a fixed PCB near the shaft. They are not normally embedded in the rotating rotor; the rotor or rotor-shaft magnet moves past the fixed sensors.

Hall sensors can be placed:
- inside the motor
- at the end of the motor shaft
- around a ring magnet attached to the rotor shaft

Figure: typical sensor IC locations: inside the motor, at the shaft end, or around a rotor-shaft ring magnet. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 5.

Exact angular spacing depends on pole count, sensor type, and commutation scheme. Beginner sources often describe three Hall sensors spaced 120 electrical degrees apart; Honeywell's example discusses a specific eight-pole motor/sensor arrangement where three sensor ICs are placed 30 electrical degrees apart.
# Advantages
- simple rotor-sector feedback
- reliable commutation
- speed and direction sensing
- solid-state, no mechanical wear
- useful startup behavior compared with purely sensorless commutation
# Limitations
- added sensor/wiring/package complexity
- magnetic interference sensitivity
- temperature effects
- finite response time at high speed
- lower position resolution than high-resolution encoders
# Contrast With Vector-Control Feedback

Hall sensors are usually enough for six-step BLDC commutation because they identify rotor sectors. High-performance PMSM vector control needs a finer electrical angle so the controller can align d-q axes with rotor flux. Chapter 9 distinguishes that feedback layer from Hall-sector commutation:
- resolvers provide robust absolute rotor position and are useful for PMSM startup
- incremental encoders provide relative A/B/Z pulse information, so PMSM startup also needs index/homing or initial-position detection
- sensorless control removes the position sensor but depends on motor model, back EMF, saliency, or injected signals

Figure: induction motor and PMSM drive systems using encoder or resolver position feedback. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, page 10, Fig. 9.11.

The practical comparison is not "sensor" versus "sensorless" in general. It is a control-performance trade: sensored vector control gives the best low-speed/startup behavior, while model-based sensorless control is weakest where back EMF is small. The source-level route is chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

pages.title: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
pages.text:
# Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors

Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf - Honeywell/Sensing and Productivity Solutions application note, "Magnetic Position Sensing in Brushless DC Electric Motors" (March 2016).
# Core Idea

BLDC motors replace brush/commutator mechanical switching with electronic commutation. Hall-effect sensor ICs detect rotor magnetic position and send angular-position information to logic/driver circuitry, which switches winding polarity in the correct sequence.
# Key Details
- BLDC motors are described as brush-type motors "turned inside out":
- power is fed to stationary armature/stator windings
- permanent magnet rotor is the rotating member
- position sensors and electronic switching replace brushes and commutator
- Rotor magnets operate Hall-effect sensor ICs.
- Sensor outputs act as shaft-position encoder signals.
- Logic circuitry uses these signals to switch power transistors in an H-bridge/three-phase driver circuit.
- Windings alternate polarity relative to shaft position, producing torque from the interaction of winding field and rotor permanent magnets.
- Hall sensor ICs can be placed inside the motor, at the end of the shaft, or around a ring magnet attached to the rotor shaft.
- Hall-effect sensor ICs can also support RPM and direction sensing.
# Honeywell Product Notes

The app note discusses bipolar and latching Hall-effect sensor IC families such as SS30, SS31x/SS41x, SS36x/SS46x, VF360NT/VF360ST/VF460S, and related variants.

Promoted benefits include:
- no moving parts
- reduced brush-wear maintenance
- compact packaging such as SOT-23
- low-voltage operation in selected parts
- reverse-voltage protection in selected parts
- operation up to 150 °C in selected parts
- high sensitivity enabling smaller magnets
# Related
- hall-effect-position-sensing
- bldc-electronic-commutation
- motor-actuator-structure

pages.title: Induction Motor Basics
pages.text:
# Induction Motor Basics

An induction motor is an AC motor whose stator is energized directly but whose rotor current is produced by electromagnetic induction. It is less central than PMSM/SPMSM in the robot-actuator lecture path, but it is important background for rotating magnetic fields, slip, equivalent circuits, and reference-frame modeling.
# Rotating Magnetic Field

Balanced three-phase stator currents in windings separated by $120^\circ$ electrical produce a single rotating air-gap magnetomotive-force vector. For ideal sinusoidal phase currents, the resultant mmf has constant amplitude and rotates at the electrical angular frequency of the currents.

For a motor with $P$ poles and stator frequency $f$, the synchronous speed is:

$$
ns = \frac{120f}{P}\quad\text{r/min}
$$

The electrical and mechanical angles satisfy:

$$
\thetae = \frac{P}{2}\thetam
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 6-10.
# Why Slip Is Required

The rotating field induces voltage in the rotor conductors. Since the rotor bars are shorted in a squirrel-cage rotor, that induced voltage drives rotor current. Rotor current in the stator's rotating magnetic field creates torque.

If the rotor reached the same speed as the rotating field, the rotor conductors would no longer see a changing field. No rotor EMF would be induced, rotor current would vanish, and torque would disappear. That is why an induction motor under load runs slightly below synchronous speed.

Slip is usually defined as:

$$
s = \frac{ns-nm}{ns}
$$

where $nm$ is the rotor mechanical speed.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 5-6 and 15-17.
# Equivalent-Circuit Intuition

The induction motor equivalent circuit separates:
- stator copper resistance and leakage inductance
- magnetizing branch that produces air-gap flux
- rotor resistance and leakage terms reflected to the stator side
- a slip-dependent rotor resistance term

Only the mutual or magnetizing flux crosses the air gap and contributes to energy conversion. Leakage flux links only its own winding and does not directly produce torque.

Because the machine has an air gap, its magnetizing inductance is much lower than a transformer's. The textbook states that transformer magnetizing current is typically about 1-5% of rated current, while induction-motor magnetizing current is about 30-50% of rated current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 11-12.
# Power Flow and Efficiency

Air-gap power $P{ag}$ is the power transferred from the stator field into the rotor circuit. The textbook splits that power into rotor copper loss and mechanical output:

$$
Pr = sP{ag}
$$

$$
P{mech}=(1-s)P{ag}
$$

So lower slip means less rotor copper loss and more mechanical output power. This is why low-slip operation is usually more efficient.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 20-26.
# Speed Control and Operating Regions

The most fundamental way to change induction-motor speed is to change synchronous speed by changing stator frequency. Below base speed, the drive increases voltage roughly in proportion to frequency so the air-gap flux stays near constant. This is constant $V/f$ control, and it gives a constant-torque region.

Above base speed, the terminal voltage has reached its limit, so flux must weaken as frequency rises. The motor then enters a field-weakening range split into a constant-power region and a breakdown-torque-limited region.

Figure: induction motor torque capability divided into constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 45, Fig. 3.47.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 39-47.

Chapter 8 refines the high-speed picture for vector-controlled induction motors. Under rotor-flux orientation, torque is proportional to $id iq$, so field weakening becomes a constrained current-command problem: choose flux-producing d-axis current and torque-producing q-axis current inside both the current circle and the speed-dependent voltage ellipse. The practical result is more specific than "reduce flux above base speed": the optimal current split depends on speed, voltage limit, current limit, leakage inductance, and torque demand.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 5-16.
# d-q Model Connection

Chapter 4 uses the induction motor to motivate reference-frame transformation. In abc variables, mutual inductances between stator and rotor windings vary with rotor angle. Transforming to d-q-n variables makes the mutual-inductance relationships constant in the chosen reference frame and magnetically decouples the d and q axes.

For a squirrel-cage rotor, the rotor voltages are zero because the rotor bars are shorted, but the rotor currents and flux linkages still appear in the d-q dynamic model.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 36-40.
# Vector-Control Connection

Chapter 5 turns the induction d-q model into field-oriented control. The usual choice is rotor-flux orientation: align the d-axis with rotor flux, make q-axis rotor flux zero, use d-axis stator current as the flux channel, and use q-axis stator current as the torque channel. That makes an induction motor behave more like a separately excited DC motor from the controller's point of view.

Indirect vector control computes synchronous angle from rotor speed plus slip speed. With constant rotor flux, the slip frequency depends on the current ratio:

$$
\omega{sl}=\frac{1}{Tr}\frac{iq^e}{id^e}
$$

where $Tr=Lr/Rr$ is the rotor time constant. This is powerful but sensitive to detuning: rotor resistance changes with temperature, so the slip calculation can misalign the d-q frame from the true rotor flux. Direct vector control estimates rotor flux instead, trading slip-equation sensitivity for flux-estimator quality.

Figure: induction motor vector-control system with speed controller, flux controller, current controller, coordinate transformation, and flux estimator. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, page 32, Fig. 5.22.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 6-34; see chapter-05-vector-control.
# Related

- motor-operating-region
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
- bldc-pmsm-spmsm
- chapter-05-vector-control
- chapter-08-high-speed-operation

pages.title: Jacobian Inverse Kinematics
pages.text:
# Jacobian Inverse Kinematics

Jacobian inverse kinematics uses the local velocity model

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q}
$$

to choose joint motion that moves the end effector toward a target.

If $J$ is square and full rank, the resolved-rate form is

$$
\dot{q}=J^{-1}(q)\dot{x}.
$$

For nonsquare or rank-deficient cases, implementations usually use the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse:

$$
\dot{q}=J^+(q)\dot{x}.
$$

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, Jacobian Inversion Method and pseudo-inverse sections.
# Velocity-Controlled Actuators

For joints that accept velocity commands:

1. compute current end-effector pose from forward kinematics
2. compute task error from current pose to goal pose
3. convert the task error into a desired task velocity, often with proportional gain
4. evaluate $J(q)$ at the current joint positions
5. compute $\dot{q}=J^+\dot{x}$
6. command the joint velocities, then repeat until the task error is small

The Jacobian must be recomputed as the robot moves because it depends on $q$.
# Position-Controlled Actuators

For joints that accept position commands, use the same linearization as a small displacement step:

$$
\Delta x\approx J(q)\Delta q.
$$

Then

$$
\Delta q=J^+(q)\Delta x.
$$

This is an iterative method, not a one-shot global inverse. The displacement step must be kept small enough that the local Jacobian approximation remains useful.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, position-controlled actuator method section.
# Singularities And Limits

Near singular configurations, a finite task-space motion can require very large joint velocities or position increments. The pseudo-inverse helps with nonsquare matrices and rank loss, but it does not remove actuator limits, joint limits, collision risk, or numerical sensitivity.

Practical controllers should still limit joint velocity, limit step size, monitor singular values or manipulability, and define what happens when the requested end-effector motion is not achievable.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, singularity and pseudo-inverse discussion; sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 5 and 7.
# Related
- robot-jacobian
- jacobian-singularities
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- space-and-body-jacobians

pages.title: Jacobian Singularities
pages.text:
# Jacobian Singularities

A Jacobian singularity is a configuration where the Jacobian loses rank. Physically, some end-effector velocity directions cannot be produced by any finite joint velocity command.

For a square Jacobian, singularity is often checked by

$$
\det J(q)=0.
$$

For a nonsquare Jacobian, use rank or singular values instead of determinant.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 5; sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, Problems with Jacobian Method section.
# Two-Link Arm

For the two-link planar arm in robot-jacobian, the determinant simplifies to a term proportional to

$$
L1L2\sin\theta2.
$$

Assuming nonzero link lengths, the arm is singular when

$$
\sin\theta2=0,
$$

so $\theta2=0$ or $\theta2=\pi$ in the usual planar two-link convention. These are the fully stretched or fully folded collinear postures.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 5; sources/web-clippings/craig-robotics-chapter-05-jacobian.md, Singularities section.
# Why Near-Singular Can Be Worse In Control

At the exact singularity, the lost direction is obvious: the linearized map cannot produce motion there. Near the singularity, the controller may still try to invert the Jacobian. A modest desired end-effector motion can then require very large joint velocities or very large joint-position increments.

That is the practical control risk: actuator velocity limits, torque limits, and numerical noise all appear before the math reaches an exact determinant of zero.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, singularity discussion near Jacobian inversion.
# Design Reading

Singularities are not only software problems. A manipulator that must produce force or velocity in a specific direction should be checked over the task workspace. A flattened manipulability ellipsoid is an early warning that useful motion directions are disappearing.
# Related
- robot-jacobian
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- jacobian-inverse-kinematics
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping

pages.title: Jacobian Transpose Force Mapping
pages.text:
# Jacobian Transpose Force Mapping

The Jacobian transpose maps an end-effector wrench into the joint torques needed to hold or resist it:

$$
\tau=J^T(q)F.
$$

The clean derivation is virtual work or power consistency. Since

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q},
$$

the task-space power and joint-space power must match:

$$
F^T\dot{x}=\tau^T\dot{q}.
$$

Substituting $\dot{x}=J\dot{q}$ and requiring the equality for any $\dot{q}$ gives $\tau=J^TF$.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 8.
# Frame And Sign Discipline

The formula is only meaningful when $J$, $F$, and $\tau$ use compatible coordinates and sign conventions. If $J$ maps joint rates to a task velocity expressed in a particular frame, then the force or wrench must be expressed in the dual coordinates for that same task representation. Changing the Jacobian frame without changing the wrench frame breaks the power equality.

This is one reason space and body Jacobians need explicit frame labels.
# Force Capability

Given joint-torque limits, the same equation can be read backward. If $J^T$ is invertible, the end-effector force that can be counteracted is

$$
F=(J^T(q))^{-1}\tau.
$$

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 9.

Figure: joint torque limits map through $(J^T)^{-1}$ into a configuration-dependent end-effector force set. Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 9.
# Hardware Use

Lecture 4 uses the same relation for payload torque sizing. For a chosen payload force and manipulator configuration, $J^TF$ tells the designer which joint torques are required before selecting actuator mass, rated torque, peak torque, and reflected inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10.

In transparent leg or arm hardware, the relation also runs in the proprioceptive direction: if motor current estimates joint torque accurately and the transmission is low-friction, the robot can estimate contact force through the Jacobian. The estimate is only as good as the torque model, friction compensation, and kinematic model.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II and force-control model discussion.
# Related
- robot-jacobian
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- transmission-transparency
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- space-and-body-jacobians

pages.title: Lecture 1 Study Path
pages.text:
# Lecture 1 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 1: 구동기 설계 개론.

Use this as the entry point if you are new to actuator design and want the concepts in a low-friction order.
For the source-level overview, read lecture-01-actuator-design-introduction first.
# 0. Start Here: Basic Vocabulary

Read motor-actuator-structure first if any of these terms are unclear:
- motor vs actuator
- stator / rotor
- air gap
- winding
- motor driver
- reducer / gearbox
- end turn
- housing

Goal: understand what physical parts the lecture is talking about before reading the design principles.

Optional BLDC/SPMSM branch:
- bldc-pmsm-spmsm — BLDC/PMSM/SPMSM naming
- outrunner-vs-inrunner-bldc-motors — outer-rotor vs inner-rotor packaging and drone propeller fit
- bldc-electronic-commutation — brushes, commutator, and electronic phase switching
- hall-effect-position-sensing — rotor-position feedback and Hall sensor ICs
# 1. Understand the Design Frame

Read pareto-optimality.

Core idea: there is no universally "best" motor. There are trade-off fronts. First eliminate dominated designs, then choose the right point for the application.

Key intuition: MTB vs road bike is not a universal ranking problem; it is an application-fit problem.
# 2. Learn the Three Real Metrics

Read actuator-performance-metrics.

Focus on:
- mass
- max torque
- joint/output inertia
- why power and max speed are misleading standalone metrics
- why "bigger motor + bigger gearbox" is not free

Checkpoint: explain why $10:1$ reduction gives about $10\times$ torque but $100\times$ reflected rotor inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-138; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 12-13 and 22.
# 3. Learn the Three Design Principles

Read actuator-design-principles.

Focus on:
- winding does not change ideal torque capability when geometry/materials are fixed
- winding is mostly driver matching
- fixed mass + fixed max torque largely determines output inertia
- ideal pizza vs kimbap equivalence vs real gearbox/end-turn penalties

Checkpoint: explain why low-turn/thick-wire and high-turn/thin-wire motors can target different voltage/current drivers without changing the ideal motor constant.
# 4. Connect the Metrics to Legged Robots

Read legged-robot-actuation.

Focus on:
- why legged robots care about impact and low passive impedance
- why high reduction can damage backdrivability, force control, efficiency, and durability
- why QDD is a practical design direction, not a magical "low gear ratio is always best" rule
- why manipulators and legs can prefer different points on the same Pareto front

Checkpoint: explain why HOUND/MIT-Cheetah-style leg actuators accept heavier motors to reduce reflected inertia and impact losses.
# 5. Study the Paper Behind the Lecture

Read seok-2012-high-force-proprioceptive-actuation.

Focus on:
- power density vs torque density
- gap radius scaling
- reflected inertia thought experiment
- MIT Cheetah 1:5.8 low-reduction design
- proprioceptive force-control result

Then read transmission-transparency.

Checkpoint: explain why the paper's real target is transmission transparency for force control, not simply a small gear ratio.
# 6. Study the Intrinsic Actuator Quality Metric

Read actuator-constant, then motor-constant for the $Km$ ingredient.

Focus on:
- what $C$ tries to capture
- how $Kt$ and $Rs$ combine into $Km$
- why scaling and gearing do not improve fundamental motor quality
- how better materials/topology/Cu-Iron allocation can improve the real design
- how gap radius relates to $KM^2$ in Seok et al. 2012

Checkpoint: explain the difference between choosing a point on a fixed Pareto front and moving the front itself.
# Suggested Review Loop

After reading the pages once, answer these in your own words:
- Why is power not the right motor metric for legged robot actuators?
- What does output/joint inertia mean?
- Why does reflected rotor inertia scale as $N^2$?
- Why can pizza and kimbap motors be ideal-theory equivalent?
- Why does low reduction win often in practice?
- Why can a well-designed high-ratio transmission still be viable?
- What does transmission transparency enable in proprioceptive force control?

If any answer feels fuzzy, return to the linked concept page and update the wiki with a new clarification.

pages.title: Lecture 1: Introduction to Actuator Design
pages.text:
# Lecture 1: Introduction to Actuator Design

Sources: KAIST DRCD Lab Hardware Lecture Notion export, user-provided transcript, slide PDF, and ChatGPT listening notes.
Slide deck: "Actuator Optimal Design — Lecture 1: Pareto Optimality & Design Principles" (2026-03-02, KAIST DRCD Lab, 송태규 박사과정).
Contributors: @최혁, @Yechan Seo, @JUNE, @최민석, @박경서, @이동호
# Core Question

What makes a "good" motor, and where are the physical limits of its performance?

The lecture answers this through the lens of pareto-optimality — there is no universally "best" actuator, only optimal trade-off points for a given application.
# Structure
# 1. Pareto Optimality in Design
- Design "goodness" requires defined criteria — like MTB vs road bike, the optimal point depends on purpose
- Pareto front = the set of solutions where improving one metric necessarily sacrifices another
- Goal: select the right point on the Pareto front for your application (legged robot, manipulator, etc.)
# 2. Three Performance Metrics
See actuator-performance-metrics for details.
- Mass — total actuator weight. Torque/mass ratio worsens with scale ($\tau \propto L^2$ but mass $\propto L^3$)
- Max Torque — limited by thermal dissipation (heat generation $\propto L^3$, dissipation $\propto L^2$)
- Joint Inertia — $J{out} = N^2 Jm + J{gear}$. Gear ratio $10\times$ → inertia $100\times$. This is why QDD avoids high gear ratios

Key insight: Power, torque-speed curve area, and max angular velocity are NOT the right standalone metrics for actuator evaluation. Electric motor operating regions can be reshaped with gearing and voltage, but mass, max torque, and joint inertia expose the real Pareto trade-off.

The slide deck gives the concrete comparison: a 38g motor rated at 1166W vs a 1042g stator+rotor rated at 2700W. By raw power density, the small motor looks better, but if it needs huge reduction to meet the joint torque target, the reflected inertia can become worse than a heavier, lower-reduction actuator.
# 3. Three Design Principles
See actuator-design-principles for details.
1. Max torque is independent of winding — $KM$ depends on motor geometry/materials, not how you wind the coils
2. Winding is a driver-matching problem — parallel/delta/low-turns for high-current drivers; series/wye/high-turns for high-voltage drivers
3. Given fixed mass and max torque, joint inertia is determined — pizza motor (flat, wide) vs kimbap motor (long, narrow) yield same theoretical inertia at the output

The transcript also distinguishes motor and actuator: a motor is the energy-conversion core (stator + rotor), while an actuator is the usable motion unit, typically motor + reducer + driver/control electronics + housing/sensing. See motor-actuator-structure.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166 and 250-264; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 16, 20, and 22.
# 4. Legged Robot Application
See legged-robot-actuation for details.
- Ground contact is frequent and impulsive → low passive impedance is critical
- High gear ratios can destroy backdrivability, efficiency, and durability through reflected inertia, gearbox inertia, and friction
- Dominant practical direction: high-torque motor + minimum gearing (low reduction)
- Kimbap motors can have better electrical efficiency because long axial length reduces end-turn waste, but pizza motors win in many legged robots because low-reduction gearboxes are simpler and preserve backdrivability
- The lecture explicitly warns against over-reading QDD as "low gear ratio is always theoretically best." In the ideal frictionless model, pizza and kimbap can be equivalent; low reduction is a conservative practical choice because real gearboxes add friction and are hard to model/design well
# 5. Actuator Constant C
See actuator-constant for the corrected actuator-level metric and motor-constant for $Km$.
- Current wiki formula: $C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}$
- Higher C = fundamentally better actuator technology level. Same C = same Pareto front
- C is invariant to simple dimensional scaling and gear ratio changes
- To actually improve C: better magnets, better core materials, better topology, or FEA-driven Cu/Iron allocation for the intended operating region

Source: earlier Lecture 1 version in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166 and 284; corrected relationship tracked in sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117.
# 6. Cu vs Iron Trade-off
- Cu (copper): creates magnetic potential (like a battery)
- Iron: provides flux path (like a wire), but saturates (nonlinear → requires FEA)
- More iron → higher saturation limit → higher max torque
- More copper → higher efficiency → longer/farther operation
# 7. Terminology Clarifications from Listening Notes
- Stator: fixed part that carries windings and creates magnetic field
- Rotor: rotating part connected to the shaft; often carries magnets in BLDC/SPMSM-style motors
- Air gap: small gap between stator and rotor where magnetic shear force is produced; larger gap increases magnetic reluctance and weakens torque
- End turn: winding segment outside the active slot region; it adds resistance/heat but does not directly produce torque
- Motor driver: electronics that supply timed voltage/current to the phases; winding choice should be matched to this driver's voltage/current envelope
# 8. Appendix Design Parameters
The slide appendix groups actuator design knobs into three axes:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

pages.title: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
pages.text:
# Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review

This guide is the reading route for the Lecture 2 homework prompts and Lecture 3A, the homework/modeling review at the start of Lecture 3. The long derivations now live in concept pages so they can be reused outside this homework context.

Use this with spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, motor-winding-configuration, motor-operating-region, and motor-constant.

After this review block, Lecture 3 switches to Lecture 3B, Juminchan's separate Motor Driver / Current Control seminar. Use lecture-03-study-path for that branch.

Source: Lecture 3A/3B boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

Figure: target d-q voltage equations from Lecture 2. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.
# 1. Derive the d-q Voltage Equations

Read abc-to-dq-transform-derivation.

Focus on:
- starting from $\mathbf v{abc}=Rs\mathbf i{abc}+\frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}$
- why only two independent current coordinates remain in a balanced three-wire motor
- why Park/Clarke definitions are convention-dependent
- where $-\omegae\lambdaq$ and $+\omegae\lambdad$ enter
- how the ideal SPMSM assumptions produce the target d-q voltage equations

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.
# 2. Explain Torque From q-Axis Current

Read spmsm-torque-equation.

Focus on:
- the general flux-linkage torque form
- why ideal SPMSM cancels the $Ls id iq$ terms
- why $\taum \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq$ is a linear-model result
- how saliency adds reluctance torque

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# 3. Relate $Kv$, Back EMF, and $Kt$

Read motor-constant-unit-conversions.

Focus on:
- why matching SI $Ke$ and $Kt$ are numerically equal
- how the common $Kt \approx \frac{9.55}{Kv}$ conversion is derived
- why line-line vs phase, RMS vs peak, and q-axis vs line current conventions change constants
- how the oscilloscope back-EMF method estimates torque constant without a torque sensor

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-235; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33.
# 4. Check Wye/Delta Parameter Conventions

Read wye-delta-parameter-conventions.

Focus on:
- why terminal-only ABC tests do not uniquely reveal ideal internal wye vs delta topology
- converting line-to-line resistance to equivalent phase resistance
- why inductance conversion needs more care than scalar resistance conversion
- why motor-control models only need a consistent equivalent-circuit convention

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5.
# 5. Separate Exact Forms From Linear Assumptions

Read spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions.

Focus on:
- which equations remain valid in flux-linkage form
- where constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$ enter
- why saturation changes the apparent torque constant
- why the lecture frames high-current nonlinearity as $B$-$H$ saturation, not hysteresis by itself

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.
# Homework Checklist

Before considering the homework complete, be able to answer:
- Starting from $\mathbf v{abc}=Rs\mathbf i{abc}+\frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}$, where exactly does $+\omegae\phif$ enter the q-axis equation?
- Which term makes the d-axis equation depend on q-axis current?
- Why does $\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right)=-\frac{1}{2}$ appear in the ideal mutual-inductance argument?
- What convention is used for $id$ and $iq$: amplitude-invariant or power-invariant?
- Is a quoted $Kt$ per phase peak, q-axis peak, line RMS, or controller current?
- Is a quoted $Kv$ from DC bus voltage, phase voltage, or line-line RMS back EMF?
- Can a terminal-only resistance/inductance test distinguish physical wye from equivalent delta?
- Which final equations break first when iron saturates?

Source: checklist distilled from sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2, 4, and 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.
# Related
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- spmsm-torque-equation
- motor-constant-unit-conversions
- wye-delta-parameter-conventions
- spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions

pages.title: Lecture 2 Study Path
pages.text:
# Lecture 2 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 2: SPMSM 모델링.

Use this after Lecture 1 if you understand basic actuator parts, winding, and BLDC/SPMSM terminology. The first part of Lecture 3 continues this modeling path as Lecture 3A homework/modeling review; Lecture 3B then branches into Juminchan's motor-driver implementation seminar.

Source: Lecture 3 transition in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.
# 0. Refresh the Prerequisites

Read bldc-pmsm-spmsm and motor-winding-configuration first if any of these are fuzzy:
- why robotics "BLDC" often means SPMSM in stricter language
- surface vs interior permanent magnets
- wye/star vs delta winding
- phase value vs terminal-to-terminal value
- speed constant $Kv$ and torque constant $Kt$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 7.
# 1. Read the Source-Level Summary

Read lecture-02-spmsm-modeling.

Goal: get the lecture's whole arc before diving into the formulas.
# 2. Understand the Torque Mechanisms

Read magnetic-reluctance-torque.

Focus on:
- magnetic torque vs reluctance torque
- why SynRM, IPMSM, and SPMSM sit at different points on the torque-mechanism spectrum
- why ideal SPMSM has little reluctance torque
- why "IPMSM is just too nonlinear to control" is not the full robotics explanation

Then read pmsm-topology-control-consequences as the synthesis page that carries this taxonomy into Chapter 5 MTPA and Chapter 8 high-speed operation. The checkpoint is to explain why the same negative d-axis current can be a reluctance-torque lever in IPMSM MTPA and a flux-weakening lever once voltage becomes limiting.

Textbook extension source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 38-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.
# 3. Learn the Operating-Region View

Read motor-operating-region.

Focus on:
- brushed DC warm-up model
- why back EMF limits torque at high speed
- voltage limit vs current limit
- regeneration and real battery voltage sag/rise
- why a max-torque/max-RPM rectangle is dangerous for sim-to-real

Then read chapter-08-high-speed-operation for the textbook extension: current limits become circles, voltage limits become speed-dependent ellipses, and field weakening is an optimized current-command problem rather than just a note that "$id$ can be negative." Keep pmsm-topology-control-consequences nearby when deciding whether a claim is really about SPMSM, IPMSM, or PMSM control in general.

Checkpoint: explain why a robot policy can be wrong near max RPM even if the simulated actuator has the right max torque and max speed numbers.

Textbook extension source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.
# 4. Study the SPMSM Model

Read spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.

Focus on:
- why ABC phase variables reduce to two d-q coordinates
- the rotor-frame d-q voltage equations
- cross-coupling terms
- field weakening
- why $Rs$ and $Ls$ need convention checking
- which parts are linear approximations affected by saturation

Checkpoint: point to the exact terms that make the SPMSM model differ from the brushed DC model.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

For the control extension, read chapter-05-vector-control. It shows how the same d-q model becomes vector control: rotor-flux or magnet-flux alignment makes one current axis the flux channel and the orthogonal axis the torque channel.

Textbook extension source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-35.
# 5. Work Through the Homework Questions

Read lecture-02-homework-workthrough as the route through the homework pages.

Use the source slides and transcript for the homework-style prompts:
- derive d-q voltage equations from ABC phase equations with abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- explain q-axis torque production with spmsm-torque-equation
- explain why the flux/inductance terms use trigonometric structure
- relate $Kv$ or back-EMF constant to torque constant with motor-constant-unit-conversions
- decide whether an external ABC-terminal test can distinguish wye vs delta winding with wye-delta-parameter-conventions
- identify where Park/Clarke transform conventions change numerical definitions
- separate equations that remain exact from equations that depend on linear magnetic assumptions with spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2, 4, and 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.

pages.title: Lecture 2: SPMSM Modeling
pages.text:
# Lecture 2: SPMSM Modeling

Sources: KAIST DRCD Lab Hardware Lecture Notion note, slide PDFs, and transcript.
Slide deck: "Actuator Optimal Design — Lecture 2: SPMSM Modeling" (2026-03-09, KAIST DRCD Lab, 송태규 박사과정).
# Core Question

How do we model the motor type used in robotics, and why does the model matter for operating limits and sim-to-real?

The lecture builds from motor taxonomy to motor operating regions and then to SPMSM d-q voltage equations.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lecture structure; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.
# Structure
# 1. Robotics "BLDC" is Usually SPMSM

The lecture reinforces the terminology nuance from bldc-pmsm-spmsm: in robotics, the motors casually called BLDC are often better described as SPMSMs: surface permanent magnet synchronous motors.

The taxonomy used in the slides:
- SynRM: synchronous reluctance motor, no permanent magnets, pure reluctance torque
- IPMSM: interior permanent magnet synchronous motor, magnet torque plus reluctance torque from rotor saliency
- SPMSM: surface permanent magnet synchronous motor, mostly magnet torque in the ideal symmetric model

See magnetic-reluctance-torque for the torque-mechanism distinction.
# 2. Magnetic Torque vs Reluctance Torque

Magnetic torque comes from the stator field interacting with permanent magnets. Reluctance torque comes from the rotor moving toward the direction of lower magnetic reluctance.

For the lecture's qualitative model:
- A permanent magnet can be treated like a vacuum-like region for the incremental reluctance-torque argument because its magnetic domains are already aligned.
- Iron matters because its domains can align with the applied field, so flux prefers paths through iron.
- An IPMSM rotor can create path-dependent reluctance because the internal magnet/iron geometry is anisotropic.
- An ideal SPMSM rotor is symmetric after ignoring the surface magnets for reluctance, so it does not produce meaningful reluctance torque.

The lecture asks why robotics usually avoids IPMSM even though IPMSM can produce more maximum torque by combining magnet and reluctance torque. The Q&A rules out one simple guess: it is not merely because IPMSM torque is impossible to control. Properly modeled IPMSM can have smooth current-to-torque behavior, especially in EV-oriented designs. The durable bridge from this lecture question to Chapter 5 MTPA and Chapter 8 high-speed operation is pmsm-topology-control-consequences.
# 3. Magnetic Nonlinearity and Torque Constant

Before using linear motor equations, the lecture flags the main approximation: real magnetic materials do not follow a perfectly linear $B$-$H$ curve.
- Hard magnetic materials, such as permanent magnets, retain aligned magnetic domains.
- Soft magnetic materials, such as stator/rotor iron, have narrow hysteresis loops but still saturate.
- The reason torque eventually stops scaling linearly with current is the nonlinear $B$-$H$ curve and saturation, not hysteresis by itself.
- The usual torque constant is effectively the small-signal slope near the origin; high-current operation can deviate.
# 4. Brushed DC Model and MOR

The brushed DC warm-up model is:

$$
V = iR + Kb\omega + L\frac{di}{dt}
$$

For normal operating-region sketches, the lecture usually neglects the inductive term:

$$
V \approx iR + Kb\omega
$$

Combining this with voltage and current limits gives a MOR in the speed-current or speed-torque plane. The lecture emphasizes:
- The MOR is not a symmetric hexagon made by mirroring a one-quadrant datasheet plot.
- Voltage limits are hard; current limits are semi-hard because they usually represent thermal/core-saturation limits.
- Negative-power regions matter for dynamic robots because braking a moving joint can regenerate energy into the battery.
- Real batteries are not ideal voltage sources, so internal resistance shifts the apparent limit under large discharge or regeneration.
- A URDF-style rectangle using only max torque and max RPM can be badly wrong near high speed, where back EMF consumes the available voltage budget.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, brushed DC/MOR discussion; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, MOR slides.
# 5. SPMSM d-q Voltage Equations

The SPMSM model uses two electrical degrees of freedom because the motor has three phase terminals but Kirchhoff constraints remove one independent degree. The d-q frame rewrites phase variables into a rotor-aligned two-axis coordinate system.

The rotor-frame SPMSM voltage equations are the central object:

$$
v{ds}^{r} = Rs i{ds}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{ds}^{r}}{dt} - \omegar Ls i{qs}^{r}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{r} = Rs i{qs}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{qs}^{r}}{dt} + \omegar Ls i{ds}^{r} + \omegar\phif
$$

The cross-coupling terms are the key difference from the brushed DC model. If the coupling is ignored, the model collapses toward the simpler brushed DC operating-region picture. With the coupling kept, the SPMSM MOR becomes curved, and field weakening can extend the high-speed region.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-482.
# 6. Winding and Parameter Conventions

Lecture 2 adds a caution to motor-winding-configuration: when a datasheet reports resistance or inductance, first check whether it is phase, terminal-to-terminal, wye-equivalent, or delta branch value. The d-q equations use phase values $Rs$ and $Ls$ under the chosen convention.

The slides also ask whether a black-box motor's wye/delta winding can be distinguished using only the external ABC terminals. The lecture frames this as homework rather than giving the answer directly.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-253; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 7.
# 7. Homework / Study Tasks

The homework-oriented slides ask the student to:
- derive the d-q voltage equations from ABC phase equations
- explain why the flux/inductance terms use trigonometric structure
- understand Park/Clarke transformation conventions
- identify which d-q variables are convention-dependent rather than directly measured physical variables
- reason about which equations survive magnetic saturation and which are only linear approximations
- estimate torque constant from back-EMF / speed constant using only an oscilloscope-style measurement setup

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2, 4, and 7; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217 and 473-482.
