pages.title: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
pages.text:
# Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar

Sources: Lecture 3 Notion export, transcript, homework-solution slide PDF, motor-driver seminar slide PDF, and homework PDF.

Lecture 3 should be treated as two related but separate blocks, not as one continuous lecture flow.

| Block | Treat as | Main route |
|---|---|---|
| Lecture 3A | Lecture 2 homework/modeling review: torque constant, wye/delta equivalence, waveform assumptions, d-q conventions, and linear magnetic approximations | lecture-02-homework-workthrough |
| Lecture 3B | Juminchan's separate motor-driver/current-control seminar: inverter, SVPWM, current sensing, d-q PI loop, and driver nonidealities | lecture-03-study-path |

The transcript explicitly closes the homework review before introducing Juminchan at about 37:05, and the following section is framed as a motor-driver seminar on PMSM/IPMSM current control.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.
# Core Question

For Lecture 3B:

How does a torque command become real phase voltage/current in a PMSM driver, and what hardware effects break the ideal model?

The short chain is:

$$
v \rightarrow i \rightarrow \lambda \rightarrow \tau
$$

For an ideal SPMSM, torque is mainly controlled through q-axis current:

$$
\tau \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

where $p$ is pole-pair count and $\phif$ is permanent-magnet flux linkage. The driver therefore regulates current by commanding motor voltage through an inverter.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 352-355; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# 1. Lecture 3A: Lecture 2 Homework Solutions

The first slide deck answers several modeling questions that were left open in Lecture 2.

Torque constant without a torque sensor. The lecture says to measure back EMF instead of torque. Torque constant and back-EMF constant are tied by the motor equations, so an oscilloscope measurement of line-to-line back EMF while spinning the motor can identify $\phif$, then $Kt$. The waveform frequency gives electrical speed; the waveform amplitude, after the chosen phase/line and transform convention is handled, gives the voltage term proportional to $\omegae\phif$.

Wye vs delta from the outside. A black-box stator cannot be distinguished as wye or delta using only the three external ABC terminals. Thevenin-equivalent networks can present the same external behavior. For modeling, use a consistent equivalent-circuit convention, commonly a serial wye-equivalent model, and keep parameter definitions explicit.

Sinusoidal flux is not guaranteed by nature. Three phases are separated by $120^\circ$ electrical, but the waveform does not have to be sinusoidal. Distributed windings and flux-leakage effects can make back EMF close to sinusoidal; trapezoidal BLDC-style machines deliberately differ.

d-q variables are convention-dependent. The lecture's practical rule is that it is acceptable if the forward and inverse transforms are consistent: $x = \mathrm{Park}(\mathrm{Clarke}(x))$ under the selected convention. Many modeling and firmware mistakes come from mixing conventions.

The d-q voltage equations contain approximations. Ideal SPMSM can use $Ld = Lq$, but saliency, IPMSM geometry, rotor-angle-dependent inductance, and current-dependent saturation can break that equality. The linear flux-linkage relation is the approximation to watch.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 29-74, 123-161, and 290-316; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.
# 2. Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar

The motor-driver material after the transition is Juminchan's separate practical seminar. It should be connected to the actuator-design and SPMSM-modeling sequence as a driver/current-control branch, while keeping Lecture 3A as the homework-review closure for Lecture 2.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 19-21 and 135-139; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 324-340.
# 3. Motor Driver as Current Controller

The motor driver is not just a power amplifier. It is a current-control system that uses voltage commands to force measured phase currents to track desired values.

See motor-driver-inverter for the inverter view and motor-current-control for the d-q PI-loop view.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# 4. Inverter and PWM

Real drivers usually do not synthesize smooth analog phase voltages. They switch MOSFETs rapidly and use PWM. The motor winding is a large inductor, so the applied voltage pulses produce smoother current than the voltage waveform itself.

A three-phase inverter is built from three half-bridges, or six switches. Its legal switch states form eight space vectors:
- two zero-voltage vectors, $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$
- six active voltage vectors

High-side MOSFETs require gate-drive circuitry because their source can sit near $V{dc}$; the gate must still be driven above the source. The lecture contrasts older bootstrap circuits with charge-pump gate drivers.
# 5. SVPWM and Voltage Margin

SVPWM combines adjacent active vectors and zero vectors over each switching period to create the desired average voltage vector. Splitting the zero-vector time symmetrically reduces current ripple, and lower current ripple means lower torque ripple.

For normal non-overmodulated SVPWM, the usable voltage region is the inscribed circle inside the space-vector hexagon. The lecture recommends not using the full theoretical boundary; leave roughly 1-5% phase-voltage margin. That margin matters because it:
- avoids overmodulation vibration
- preserves current-sensing windows for low-side shunts
- shrinks the practical motor operating region
# 6. Current Sensing

The lecture focuses on triple-shunt low-side current sensing because it is low-cost and practical.

The signal path is:

$$
i \rightarrow V{shunt}=iR{shunt} \rightarrow \text{op-amp} \rightarrow \text{ADC}
$$

Important implementation details:
- shunt resistors are usually around $1-10\ \mathrm{m\Omega}$
- low-side shunts have a convenient ground reference
- high-side or in-line shunts need better common-mode rejection
- in-line sensing is electrically cleaner but more expensive
- Hall sensors can be attractive in high-current drivers because shunt heating changes resistance
- low-side sensing only works when the relevant low-side FET is on
- ADC samples must be synchronized and placed inside the valid sensing window

Noise, op-amp gain, low-pass filters, ADC scaling, Clarke/Park convention, and ground bounce can all corrupt the current estimate. See motor-current-sensing.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-223; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-18.
# 7. Current Control PI Loop

Ignoring speed-dependent terms at first, each d-q current channel looks like a first-order electrical plant:

$$
G(s)=\frac{i}{V}=\frac{1}{Ls+R}
$$

The lecture uses a PI controller with pole-zero cancellation:

$$
KP = L\omega{des}
$$

$$
KI = R\omega{des}
$$

The resulting closed-loop form is a first-order low-pass response:

$$
Gs(s)=\frac{\omega{des}}{s+\omega{des}}
$$

The speed-dependent cross-coupling and back-EMF terms can be added as feedforward. In practice, the lecture recommends tuning from small gains and starting without feedforward, because incorrect feedforward can make debugging harder.

The command voltage must be saturated to the available inverter voltage, and the integrator needs anti-windup. The lecture mentions backward Euler integration and back-calculation anti-windup.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 21 and 24-25.
# 8. Real Inverter Nonidealities

The ideal command voltage is not the real motor terminal voltage:

$$
V{ref} \neq V{real}
$$

Lecture 3B flags the main causes:
- sensor noise and low-pass-filter delay
- discrete-time control delay
- MOSFET/gate-driver dead time
- body-diode voltage drop during dead time
- MOSFET turn-on/off delay
- gate charge and switching ringing from parasitic capacitance/inductance
- PCB layout and ground-bounce problems

Current feedback can often tolerate these errors, but voltage-model-based sensorless control is more sensitive because it depends on the actual applied voltage. See motor-driver-nonidealities.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 26-31.
# Homework Assigned

The Lecture 3 homework asks for:
- derivation of copper loss and torque-constant formulas, including where the linear magnetic approximation enters
- SPMSM MOR plotting from d-q voltage equations, including whether $id=0$ is optimal at all speeds
- resistive braking current and torque when all three phase terminals are shorted

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - homework.pdf.
# Related

Lecture 3A / modeling review:
- lecture-02-homework-workthrough
- motor-constant
- motor-constant-unit-conversions
- wye-delta-parameter-conventions
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
- spmsm-torque-equation
- spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions

Lecture 3B / Juminchan motor-driver seminar:
- motor-driver-inverter
- space-vector-pwm
- motor-current-sensing
- motor-current-control
- motor-driver-nonidealities
- motor-operating-region

pages.title: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
pages.text:
# Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path

This is the reading order for Lecture 3B, Juminchan's separate Motor Driver / Current Control seminar.

Use lecture-02-homework-workthrough for Lecture 3A, the Lecture 2 homework/modeling review that comes before the seminar. Use this page only after you already understand the basic SPMSM d-q model and motor-operating-region idea.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340; route synthesized from sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 135-223 and 225-299; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 12-25.
# 0. Close Lecture 3A if Needed

Read lecture-02-homework-workthrough first if the Lecture 2 homework corrections are still unresolved.

Focus on:
- torque constant estimation from back EMF
- wye/delta equivalent-circuit conventions
- Park/Clarke convention dependence
- exact flux-linkage forms vs linear SPMSM approximations

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-318; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.
# 1. Refresh the Modeling Prerequisites

Read spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, motor-winding-configuration, motor-operating-region, and pmsm-topology-control-consequences first if any of these are fuzzy:
- phase values vs terminal-to-terminal values
- Park/Clarke convention dependence
- why d-q voltage limits shape the MOR
- why q-axis current is torque-producing in ideal SPMSM
- why negative d-axis current means different things in IPMSM MTPA and high-speed PMSM flux weakening

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# 2. Read the Source-Level Summary

Read lecture-03-motor-driver-and-homework-review.

Goal: see the Lecture 3A / 3B boundary before studying the seminar-specific concepts.
# 3. Learn What the Inverter Is Doing

Read motor-driver-inverter.

Focus on:
- why the driver controls current by applying voltage
- why PWM is used instead of true analog voltage
- half-bridge and three-phase inverter switch states
- zero vectors and active vectors
- why high-side MOSFETs need gate-driver boost circuitry
# 4. Study SVPWM and Voltage Margin

Read space-vector-pwm.

Focus on:
- time-weighted mixing of active and zero vectors
- why symmetric zero-vector placement reduces ripple
- why the usable voltage region is the inscribed circle
- why 1-5% voltage margin is kept in practice
- how that margin shrinks the real MOR
# 5. Understand Current Measurement

Read motor-current-sensing.

Focus on:
- shunt resistor measurement
- low-side vs in-line sensing
- ADC timing windows
- why voltage margin affects sensing
- CMRR, op-amp gain, unit scaling, noise, and ground bounce
# 6. Build the Current Loop

Read motor-current-control.

Focus on:
- first-order $RL$ plant model
- PI gains from pole-zero cancellation
- desired bandwidth
- cross-coupling and back-EMF feedforward
- voltage saturation and anti-windup
- why practical tuning starts small and often omits feedforward at first
# 7. Remember the Hardware Nonidealities

Read motor-driver-nonidealities.

Focus on:
- dead time and body-diode voltage drop
- MOSFET delay and gate charge
- switching ringing
- sensor/filter/control delay
- why current control can tolerate more voltage error than sensorless voltage-model control
# 8. Add the Textbook Reinforcement Branch

After the lecture route, use the textbook chapter summaries to deepen each layer without changing the lecture order:
- chapter-05-vector-control for why AC vector control tries to reproduce the DC-motor separation of flux and torque current
- chapter-06-current-regulators for synchronous-frame PI gains, feedforward, and anti-windup as the formal current-loop basis
- chapter-07-pwm-inverters for SVPWM voltage vectors, modulation index, overmodulation, dead time, and PWM-synchronized current measurement
- chapter-08-high-speed-operation for the voltage/current limit geometry behind field weakening
- chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control for resolver/encoder feedback, M/T speed estimation, and sensorless-control limits

Use pmsm-topology-control-consequences to connect the Chapter 5 PMSM MTPA material to the Chapter 8 flux-weakening material without folding that topology discussion into the motor-driver implementation pages.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-35; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-76; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.
# 9. Homework Branch

For the Lecture 3 homework, return to spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, motor-constant, and motor-operating-region. This homework uses the same SPMSM model, but it should not blur the wiki boundary: Lecture 3A is the earlier homework review, and Lecture 3B is the Juminchan motor-driver seminar.

The assigned problems ask you to:
- identify where linear magnetic assumptions enter torque/copper-loss formulas
- plot SPMSM MOR from the d-q voltage equations
- compare $id=0$ operation against optimized $id$ by speed
- derive d-q short-circuit currents and braking torque under resistive braking

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - homework.pdf; supporting model material in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

pages.title: Lecture 4 Study Path
pages.text:
# Lecture 4 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 4: Actuator Design Process.

Use this after Lecture 1, Lecture 2, the Lecture 3A modeling review, and the motor-driver basics from Lecture 3B if you understand pareto-optimality, actuator-performance-metrics, and spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.
For the full chain across all four lectures, see actuator-design-to-current-control-map.
# 1. Read the Source-Level Summary

Read lecture-04-actuator-design-process.

Goal: understand the design arc before diving into each requirement.
# 2. Separate Requirements From Optimization

Read actuator-requirement-selection.

Focus on:
- choosing a point on the current Pareto front
- pushing the Pareto front as a separate activity
- primary vs secondary requirements
- the corrected normalized actuator relationship from Lecture 4
# 3. Work Through T-M-I

Read torque-mass-inertia-selection.

Focus on:
- distal-to-proximal manipulator design
- torque from payload via $\tau = J^T F$
- static QDD assumptions
- reducer ratio as the remaining inertia knob after motor selection
- the 1-DOF dynamic rule $N^2Jr = J{link}$
# 4. Clarify Backdrivability

Read backdrivability.

Focus on the difference between:
- backward efficiency
- reverse-drive starting torque / Coulomb friction
- reflected inertia

Use transmission-transparency afterward if you want the proprioceptive force-control motivation.
# 5. Compare Reducer Types

Read robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

Focus on:
- planetary vs cycloidal vs harmonic drives
- torque density vs efficiency
- why serial spur gears are not enough
- why harmonic drives suit CNC-style position control better than QDD-style low-friction control
- why cycloidal drives are attractive in theory but hard to manufacture well
# 6. Keep the Open Ends Visible

Lecture 4 is the first half of the design process. Reducer detailed design and structural-component design are deferred to later material.

pages.title: Lecture 4: Actuator Design Process
pages.text:
# Lecture 4: Actuator Design Process

Sources: Lecture 4 Notion export, transcript, and slide PDF.

Lecture 4 shifts from motor modeling to a practical actuator design workflow. It covers the first half of the design process: requirements, torque-mass-inertia selection, and reducer type selection. Reducer detailed design and structural-component design are deferred.
# Core Question

Given a robot concept, how do we choose actuator requirements before designing or selecting hardware?

The lecture's process is:

1. define requirements
2. define torque-mass-inertia (T-M-I)
3. select reducer type
4. design reducer
5. design structural components

Figure: Lecture 4 actuator design process. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 2.
# 1. Define Requirements Before Optimization

Requirements must be chosen before actuator optimization. Lecture 4 distinguishes two different activities:
- choosing where to sit on the current Pareto front
- pushing the Pareto front outward by better topology, materials, manufacturing, or reducer quality

The lecture uses the mountain-bike analogy: decide whether the product is an MTB or road bike before optimizing materials and bearings. For robot actuators, this means deciding what kind of robot and task envelope you want before trying to improve hardware quality.

See actuator-requirement-selection.
# 2. Primary vs Secondary Requirements

Lecture 4 states that actuator primary requirements are:
- mass
- rated torque
- joint/output inertia
- peak or saturation torque

These are fundamentally coupled. Improving one generally worsens at least one other at the same design/manufacturing level.

Secondary requirements include:
- backlash
- volume
- stiffness
- velocity / power

The lecture calls these secondary because their limits are practical rather than fundamental in the same way: manufacturing precision, driver voltage/current capability, assembly quality, and cost can improve them without the same clean Maxwell/F=ma coupling.
# 3. Corrected Normalized Actuator Metric

Lecture 4 explicitly says the earlier normalized actuator metric was corrected again. The slide gives the current relationship as:

$$
C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}
$$

where $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}$, $\tau{sat}$ is torque limited by core saturation, $M$ is copper mass, and $I$ is joint inertia.

For off-the-shelf frameless motors, $Km$ and $\tau{sat}$ are usually coupled by the motor family/design. That reduces practical selection to torque, mass, and inertia, with one relationship constraining them. See actuator-constant, motor-constant, and actuator-performance-metrics.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.
# 4. T-M-I Selection Workflow

For a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator, the lecture gives a distal-to-proximal workflow:

1. define kinematic parameters
2. set rated and peak payload
3. define wrist actuator T-M-I
4. define forearm mass
5. define elbow actuator T-M-I
6. define upper-arm mass
7. define shoulder actuator T-M-I

If the system becomes too heavy or fails the goal, iterate backward. If iteration cannot meet the target, lower the goal or push the Pareto front with better motor, material, or reducer design.

Torque is set from payload and child-link mass using Jacobian transpose force mapping:

$$
\tau = J^T F
$$

For fixed-base industrial manipulators, the design often considers worst configuration. For mobile anthropomorphic manipulators, the lecture argues it is acceptable to consider favorable configurations because the base/body can move to make the task mechanically reasonable.

See torque-mass-inertia-selection.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155 and 223-229; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 7 and 10-11.
# 5. Static vs Dynamic Design

For static QDD manipulator design with off-the-shelf frameless motors:
- lighter motors are better
- maximize reduction ratio within the QDD/backdrivability limit
- check joint velocity under the given battery voltage
- reducer mass depends mainly on peak joint/output torque, not motor torque

For dynamic design, the metric is not maximum speed but maximum angular acceleration. In a 1-DOF system, acceleration is maximized when reflected rotor inertia equals link inertia:

$$
N^2Jr = J{link}
$$

Below that critical ratio, both static torque and dynamic acceleration can be worse. Above it, static performance improves while dynamic performance trades off.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 12-13.
# 6. Reducer Type Selection

Lecture 4 covers common robot reducer families:
- single-stage planetary drive
- two-stage planetary drive
- 3K compound planetary drive
- cycloidal drive
- harmonic drive

Reducer primary requirements are torque density and efficiency; these have a fundamental trade-off. Backlash is important in robotics, but the lecture treats it as secondary because precision/manufacturing and preload decisions dominate it.

See robot-actuator-reducer-selection.
# 7. Backdrivability Is Ambiguous

The lecture warns that "backdrivability" is used loosely. It can mean:
- backward efficiency
- reverse-drive starting torque / Coulomb friction
- reflected joint inertia

For this wiki, use backdrivability mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior, and discuss reflected inertia separately.
# Related
- actuator-requirement-selection
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping
- robot-actuator-reducer-selection
- backdrivability
- actuator-constant
- motor-constant
- actuator-performance-metrics
- transmission-transparency

pages.title: Lecture 5 Study Path
pages.text:
# Lecture 5 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant.

> WARNING
> The transcript is incomplete between 22:48 and 54:56. Use the summary and selected slide-derived concept pages as the primary route through the missing middle.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 220-240.
# 1. Start With the Source Summary

Read lecture-05-analytical-torque-constant.

Goal: separate the Lecture 3 homework-solution prelude from the main Lecture 5 derivation.
# 2. Close the Homework Threads

Read motor-operating-region, pmsm-topology-control-consequences, and resistive-braking.

Focus on the field-weakening comparison in the MOR solution and the safety interpretation of resistive braking.

Use chapter-08-high-speed-operation as the textbook cross-check for the field-weakening side: it explains why negative d-axis current changes the PMSM voltage boundary and why the feasible region is the intersection of current and voltage limits. Use pmsm-topology-control-consequences to keep that homework result tied to the SPMSM/IPMSM distinction rather than reading field weakening as a generic add-on to the SPMSM torque-constant derivation.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.
# 3. Rebuild the EMF Link

Read motional-emf-and-flux-linkage.

Focus on why $e=Blv$ is an electromotive-force statement, not a guarantee of terminal current, and why EMF can be integrated into flux linkage.
# 4. Understand the Winding Representation

Read winding-function-and-factor.

Focus on conductor density, winding function, overlap with radial magnet flux, and why winding factor is a correction for imperfect addition of conductor EMF contributions.
# 5. Derive the Torque Constant

Read analytical-torque-constant-spmsm, then revisit motor-constant and actuator-constant.

Focus on the final relationship:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g}BrNRl
$$

and on the design warning that pole-pair count is not an independent torque-constant multiplier when total effective conductor count is fixed.
# 6. Keep the Deferred Material Visible

Lecture 5 is Part 1. Magnetic-circuit modeling, real winding choices, saturation torque, and the final actuator-limit argument are deferred.

For the broader chain, return to actuator-design-to-current-control-map.

pages.title: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
pages.text:
# Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant

This source bundle contains a Lecture 3 homework-solution prelude and Lecture 5, "Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant (Part 1)." The source files are organized under sources/lectures/lecture-05/; their contents remain unchanged.

Sources: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md and sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf.

> WARNING
> The transcript has a large gap from 22:48 to 54:56, caused by recording trouble. The pages covering magnetic co-energy, flux linkage setup, and part of the winding-function derivation are therefore summarized from the slide deck and the later resumed transcript rather than from continuous spoken explanation.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 220-240.
# Homework Solution Prelude

The first twelve slides are Lecture 3 homework solutions. The durable items for the wiki are:
- The motor operating region (MOR) homework uses the SPMSM d-q voltage equation, ignores heating and magnetic saturation, and compares the $id=0$ boundary against a field-weakening-current (FWC) boundary.
- The example parameters are $V{DC}=100.8\,\mathrm{V}$, 30 poles, terminal-to-terminal $R{tt}=0.42\,\Omega$, $L{tt}=1.58\,\mathrm{mH}$, and $Kt=0.5881\,\mathrm{Nm/A}$.
- The plotted solution shows that $id=0$ is not the best high-speed strategy; choosing negative d-axis current extends the feasible q-axis current region at high electrical speed.

Figure: MOR homework prompt and parameters. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 5.

Figure: $id=0$ boundary compared with the field-weakening-current boundary. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 9.

Problem 3 introduces resistive braking as a safety behavior: shorting or otherwise resistively loading the motor can remove kinetic energy quickly and create braking current. The lecture frames this as a HOUND2 safety behavior for stopping a leg before self-collision or damage.

Figure: resistive-braking q-axis current plot and HOUND2 demonstration image. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 12.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 46-54; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 5-12.
# Lecture Goal

Lecture 5 asks why an analytical torque-constant model matters. Physical testing can measure $Kt$ after fabrication, and finite-element analysis can estimate it numerically, but a design workflow needs a low-dimensional hand model before testing every candidate geometry. The lecture's target is to estimate SPMSM torque constant from cross-section geometry, axial length, magnet grade, and winding pattern before manufacturing or full FEM.

The slide-level result is:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

Figure: analytical torque-constant target formula and variable definitions. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72 and 86-90; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 13 and 20.
# Motional EMF and Voltage Are Different

The lecture revisits $e=Blv$ from a moving-conductor viewpoint. The important distinction is that $e$ is electromotive force, not ordinary terminal voltage. In the lecture's examples, a rod moving through a uniform magnetic field can have an induced EMF between its ends, but current does not necessarily flow unless the physical circuit provides a return path. The rail example is assigned as a way to separate EMF, voltage, resistance, and the chosen stationary reference frame.

This matters because the later motional-EMF derivation treats each conductor segment in the motor as a small generator, then accumulates those contributions into phase flux linkage and torque.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 95-191.
# From EMF to Flux Linkage

After the transcript gap, the lecture resumes by saying the derivation had just computed EMF, while the desired quantity was flux linkage. The bridge is that EMF is the time derivative of flux linkage, so integrating the motional-EMF result gives the flux-linkage form.

For the simplified radial-field model, the relevant overlap is:

$$
\lambda(\theta)
=
R l \int0^{2\pi}
Br(\phi-\theta)N(\phi)\,d\phi
$$

Only the radial component of the air-gap flux density is retained in this model. The winding function $N(\phi)$ acts like the angular window that counts how much conductor linkage sees each part of the rotor magnet field.

Figure: conductor density and winding function for the simplified SPMSM cross-section. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Figure: radial magnet field waveform overlapped with the winding function. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 29.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 271-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 26-30.
# Winding Factor and Pole-Count Insight

The derivation first uses an ideal concentrated full-pitch winding with winding factor $kw=1$, then introduces $kw$ as the efficiency of a real winding pattern relative to that ideal. The lecture's practical interpretation is that a winding factor below 1 means some conductor EMF contributions partially cancel instead of adding perfectly.

The most important design warning is about pole count: increasing pole-pair count does not directly multiply torque constant if total conductor count and effective linkage are fixed. More poles can still help real motors by enabling shorter end turns, better practical concentrated windings, thinner magnetic paths, and lower mass, but the final ideal formula does not contain pole-pair count as an independent torque-constant multiplier.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 318-424; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 31-34.
# Torque Result

For three balanced phases with $id=0$, the slide result sums the phase torques into:

$$
\tau = 3 Bm R l N iq
$$

and then substitutes the simplified magnet field amplitude $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$ and real-winding correction $kw$ to obtain:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

Figure: three-phase torque summation and the final analytical $Kt$ expression. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

The lecture closes by framing this as one half of a larger actuator-limit argument. Mass and inertia can be computed directly from geometry, while torque constant and saturation torque need electromagnetic modeling. Lecture 5 handles the EMF/flux-linkage side; magnetic-circuit modeling and saturation are deferred. In the broader wiki, this geometry-level $Kt$ result feeds motor-constant and then actuator-constant rather than replacing either page.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 382-386 and 468-483; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.
# Related
- analytical-torque-constant-spmsm
- motional-emf-and-flux-linkage
- winding-function-and-factor
- resistive-braking
- motor-operating-region
- motor-constant
- actuator-constant

pages.title: Legged Robot Actuation
pages.text:
# Legged Robot Actuation

Why legged robot motors are heavy: they use large, high-torque motors with minimal gear reduction. This is not a design flaw — it's the optimal solution.

Reference: Seok et al. (2012) + KAIST DRCD lecture
# Core Constraint: Contact Dynamics

Legged robots experience frequent, impulsive ground contact. This makes low passive impedance (reflected inertia + friction + damping) the dominant design requirement — more important than raw output power. Seok et al. frame this as maximizing transmission-transparency for high-force proprioceptive control.

The transcript contrasts this with manipulators: arms are often dominated by static load, precision, and force control, while legs are dominated by repeated impacts. This is why leg actuators moved toward QDD earlier and more aggressively than many manipulator joints.
# Why High Gear Ratios Fail for Legs
# 1. Control Performance
- High reflected inertia ($N^2 J{motor}$) kills force-control bandwidth
- Contact force tracking degrades
- Backdrivability/transparency destroyed
- Force estimation from motor current becomes less reliable unless transmission impedance is modeled/compensated
# 2. Efficiency
- Touchdown is roughly a quasi-inelastic impact, where impact energy scales with:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2
$$
- Larger reflected $J$ → more energy lost per impact
- $\tau{out} \approx N\tau{motor}$, but $J{ref} \approx N^2J{motor}$ — inertia grows faster than torque

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 116-138; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22; sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II.
# 3. Durability
- Large reflected inertia + low backdrivability → impact torque transients concentrate on gears
- High-ratio or poorly backdrivable harmonic/planetary geartrains can suffer accelerated fatigue, wear, backlash growth, and failure under repeated leg impacts
- This is not a ban on all planetary gearing: a simple low-ratio single-stage planetary can still fit QDD, while multi-stage or high-ratio planetary reducers add the inertia, friction, backlash, and part-count penalties that QDD tries to avoid

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 218-230; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 279-475.
# Design Direction: High-Torque Motor + Minimum Gearing
- Maximize gap radius (rgap) to increase motor torque density
- Use the lowest possible gear ratio (ideally direct drive or single-stage)
- Accept heavier motor mass as the trade-off

This is the QDD (Quasi-Direct Drive) philosophy: sacrifice some mass efficiency to gain transparency, backdrivability, and robustness.
# Important Nuance

The lecture argues against the oversimplified reading "QDD = low gear ratio is always theoretically best." In the ideal model, pizza and kimbap motor shapes can have the same output torque and reflected inertia if mass and technology level are fixed. The low-reduction choice becomes attractive because real gearboxes add friction, inertia, mass, backlash, and modeling difficulty.

Put differently: low reduction is often a robust engineering shortcut to low passive impedance. A high-reduction actuator can still be viable if the transmission is exceptionally efficient and backdrivable.

This distinction is especially important for reducer selection. Harmonic drives usually sit awkwardly with QDD-style legs because high reduction, preload/friction, and flexspline fatigue work against force transparency. Planetary drives split by implementation: the MIT Cheetah example below uses a low-ratio one-stage planetary, while a multi-stage/high-ratio planetary geartrain would drift away from the QDD goal.

Lecture 4 gives the cleaner vocabulary: backdrivability should usually mean friction/reverse-drive behavior, while reflected inertia should be tracked separately. Legged robots need both low friction and low reflected inertia because contact is impulsive.
# Seok et al. 2012 MIT Cheetah Example

The paper referenced by Lecture 1 applies the design argument to the MIT Cheetah front leg:

Figure: MIT Cheetah front leg layout with drive components near the shoulder to reduce distal leg inertia. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Fig. 1.
- Selected the largest motor radius that fit a 5 inch space constraint
- Used a minimal 1:5.8 gear ratio instead of more than 1:100 ratios common in many EM legged-robot actuators
- Designed for roughly 100 Nm desired peak joint torque, with a 21 Nm motor peak torque
- Used one-stage planetary gearing with four planet gears
- Located hip/knee motors and gear trains coaxially at the hip, with the knee driven by a steel linkage
- Reduced distal inertia using foam-core composite leg links and a tendon-reinforced foot

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section III and Figs. 1, 4, and 5.

The force-control experiment showed that after friction compensation, proprioceptive force estimates from motor current tracked force-sensor measurements during impact with mean absolute error ratios of 0.041 and 0.049 for 3406 N/m and 5038 N/m virtual stiffness tests.
# Pizza vs Kimbap in Practice

The third design principle shows that ideal scaling makes pizza and kimbap motors equivalent in output inertia. But in reality:
- Kimbap + high reduction: gearbox adds mass, inertia, friction → breaks ideal scaling
- Pizza + low reduction: simpler gearbox, fewer loss sources → easier to achieve good control

> Practical conclusion: pizza motor (flat, wide) + minimum gearing is the dominant architecture for legged robots.

The transcript adds a useful reversal: kimbap/long motors are not inherently inferior. Because longer motors waste less copper in end turns, they can be electrically more efficient. Their problem in legged robots is usually the high-quality high-reduction transmission required to preserve QDD-like behavior.
# One-Line Summary

> Legged robots are contact-driven systems where high reduction creates reflected inertia ($N^2J$) + gearbox penalties that simultaneously destroy efficiency, force control, and durability. High-torque motor + minimum gearing to secure low passive impedance is the key.

More precise version: high-torque motor + minimum gearing is the conservative default for low passive impedance; high-ratio designs may compete only when the transmission is unusually efficient, low-friction, and impact-tolerant.
# Related

- actuator-design-principles
- actuator-constant
- motor-constant
- pareto-optimality
- motor-actuator-structure
- transmission-transparency
- backdrivability
- robot-actuator-reducer-selection

pages.title: Magnetic Torque and Reluctance Torque
pages.text:
# Magnetic Torque and Reluctance Torque

Electric motors can produce torque through permanent-magnet field alignment, magnetic reluctance alignment, or both.

Figure: magnetic torque aligns a magnet with an electromagnet; reluctance torque aligns an iron rotor with the lower-reluctance flux path. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 4.
# Magnetic Torque

Magnetic torque comes from interaction between the stator's current-generated magnetic field and permanent magnets on the rotor.

In the lecture's simple picture:
- the stator winding acts like an electromagnet
- the rotor permanent magnet has a preferred magnetic direction
- current makes the stator field pull the rotor magnet into alignment

This is the dominant ideal torque mechanism in SPMSM motors used in many robot actuators.
# Reluctance Torque

Reluctance torque comes from a rotor moving toward the orientation that gives magnetic flux the easier path.

The lecture's intuition is:
- iron is a much easier flux path than air/vacuum
- a rotor with direction-dependent iron/air paths has different reluctance depending on angle
- applying stator current makes the rotor rotate toward the lower-reluctance orientation

So reluctance torque is not "magnet pulls magnet"; it is the motor using a geometry-dependent magnetic-resistance difference.

The textbook formalizes this with magnetic reluctance $\mathcal R$. For a coil with $N$ turns:

$$
L=\frac{\lambda}{i}=\frac{N^2}{\mathcal R}
$$

So geometry that changes the air-gap reluctance also changes inductance. In a rotating machine, torque can come from the rotor moving toward the angle where the magnetic system has lower reluctance and higher inductance for the applied current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 10 and 23-25.
# Why Magnets Can Be Ignored in the Reluctance Argument

For the reluctance-torque intuition, the lecture treats permanent magnets as vacuum-like obstacles rather than as extra iron.

The reason is local and qualitative: in a good permanent magnet, the magnetic domains are already aligned. Additional stator field does not align much more material in the same way it does in soft iron. For deciding whether a flux path has easy iron or difficult non-iron regions, the magnet region can be grouped with high-reluctance regions.

This does not mean a permanent magnet is physically the same as vacuum. It means the magnet is not the part that creates the useful incremental saliency for reluctance torque in this simplified argument.
# Motor-Type Spectrum

Figure: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM arranged from reluctance-torque-dominant to magnet-torque-dominant. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 3.

The slide places motor types on a rough spectrum:
- SynRM: pure reluctance motor; no permanent magnet
- PM-assisted SynRM / IPMSM / inset SPMSM: mixed magnet and reluctance torque
- SPMSM: mostly pure magnet torque in the ideal symmetric model

For an ideal SPMSM, if you ignore the surface magnets for the reluctance calculation, the remaining rotor iron looks nearly rotationally symmetric. There is no strong angle-dependent flux path, so ideal reluctance torque is near zero.

For an IPMSM, the internal magnet cavities and iron bridges create anisotropic flux paths. That saliency lets the motor use reluctance torque in addition to magnet torque.

Chapter 4 presents the same point in PMSM modeling language: for an IPMSM, $Ld$ and $Lq$ differ, so the torque equation contains both a permanent-magnet term and a reluctance/saliency term. For an ideal SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the reluctance term largely cancels.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 48-49.
# Control Consequences

In vector-control terms, saliency gives IPMSM one extra design lever. Chapter 5 shows that when $Lq>Ld$, negative d-axis current can make reluctance torque add to magnet torque, so maximum-torque-per-ampere control is not simply $id=0$ as it is for the ideal SPMSM.

Chapter 8 adds the high-speed consequence. In a PMSM, magnet flux cannot be reduced directly, so flux weakening uses negative d-axis current to oppose the magnet flux. That makes larger d-axis inductance useful: it gives each ampere of negative d-axis current more authority over effective flux linkage. The combined insight is captured in pmsm-topology-control-consequences: IPMSM saliency helps both low-speed MTPA and high-speed flux weakening, while SPMSM keeps simpler low-speed control but usually has a weaker high-speed flux-weakening lever.

This is why IPMSM is attractive in applications that need wide constant-power speed range. It does not automatically mean IPMSM is the right robot actuator choice; the robotics question also depends on manufacturability, rotor stress, torque ripple, saliency maps, sensor/control complexity, thermal behavior, and the joint speed range.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 38-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; see chapter-05-vector-control and chapter-08-high-speed-operation.

> QUESTION
> Lecture 2 asks why robotics usually uses SPMSM rather than IPMSM, even though IPMSM can combine magnet torque and reluctance torque. Chapter 8 explains why IPMSM is favored for high-speed operation, but it still does not fully answer the robotics-vs-EV topology trade-off. Revisit this when a source covers robot actuator motor topology selection directly.

pages.title: Manipulability Ellipsoid
pages.text:
# Manipulability Ellipsoid

The manipulability ellipsoid visualizes how a bounded set of joint velocities maps into end-effector velocities through the Jacobian.

If joint velocities satisfy

$$
\dot{q}^T\dot{q}\le 1,
$$

then the possible end-effector velocities are

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q}.
$$

For a two-dimensional task, the unit disk in joint-velocity space becomes an ellipse in end-effector-velocity space. For a spatial task, the same idea becomes an ellipsoid.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 7.

Figure: a unit disk of joint velocities maps through $J(q)$ into a configuration-dependent end-effector velocity ellipsoid. Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 7.
# How To Read It

Long axes mean directions where the end effector can move quickly for a small joint-rate effort. Short axes mean directions where the same joint-rate effort produces little motion.

A very flat ellipsoid means the manipulator is close to a singular configuration. In that posture, some directions are almost unavailable, and moving in those directions requires large joint velocities.
# Velocity Limits Versus Torque Limits

The velocity ellipsoid starts from a joint-velocity limit. A related force-capability plot starts from joint-torque limits and uses the reciprocal force relation

$$
\tau=J^T(q)F.
$$

When $J^T$ is invertible, allowable joint torques can be mapped into the end-effector forces the robot can counteract. A posture that is good for velocity in one direction may be weak for force in a related direction, so both views matter during actuator and task-workspace design.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 8-9.
# Related

- jacobian-singularities
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping

pages.title: Motional EMF and Flux Linkage
pages.text:
# Motional EMF and Flux Linkage

Motional EMF is the electromotive force induced when a conductor moves through a magnetic field. Lecture 5 uses it as the entry point for deriving analytical SPMSM torque constant from geometry.
# EMF Is Not Terminal Voltage

For a straight conductor of active length $l$ moving perpendicular to flux density $B$ at speed $v$, the induced EMF magnitude is:

$$
e = B l v
$$

The lecture emphasizes that $e$ is not simply the same thing as terminal voltage. A moving rod in a uniform magnetic field can develop EMF between its ends, but without a closed return path there is no steady circuit current. Once rails, a load, and a voltmeter are added, the measured voltage depends on the actual circuit and the reference frame used for the measurement.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 140-187.

The DC motor chapter uses the same conductor-level relation to introduce back-EMF, then writes the armature back-EMF as:

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

That is the machine-level version of $e=Blv$: the conductor speed scales with rotor speed, and the summed winding EMF scales with field flux and angular velocity.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-5.
# Why Lecture 5 Starts Here

Each active conductor in a motor can be treated as a small moving-conductor EMF source. Summing those small contributions over the winding gives a phase EMF. Since EMF is the time derivative of flux linkage, the same calculation can be integrated into phase flux linkage:

$$
e = \frac{d\lambda}{dt}
$$

The transcript resumes after the recording gap by stating exactly this bridge: the lecture had just computed EMF, while flux linkage is recovered by integrating that EMF in time.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 271-279.

The textbook introduces flux linkage as $\lambda=N\phi$ for an $N$-turn coil and inductance as $L=\lambda/i$. It also shows why this relation is only locally linear: once the iron core saturates, extra current produces much less extra flux, and the effective inductance drops.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 9-10.
# Stationary-Frame Convention

Lecture 5 keeps the discussion in a stationary reference frame. The moving conductor sees charge redistribution until the body-frame electric field reaches the steady-state condition, while the stationary-frame observer accounts for the velocity-cross-magnetic-field term. For the wiki, keep the sign convention attached to the chosen current, path, and phase definitions; the durable magnitude relationship is the conductor-level link between motion, magnetic field, EMF, and flux linkage.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 95-133.
# Connection to Torque

Flux linkage is the bridge from motion-induced voltage to torque. Once the winding flux linkage is expressed as a function of rotor angle, magnetic co-energy or virtual work can produce torque by differentiating with respect to rotor angle. Lecture 5 uses that route to separate useful current-generated torque from cogging and reluctance effects before returning to the compact $Kt$ formula.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 207-224; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 22-25.
# Related

- spmsm-torque-equation

pages.title: Motor and Actuator Structure
pages.text:
# Motor and Actuator Structure

Basic terminology needed for actuator-design-principles and legged-robot-actuation.
# Motor vs Actuator

Motor = the energy-conversion core. In this lecture, it mainly means the electromagnetic machine made from stator + rotor that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.

Actuator = the usable motion unit. For robot joints, this usually includes:
- motor
- reducer / gearbox
- motor driver or controller electronics
- sensing such as encoder or rotor-position sensing
- housing and mechanical support

The lecture prefers keeping these terms separate because the third design principle is about the whole actuator, not just the bare motor.

Gearbox / reducer = a mechanical transmission between the motor shaft and output joint. It usually reduces speed and multiplies torque: a $10:1$ gearbox makes the motor rotate about 10 times for one output rotation and ideally gives about $10\times$ output torque before losses. Real gearboxes also add inertia, friction, backlash/compliance, mass, and efficiency loss.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - transcript.md, motor/actuator terminology discussion; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-239.
# Core Motor Parts

Stator = fixed part. It usually contains the iron core/teeth and copper windings. Current in the windings creates the magnetic field.

Rotor = rotating part. In BLDC/SPMSM-style motors, it often carries permanent magnets and back iron, and it connects mechanically to the shaft.

Figure: basic BLDC motor system elements: controller, driver circuit, windings, Hall-effect sensor ICs, and power input. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 2.

For the naming distinction, see bldc-pmsm-spmsm. For how the driver replaces mechanical brushes/commutators, see bldc-electronic-commutation.

Air gap = the small gap between stator and rotor. Magnetic force is transmitted across this gap. A larger gap increases magnetic reluctance, weakens the air-gap flux, and reduces torque for the same excitation.

Winding = copper wire wound on the stator. Turns, wire diameter, series/parallel choice, and wye/delta connection change voltage/current matching, but the ideal torque capability is constrained by geometry/materials. See actuator-design-principles and bldc-electronic-commutation for how current is switched through the phases.

End turn = the part of the winding outside the active slot region that bends around to the next slot. It adds wire length, resistance, heat, mass, and packaging cost without directly producing useful torque. Long, narrow "kimbap" motors reduce the relative penalty of end turns.

Housing = external mechanical structure. It holds the stator, protects the motor, supports bearings and assembly interfaces, and often provides a heat path to the environment.
# Outrunner vs Inrunner Packaging

Outrunner and inrunner are rotor-packaging terms, not separate motor-control families.

In an outrunner, the outer bell or casing rotates with the permanent magnets, while the stator windings stay fixed near the center. This gives a larger magnetic radius, so the layout tends toward high low-speed torque, larger diameter, shorter axial length, and higher rotor inertia.

In an inrunner, the central rotor and shaft rotate inside a stationary outer stator and housing. This tends toward a narrower, longer package with lower rotor inertia, higher maximum RPM, and a more direct heat path from stator to housing.

For drone propulsion, this packaging distinction matters because direct-drive multirotors usually want large, low-RPM propellers and therefore favor outrunners. Inrunners fit better when the load is a ducted fan, small high-speed propeller, geared propulsion path, or sealed/narrow package.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner BLDC Motor vs Inner Rotor BLDC Motor.md, mechanical-structure comparison; sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, structural differences and applicable scenarios.
# Textbook Drive-System View

The Electric Motor Control textbook frames the motor as one part of a larger electromechanical drive system: power source, power electronic converter, digital controller, voltage/current/position sensing, motor, and mechanical load. That matches the lecture convention that a robot actuator is not "just a motor"; the usable joint behavior depends on electronics, sensing, thermal limits, transmission, and load dynamics.

Figure: motor-drive-system block diagram tying source, converter, controller, sensors, motor, and load together. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 35, Fig. 1.32.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 3 and 35.
# Design Parameters from Slides

The slide appendix groups the hardware knobs as:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer/gearbox type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

This grouping is useful because it maps directly onto the lecture's three principles: geometry sets the electromagnetic capability, reducer choice sets the actuator-level torque/speed/inertia trade-off, and winding is mainly driver matching.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.
# Motor Driver

The motor driver is the power electronics/control unit that supplies timed voltage and current to the motor phases.

For a three-phase motor, the driver must:
- regulate voltage/current
- switch phase currents with the correct timing
- use rotor position or commutation logic to produce torque in the desired direction

Lecture 3B makes this more concrete: a DC bus is switched by a three-phase inverter, SVPWM turns d-q voltage commands into timed switch states, current sensing closes the feedback loop, and current control makes the q-axis current that creates torque. This is also why winding is a driver-matching problem. A low-turn, thick-wire or delta/parallel configuration is suitable for high-current drivers; a high-turn, thin-wire or wye/series configuration is suitable for high-voltage drivers.

BLDC/SPMSM-style motors need rotor-position information for commutation. This can come from Hall effect sensors, encoders, or sensorless estimation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 8 and 12-18.

pages.title: Motor Constant
pages.text:
# Motor Constant

Motor constant $Km$ measures how much motor torque is available per square-root copper loss. It is a motor-level metric: it combines torque production through the torque constant $Kt$ with resistive heating through winding resistance $Rs$.

The related actuator-level normalized metric is now split out to actuator-constant.
# Torque Constant $Kt$

$Kt$ is the torque constant: the proportionality between motor current and produced torque under a stated current convention.

$$
\tau = Kt I
$$

For a one-axis brushed-DC-style model, $I$ is just the motor current. For a sinusoidal SPMSM under field-oriented control, the clean ideal relation is:

$$
\taum \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

Under the q-axis peak-current convention, this means:

$$
Kt \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif
$$

where $p$ is pole-pair count, $\phif$ is permanent-magnet flux linkage, and $iq$ is q-axis current. The coefficient changes if the current is line RMS, phase peak, terminal current, or a different Park/Clarke convention. That is why this page treats $Kt$ as convention-dependent and points detailed conversion work to motor-constant-unit-conversions.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 46-74; linear SPMSM approximation caveat in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.
# Definition of $Km$

Copper loss under the matching resistance/current convention is:

$$
P{cu} = I^2Rs
$$

Combining this with $\tau = KtI$ gives:

$$
I = \sqrt{\frac{P{cu}}{Rs}}
$$

$$
\tau = Kt\sqrt{\frac{P{cu}}{Rs}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Km = \frac{\tau}{\sqrt{P{cu}}} = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}
$$

The common unit is $\mathrm{N\,m}/\sqrt{\mathrm{W}}$ when torque is in $\mathrm{N\,m}$ and copper loss is in watts.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, line 166.
# Physical Meaning

Read $Km$ as torque per square-root heat generation:
- Higher $Kt$ means the motor creates more torque for the same current.
- Lower $Rs$ means the same current causes less copper loss.
- Higher $Km$ means the motor can create more continuous torque for the same allowed copper heating.

If the cooling condition allows a continuous copper loss of $P{cu,max}$, then the rated-torque estimate is:

$$
\tau{rated} \approx Km\sqrt{P{cu,max}}
$$

This is why Lecture 4 describes motor constant as tied to rated torque: rated torque is the torque that can be held while the thermal condition remains steady. Peak torque belongs to saturation and is handled separately by actuator-constant.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.
# Why $Kt$ Alone Is Not Enough

$Kt$ answers "how much torque per ampere?" $Km$ answers "how much torque per heat?"

That distinction matters because winding choices can change $Kt$ and $Rs$ together. A high-turn winding raises $Kt$, but it also raises resistance. A low-turn winding lowers $Kt$, but it also lowers resistance. For fixed geometry and fixed copper volume, the ideal motor constant stays approximately invariant:

$$
\tau{rotor} \propto N
$$

$$
R \propto \frac{L}{A} \propto N^2
$$

$$
KM = \frac{\tau{rotor}}{\sqrt{I^2R}}
$$

So winding is mainly a driver matching decision: high-turn/wye/series versions suit higher voltage and lower current, while low-turn/delta/parallel versions suit lower voltage and higher current.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-168.
# Measuring $Kt$ from Back EMF

Lecture 3A answers a homework question from Lecture 2: if a torque sensor is unavailable, estimate torque constant from the back-EMF constant. The reason this works is energy consistency: in matching SI definitions, the same permanent-magnet flux linkage appears in torque production and generated voltage.

Spin the motor, measure an open-circuit line-to-line back-EMF waveform with an oscilloscope, and use the waveform frequency to get electrical angular speed. After applying the correct line/phase and transform convention, the measured voltage amplitude identifies the $\omegae\phif$ term. Then $\phif$ gives $Kt$.

For the unit-conversion details, see motor-constant-unit-conversions. The method is simple; the numerical coefficient is not, because phase vs line-line, peak vs RMS, mechanical vs electrical speed, and q-axis vs line current conventions all matter.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 46-74.
# Estimating $Kt$ Before FEM

Lecture 5 extends the same EMF/flux-linkage idea into a geometry-level estimate for surface-magnet motors:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g}BrNRl
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

This is upstream of FEM. It explains how geometry, magnet grade, air gap, and winding factor enter $Kt$ before saturation, leakage, fringing, cogging, and detailed magnetic-circuit effects are added. To turn this into $Km$, the designer still needs the matching winding resistance $Rs$. To turn it into actuator-level quality, the designer also needs saturation torque, mass, and output inertia; see actuator-constant.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 20 and 32; design motivation in sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 468-483.
# Gap Radius Scaling

Seok et al. write motor constant as:

$$
KM^2 = \frac{Kt^2}{R} = \frac{\tau^2}{I^2 R}
$$

This is the squared form of the same heat-normalized torque metric. Since $\tau = Kt I$ and copper loss is $P{cu} = I^2R$, the motor constant can be read as:

$$
KM = \frac{\tau}{\sqrt{P{cu}}} = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{R}}
$$

Squaring it makes the design meaning explicit: $KM^2$ measures how much torque squared the motor produces per watt of winding copper loss. A motor does not improve this metric merely by having a larger $Kt$; it must also avoid paying for that torque constant with proportionally larger winding resistance.

Under their electromagnetic-similarity model:

Figure: gap radius scaling for torque density, torque/inertia, and $\frac{Kt^2}{R}$. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Fig. 3.

$$
KM^2 \propto \frac{n l0 B^2 r{gap}^2 A}{\rho}
$$

where $n$ is the number of wires in cross section, $l0$ is axial length, $B$ is magnetic field strength, $r{gap}$ is motor gap radius, $A$ is wire cross-sectional area, and $\rho$ is wire resistivity.

The proportional form comes from combining conductor force, torque arm, and copper resistance. A current-carrying wire segment in the air-gap field produces a tangential force that scales like:

$$
F{wire} \sim B I l0
$$

That force acts at the gap radius, so one active wire segment contributes:

$$
\tau{wire} \sim B I l0 r{gap}
$$

With $n$ effective active wire segments:

$$
\tau \sim n B I l0 r{gap}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Kt = \frac{\tau}{I} \sim n B l0 r{gap}
$$

The winding resistance follows the usual conductor relation:

$$
R \sim \rho \frac{L}{A}
$$

If the active conductor length scales as $L \sim n l0$, then:

$$
R \sim \rho \frac{n l0}{A}
$$

Combining the two estimates gives:

$$
KM^2 = \frac{Kt^2}{R}
\sim
\frac{(n B l0 r{gap})^2}{\rho n l0 / A}
=
\frac{n l0 B^2 r{gap}^2 A}{\rho}
$$

The important reading of $n$ is "total active conductor cross-sections around the gap," not "more turns forced into one slot." If the wire gauge is fixed, each wire cross-section $A$ is fixed. If the radial slot/copper thickness and fill factor are held similar while the air-gap radius grows, the available copper cross-section around the circumference scales like:

$$
A{cu,total}
\sim
\eta{fill}(2\pi r{gap})t{slot}
$$

where $\eta{fill}$ is winding fill factor and $t{slot}$ is the radial thickness available for copper. Then:

$$
n \sim \frac{A{cu,total}}{A} \propto r{gap}
$$

So for fixed wire gauge, similar magnetic loading, and fixed axial length:

$$
KM^2 \propto n r{gap}^2 \propto r{gap}^3
$$

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 3, Fig. 3 and accompanying motor-constant paragraph.

The paper's empirical motor comparison found the same qualitative trend: torque-production efficiency rises strongly with gap radius, although real manufacturing details shift the exponent.
# What $Km$ Does Not Say

$Km$ is a compact motor metric, but it is not the whole actuator:
- It does not include core saturation or peak torque; use actuator-constant for the normalized metric that includes $\tau{sat}$.
- It does not include reducer ratio, gearbox friction, reflected inertia, or output bearing design.
- It does not replace thermal modeling; it only converts an allowed copper-loss budget into torque.
- It is only comparable when current, voltage, and resistance conventions match.
# Web References
- MIT Biomimetic Robotics Lab, "Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control in Fast Legged Locomotion," https://biomimetics.mit.edu/publications/56708036-8946-4bf4-af21-a6e66a04942f/, accessed 2026-05-29, found via Explore web search. External publication page for the Seok et al. paper.
- maxon Support, "Motor constants," https://support.maxongroup.com/hc/en-us/articles/360005873794-Motor-constants, accessed 2026-05-29, found via Explore web search. General external reference for how motor constants depend on geometry, winding, resistance, and magnetic loading.
# Related

pages.title: Motor Constant Unit Conversions
pages.text:
# Motor Constant Unit Conversions

Motor constant conversion is easy to get wrong because datasheets may mix speed constant $Kv$, back-EMF constant $Ke$, torque constant $Kt$, terminal voltage, phase voltage, peak current, RMS current, and controller-defined d-q current.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-235; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33.
# Energy Consistency Rule

With matching SI definitions, torque constant and back-EMF constant have the same numerical value.

For the one-axis brushed-DC-style convention:

$$
e = Ke\omegam
$$

$$
\tau = Kt i
$$

Power balance gives:

$$
ei = \tau\omegam
$$

Therefore:

$$
Ke = Kt
$$

when $Ke$ is in $\mathrm{V}/(\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s})$ and $Kt$ is in $\mathrm{N\,m}/\mathrm{A}$ under the same current and voltage convention.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-235; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 46-55.
# Convert $Kv$ to $Kt$

If $Kv$ is given in $\mathrm{rpm}/\mathrm{V}$ and represents the matching no-load speed constant:

$$
Kv
=
\frac{\omegam}{V}
\frac{60}{2\pi}
$$

Since $V \approx Ke\omegam$ at no load:

$$
Ke
=
\frac{60}{2\pi Kv}
$$

So the common conversion is:

$$
Kt
=
\frac{60}{2\pi Kv}
\approx
\frac{9.55}{Kv}
$$

This conversion is only safe when the datasheet's $Kv$ and $Kt$ use matching terminal/current conventions.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 403-421.
# Oscilloscope Back-EMF Method

Lecture 2 and Lecture 3A both ask how to estimate torque constant without a torque sensor. The answer is to measure back EMF: spin the motor, measure an open-circuit line-to-line voltage waveform, infer electrical speed from the waveform, then convert the back-EMF constant to the torque constant under a stated convention.

For a sinusoidal SPMSM measured with an oscilloscope, suppose the motor is spun at known mechanical speed $\omegam$ and the open-circuit line-to-line RMS voltage is $V{LL,rms}$. Then:

$$
K{e,LL,rms}
=
\frac{V{LL,rms}}{\omegam}
$$

For ideal sinusoidal back EMF:

$$
V{LL,rms}
=
\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}p\phif\omegam
$$

Thus:

$$
\phif
=
\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}
\frac{K{e,LL,rms}}{p}
$$

The torque constant per q-axis peak current is:

$$
K{t,q\text{-peak}}
=
\frac{3}{2}p\phif
=
\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}K{e,LL,rms}
$$

The torque constant per sinusoidal line RMS current is:

$$
K{t,line\text{-}rms}
=
\sqrt{3}K{e,LL,rms}
$$

This is why the homework asks for both the method and the formula: the measurement is simple, but the numerical constant depends on whether voltage is phase or line-line, peak or RMS, and whether current is q-axis peak or line RMS. Winding and terminal conventions are handled separately in motor-winding-configuration.

Source: measurement method in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; oscilloscope and line-to-line detail in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 29-74.
