pages.title: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
pages.text:
# Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control

Transmission transparency is the degree to which forces generated by the actuator are faithfully transmitted to the end effector, and external forces at the end effector are faithfully "felt" by the actuator.

It is closely related to backdrivability and low passive impedance.
# What Reduces Transparency

Transmission transparency is degraded by:
- reflected rotor inertia
- gear-train inertia
- friction
- damping
- backlash and compliance
- non-collocated force sensing with unmodeled modes

For legged-robot-actuation, these effects are especially damaging because ground contact is frequent, fast, and impulsive.
# Why It Matters

If the actuator/transmission has low enough mechanical impedance, motor torque can approximate end-effector force through Jacobian transpose force mapping:

$$\tau \approx J^T F{ext} + fc(q,\tau)$$

where $fc$ captures torque-dependent Coulomb friction. This makes proprioceptive force control possible: the robot estimates contact force from internal signals such as motor current and encoder position, without requiring a force sensor at the foot.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 8; sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II and force-control model discussion.
# Seok et al. 2012 Result

Seok et al. designed the MIT Cheetah front leg to maximize transparency through:
- large-radius, high-torque motors
- low reduction ratio, 1:5.8
- one-stage planetary gearing
- motors and gear trains located at the hip
- lightweight distal leg structure
- friction characterized and compensated experimentally

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, pages 3-4, MIT Cheetah leg design.

The paper then checks whether that hardware makes motor-current force estimation usable:

Figure: force-displacement calibration and impact force-control results comparing motor-current force estimates to force-sensor measurements. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 5, Fig. 8.

Fig. 8 is the paper's direct test of whether motor current can stand in for a foot force sensor. The force sensor is used as the reference measurement; the proposed control signal is the proprioceptive estimate built from motor current, encoder position, the leg Jacobian, and friction compensation.
- Fig. 8(a) plots vertical force against leg displacement while the leg is compressed and released. The loop is hysteresis: at the same displacement, the measured force differs depending on motion direction. In this experiment the loop is interpreted mainly as energy dissipated by transmission friction, and similar loop shapes appear at 7, 213, and 427 mm/s.
- Fig. 8(b) turns that hysteresis into a calibration. Compression and extension need separate linear fits because the friction direction reverses. The reported calibration slopes are about 0.886 in compression and 0.696 in extension, so a single scale factor would hide a direction-dependent force-estimation error.
- Fig. 8(c) compares force over time. The raw motor-current estimate misses the force-sensor curve, while the friction-compensated estimate tracks it much more closely. This is the practical point: current-based force estimation is useful only after the transmission friction is characterized.
- Fig. 8(d) repeats the comparison during impact-like virtual spring tests rather than slow calibration pushes. The compensated estimate follows the force sensor for 3406 N/m and roughly 5038 N/m stiffness settings, with mean absolute error divided by full-scale sensor force of 0.041 and 0.049.

> WARNING
> The paper is internally inconsistent on the higher virtual stiffness: the abstract and body text report 5038 N/m, while the Fig. 8 labels and caption report 5083 N/m. This page uses 5038 N/m when referencing the reported error sentence, and treats the plot label as 5083 N/m. Sources: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 1; sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 5, Fig. 8.

The figure therefore supports the narrow claim that a low-reduction, low-friction, low-impedance leg can estimate foot force well enough for fast proprioceptive force control. It does not mean motor current is automatically an accurate force sensor on any geared leg; the hardware transparency and friction compensation are part of the measurement chain.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 5, force-control experiment text and Fig. 8.
# Design Implication

The point of QDD-style hardware is not merely "use a small gear ratio." The point is to reduce passive impedance enough that the actuator behaves transparently for force control and survives impact. Low gear ratio is one practical route; a high-ratio transmission can compete only if it remains efficient, low-friction, backdrivable, and impact-tolerant.

Lecture 4 clarifies the terminology: use "backdrivability" mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior, not as a synonym for reflected inertia. Reflected inertia should be discussed separately as:

$$
J{out}=N^2Jr
$$

Both low friction and low reflected inertia improve transparency, but they are different design problems.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

pages.title: Understanding Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors
pages.text:
# Understanding Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors

Source: sources/web-clippings/Understanding the Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors.md - MECHTEX clipping, "Understanding the Hall Effect Sensors in BLDC Motors."
# Main Points from Source
- BLDC motors avoid brushes and mechanical commutators by using electronic commutation.
- Hall effect sensors detect rotor position and provide feedback to the controller.
- The controller uses rotor-position feedback to energize the correct stator winding at the correct time.
- In a typical three-phase BLDC motor, three Hall sensors are often arranged with a fixed angular relationship so the controller can infer rotor sector.
- Benefits:
- accurate rotor-position feedback
- reliable commutation
- speed control
- direction sensing
- durability and low maintenance
- Limitations:
- high-speed response limitations
- temperature sensitivity
- magnetic interference
- additional packaging/space/wiring complexity
# Related

pages.title: Winding Function and Winding Factor
pages.text:
# Winding Function and Winding Factor

The winding function is a compact way to turn a physical slot/conductor layout into a flux-linkage calculation. Lecture 5 uses it to move from conductor-level motional EMF to the phase torque result for an SPMSM.
# Conductor Density

The conductor density function $n(\phi)$ counts conductors per angular interval. If $d\phi$ is a small angular slice at angular position $\phi$, then:

$$
\text{conductors in } d\phi = n(\phi)d\phi
$$

In the idealized slot model, $n(\phi)$ can contain impulse-like positive and negative conductor groups. This is inconvenient for flux linkage, so the lecture introduces the winding function $N(\phi)$ as the more useful integrated representation.

Figure: conductor density and winding function for the simplified SPMSM cross-section. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.
# Flux-Linkage Overlap

For the simplified radial air-gap model, the phase flux linkage can be written as an overlap integral:

$$
\lambda(\theta)
=
R l \int0^{2\pi}
Br(\phi-\theta)N(\phi)\,d\phi
$$

Only the radial component $Br$ is kept because the simplified model is counting flux crossing the air-gap surface and linking axial conductors. As the rotor moves, the magnet-field waveform shifts relative to the winding function. Larger aligned overlap gives larger flux linkage.

Figure: radial magnet field waveform shifted against the winding function. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 29.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 281-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 29-30.
# Winding Factor

The winding factor $kw$ is the ratio between the useful fundamental EMF/torque contribution of a real winding and the ideal concentrated full-pitch winding. A winding factor of 1 means the conductor EMF contributions add without cancellation in the modeled fundamental. A lower winding factor means some of the conductor contributions partially cancel.

Lecture 5 first derives the ideal case, then inserts $kw$ into the final torque-constant formula:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 335-353 and 382-386; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 20 and 32.
# Pole Count Is Not a Free Torque Multiplier

The lecture warns against saying "more pole pairs directly increase torque constant." In the ideal formula, pole-pair count drops out when the total effective conductor count and linkage are fixed. More poles can still improve a real motor by allowing shorter end turns, better practical winding choices, thinner magnetic paths, and lower mass, but that is a packaging and winding-realization advantage rather than a direct independent multiplier in the ideal $Kt$ formula.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 388-424.

pages.title: Wye-Delta Parameter Conventions
pages.text:
# Wye-Delta Parameter Conventions

Wye-delta parameter conventions matter because a motor's internal winding topology and its external ABC-terminal equivalent are not the same question. For modeling and control, the practical requirement is to use one consistent equivalent-circuit convention.

For the basic winding/topology explanation, see motor-winding-configuration.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5.
# Terminal Equivalence

For ideal balanced linear passive branches, external ABC-terminal tests cannot uniquely distinguish wye from delta. A wye network and a delta network can have the same terminal behavior.

Practical answer:
- If only the three external ABC terminals are available and the motor behaves like an ideal balanced linear three-terminal network, no terminal-only test proves whether the internal winding is physically wye or delta.
- If the neutral point is externally accessible, the answer changes because wye exposes a node that delta does not.
- Real motors may show nonideal differences from saturation, parasitic capacitance, circulating harmonic currents, manufacturing asymmetry, or temperature gradients, but those effects do not give a clean general topology proof.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.
# Resistance Conversion

For equal wye phase resistance $Rs$, a line-to-line terminal measurement is:

$$
R{ab,\text{wye}} = 2Rs
$$

For equal delta branch resistance $R{\Delta}$, the line-to-line terminal resistance is:

$$
R{ab,\text{delta}}
=
R{\Delta}
\parallel
\left(R{\Delta} + R{\Delta}\right)
=
\frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

Equating the external terminal measurement:

$$
2Rs
=
\frac{2}{3}R{\Delta}
$$

So:

$$
Rs = \frac{R{\Delta}}{3}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.
# Inductance Conversion Caveat

The same equivalence habit can be used for terminal inductance under a simplified linear scalar model:

$$
2Ls
=
\frac{2}{3}L{\Delta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Ls = \frac{L{\Delta}}{3}
$$

Use this carefully. Real motor inductance includes mutual inductance, rotor-position dependence, saliency, saturation, measurement frequency, and controller transform convention. A datasheet value must be classified before it is inserted into spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.

Source: equivalent-circuit prompt in sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 243-253; Lecture 3A equivalent-circuit conclusion in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 51-59.
# Related
- motor-winding-configuration
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- motor-constant-unit-conversions
- motor-current-control

headingPath: Principle 1: Max Torque is Independent of Winding
chunks.text:
Principle 1: Max Torque is Independent of Winding

If stator and rotor dimensions are fixed, the way you wind the coils (thick wire with few turns vs thin wire with many turns) does not affect theoretical torque capability.

Figure: winding changes $N$ and current matching, but fixed stator-rotor geometry constrains the ideal torque capability. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Why: Magnetic field strength $H \propto NI$ (turns times current). If total copper cross-section is the same, the product $NI$ is the same regardless of winding configuration.

The listening notes usefully clarify the beginner intuition: turns and current trade off because the winding slot has finite space, more turns increase wire length/resistance, and thicker wire allows more current but consumes more slot area. The lecture's stronger claim is that this trade-off leaves the ideal torque capability unchanged when geometry/materials are fixed.

$$KM = \frac{\tau{rotor}}{\sqrt{I^2 R}}$$
- $\tau{rotor} \propto N$
- $R \propto \frac{L}{A} \propto N^2$ (for same total copper volume)
- Therefore $KM$ is independent of $N$ and $A$ (winding parameters)

To actually increase max torque: use stronger permanent magnets, or increase motor size (more volume, more copper).

headingPath: Principle 2: Winding is a Driver-Matching Problem
chunks.text:
Principle 2: Winding is a Driver-Matching Problem

Winding configuration is the place where a fixed motor design is matched to the available motor driver. With the same geometry, materials, and copper slot fill, changing turns or connection style mainly moves the motor along the voltage/current trade-off: low-turn, thick-wire choices suit high-current drivers, while high-turn, thin-wire choices suit high-voltage drivers.

Figure: high-turn/small-diameter vs low-turn/big-diameter winding as a voltage/current matching problem. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 20.

For the detailed series/parallel, wye/delta, $Kt$, $R$, $Kv$, and equivalent-circuit consequences, use motor-winding-configuration as the canonical page.

headingPath: Principle 3: Given Fixed Mass and Max Torque, Joint Inertia is Determined
chunks.text:
Principle 3: Given Fixed Mass and Max Torque, Joint Inertia is Determined

The most critical principle. Once you fix total mass and output max torque, the output inertia is theoretically fixed — regardless of motor shape.

Figure: pizza vs kimbap motor shapes can meet the same joint torque with different gear ratios under the ideal scaling argument. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22.

Pizza motor (flat, wide, large rgap):
- High motor torque → low gear ratio needed
- Higher rotor inertia, but low $N^2$ reflection

Kimbap motor (long, narrow, small rgap):
- Low motor torque → high gear ratio needed
- Lower rotor inertia, but high $N^2$ reflection

Result: same theoretical output inertia at same technology level.

But in Practice...
- Kimbap advantage: longer axial length → less end-turn waste → higher Km (better electrical efficiency)
- Pizza advantage: low gear ratio → simpler gearbox → less friction, less added inertia → better backdrivability

> High-reduction gearboxes add real-world penalties (mass, inertia, friction) that break the ideal scaling argument. Pizza motor + low reduction is the easier path to good control performance, not an absolute theoretical law.

The transcript explicitly warns that high reduction can still work if the gearbox/transmission is designed well enough. The lecture cites the idea that a poor 100:1 reducer may lose 40-50% to friction while a high-quality 100:1 design might approach 85-90% efficiency; low reduction mainly makes acceptable friction easier to achieve.

Lecture 4 sharpens the same point with backdrivability terminology. QDD is not fundamentally the statement "small gear ratio." It is the statement that friction and transmission uncertainty are small enough that torque can be inferred from motor current without a dedicated output torque sensor. Low reduction is the common practical route because it makes that condition easier to satisfy.

Seok et al.'s paper provides the clean scaling thought experiment behind this lecture claim: if motor radius is doubled and length is halved while motor mass and output torque are held fixed, motor torque doubles and required gear ratio halves. Rotor inertia increases $4\times$, but reflected rotor inertia through the gear ratio decreases by $\frac{1}{4}$, so ideal reflected inertia stays the same. The practical reason to prefer larger gap radius is that lower required gearing reduces real gearbox mass, inertia, and friction.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 250-264; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 317-345.

Appendix: Basic Design Parameters

The slide appendix organizes practical actuator design knobs into:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

The three design principles explain why these knobs do not independently improve every metric: geometry/materials set ideal torque capability, winding mostly matches the driver, and reducer choice trades torque/speed against reflected inertia and real gearbox losses.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

Design Intentions and Contributions

In robot hardware, a design intention is the reason a concrete pre-build decision exists. A contribution is the retrospective claim made after the robot works. The two are related, but confusing them makes early hardware design feel more certain than it really is.

Design intentions answer questions like:
- Where should this bolt, bearing, actuator, wire, or service access path go?
- How modular, manufacturable, and serviceable does the design need to be?
- What simple mechanism choices will keep control, modeling, and assembly tractable?

Contributions answer questions like:
- What can this robot now demonstrate?
- What performance region did the hardware open?
- What academic story can be defended with experiments?

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 37-59.

Why the Distinction Matters

Requirement selection and Pareto-front reasoning can make actuator design look like a clean optimization problem. Mechanism development is messier. Many high-impact decisions are local and practical: hole placement, connector access, part count, assembly order, machining feasibility, wiring route, and whether one actuator family can be reused.

Those decisions may not be publishable by themselves, but they decide whether the robot becomes a testable platform. The RAIPAL lecture argues that hardware engineers spend most of their time on these design intentions, while contribution language often appears only after the system works.

RAIPAL Example

RAIPAL's major design intentions were control transparency, engineering simplicity, and streamlined symmetry:
- use QDD-like, transparent actuation for easier control and lower sim-to-real gap
- avoid heavily coupled mechanisms that make the dynamics hard to model
- reuse actuator and electronics subcomponents where possible
- keep the platform compatible with high-bandwidth motor drivers and RL-oriented rollout workflows

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 5; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 63-75.

At the mechanism level, "make it light and not broken" was essential but not enough as an academic contribution. The speaker therefore describes adding at least one mechanism-level hook and biasing small design choices toward dynamic capability where design freedom allowed. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 6; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 81-103.

Contributions Often Emerge Late

RAIPAL's retrospective contribution framing shifted toward "human-level dynamic range." The lecture connects this to the lower-than-expected forearm gear ratio and successful dynamic demonstrations, but it presents that framing as something found after competent engineering, not as a fully visible claim at the blank-page stage. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 29-31; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 96-147.

That does not mean contribution planning is useless. It means early contribution pressure should not force a complicated mechanism before basic engineering quality exists. A robust platform with transparent actuation, accurate digital interface, and credible demonstrations gives more opportunities for contribution framing than an over-specialized mechanism that cannot be built or tested.

Practical Use

When starting a robot hardware design, write intentions in engineering language first:
- "The controller should see near-linear, low-coupling dynamics."
- "The same actuator package should repeat where possible."
- "The drawing should communicate which surfaces and fits are critical."
- "The URDF mass and rotor inertia should match the physical assembly closely enough for RL."

Then let contribution candidates remain hypotheses until iteration, production, and demonstrations reveal what the hardware can actually do.

Related
- actuator-requirement-selection
- robot-hardware-iteration
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- transmission-transparency
- actuator-design-to-current-control-map

Robot Hardware Iteration

Robot hardware iteration is the loop that turns a concept into a robot that can be manufactured, assembled, modeled, controlled, and demonstrated. It is not just "more CAD"; it includes tangible prototypes, analytic checks, specialized tests, drawings, URDF conversion, middleware, and demos.

The RAIPAL special lecture gives a concrete sequence:

1. compare concepts with rough but relevant metrics
2. make the concept tangible as quickly as possible
3. iterate CAD around mistakes and structural evidence
4. freeze a production-ready design only when time and money are worth spending
5. transfer manufacturing intentions into drawings
6. assemble prototypes and run basic tests
7. build the digital interface for simulation and policies
8. devise demonstrations that verify requirements

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 8-17.

Broad Strokes First

The first CAD draft should preserve the concept's important topology while becoming tangible quickly. The point is not a finished part; it is to make the idea interactable so errors in packaging, motion, task space, assembly, and intuition appear early.

The RAIPAL lecture calls this "broad strokes first": concept to crude real-life version, tangible as soon as possible, with multiple iterations intended from the start. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 9; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 127-137.

Catch Mistakes Quickly

The lecture's CAD iteration rule is blunt: first ideas are always terrible. The task is not to make perfect decisions from the beginning, but to expose mistakes quickly and convert them into improvements.

RAIPAL's structural iteration shows the pattern. The slide compares v0-1 with minimum safety factor 1.8 and roughly 15 mm maximum deformation at peak torque against v1-1 with minimum safety factor 3.2 and roughly 1 mm maximum deformation. The transcript explains that these were not exact full-assembly truth claims; the simulations were simplified enough to answer the design question quickly. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 10-11; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 139-177; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 182-193.

Use Three Tool Types

The retrospective groups useful iteration tools into three categories:

Figure: tangible, analytic, and specialized tools used during RAIPAL iteration. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28.
- Tangible: miniatures, prototypes, and physical manipulation expose geometry and assembly failures.
- Analytic: simple FEA and mass/inertia estimates catch structural or performance problems before spending production time.
- Specialized: dedicated variations and test fixtures answer narrow questions such as fits, bearings, backlash, thermal behavior, or actuator characterization.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 88-95.

Drawings Are Part of Design

Production drawings transfer design intentions into a common manufacturing language. They are not just administrative output. A drawing decides which dimensions, fits, tolerances, surfaces, and assembly relations must be preserved by the manufacturer.

The RAIPAL transcript notes that the act of making drawings can force another design iteration when the manufacturer cannot make the part or when the drawing cannot express the intended precision cleanly. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 14; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 232-240.

Digital Interface Is Hardware Completion

For RL-oriented robot hardware, the iteration loop is incomplete until the robot can enter simulation and receive policies back through real middleware. RAIPAL's digital-interface work included URDF conversion, mass agreement around 5 g, rotor inertia calculation, real-time communication, and crossed-4-bar calculation through the middleware layer.

This connects directly to motor-operating-region and transmission-transparency: the hardware may be mechanically good, but a bad digital model or delayed middleware can create a sim-to-real gap large enough to hide that quality from the controller. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 16; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 9-30.

headingPath: Plan Around Real Time, Not Ideal Time
chunks.text:
Plan Around Real Time, Not Ideal Time

RAIPAL's development statistics show how expensive integration is:

Figure: RAIPAL time allocation by phase: concept, draft, design iteration, production, digital interface, and testing. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 25.

Design iteration was the largest named category at 30.6%, but production, digital interface, and testing together took more than half of the non-delay work. A separate chart shows delay at 54.5% of the calendar. The planning lesson is conservative: even a main hardware project may receive only part of a week after demos, unrelated lab work, proposal work, renovation, health, and coordination bottlenecks. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 25-26; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 42-69; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 81-84.

Actuator Constant

Actuator constant $C$ is the normalized actuator-level metric for the coupled trade-off among mass, rated torque, peak torque, and joint/output inertia. It uses motor constant $Km$ as one ingredient, but it is not the same concept.

Use this page for the $C$ metric. Use motor-constant for $Kt$, copper loss, and $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}$.

Corrected Definition

Lecture 4 supersedes the earlier normalized metric and gives the current relationship as:

$$
C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}
$$

where:
- $Km$ = motor constant, roughly torque per square-root copper heat
- $\tau{sat}$ = torque limit from stator core magnetic saturation
- $M$ = mass of copper in the electromagnetic scaling relation
- $I$ = joint/output inertia

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117.

The slide uses copper mass because the formula is about the electromagnetic scaling relation. The transcript notes that total motor mass can be a rough proxy because copper, iron, rotor, and stator masses tend to scale together within a similar motor family.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.

> WARNING
> Lecture 4 says the normalized formula from Lectures 1-2 was corrected again. Treat the Lecture 4 relation $C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}$ as the current canonical version in this wiki. Earlier notes using $C = \frac{Km\sqrt{\tau{sat}}}{\sqrt{M}\sqrt{I}}$ or formulas based only on power and inertia are superseded.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6; earlier version in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, line 284.

How to Read $C$

Higher $C$ means a better actuator technology level for the same coupled requirements. Similar products from the same manufacturer or design family should sit near the same $C$ because they share material choices, motor topology, magnetic loading, manufacturing quality, and packaging assumptions.

The practical reading is:
- $Km$ raises rated-torque capability for a given heat budget.
- $\tau{sat}$ raises peak torque before core saturation.
- Lower $M$ and lower output inertia $I$ are better, but they trade against the torque terms.
- If $C$ is fixed, increasing one desirable metric usually pushes another one in the wrong direction.

This is why $C$ is connected to the Pareto front: it describes the technology level of the front, while the robot task chooses where on that front the actuator should sit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117; earlier Pareto wording in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 280-302.

Relation to Off-the-Shelf Selection

For a custom motor, $Km$, $\tau{sat}$, $M$, and $I$ are design variables coupled by the formula above.

For an off-the-shelf frameless motor, Lecture 4 says $Km$ and $\tau{sat}$ are usually already coupled inside the motor family. In practice, the designer then chooses among torque, mass, and inertia rather than freely setting all four primary requirements.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.

headingPath: Why Scaling Alone Does Not Improve $C$
chunks.text:
Why Scaling Alone Does Not Improve $C$

Changing only size, aspect ratio, or gear ratio moves the actuator around a trade-off surface; it does not by itself create a fundamentally better technology level. Lecture 1 framed this as the reason same-$C$ actuators can occupy the same Pareto front: if torque is increased under fixed technology, mass or inertia usually moves with it.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 290-302.

This is also the boundary between two different design questions:
- Requirement selection: choose the correct point on the current Pareto front for the robot.
- Actuator optimal design: move the front outward by improving materials, topology, cooling, magnetic design, reducer quality, manufacturing, or assembly.

How to Improve $C$

Since dimensional scaling alone does not improve $C$, the improvement has to come from fundamental design changes.

Better Materials
- Magnets: use higher remanent flux density, such as moving from lower-grade to higher-grade NdFeB magnets.
- Core: use soft-magnetic materials with higher saturation flux density.

Better Topology

Changing topology can improve how effectively magnetic flux is converted into torque. The lecture examples include moving across IPMSM, SPMSM, and vernier/PMVM-style ideas, but a higher ideal metric can introduce other losses or constraints. For example, vernier machines can look attractive by an ideal torque metric while suffering high electrical-frequency iron loss in high-output applications.

Copper vs Iron Allocation

Copper and iron pull the design in different directions:
- More copper tends to reduce resistance and improve efficiency for long-duration operation.
- More iron can raise the magnetic saturation limit and improve peak torque.
- Iron saturation is nonlinear, so accurate design usually needs finite-element analysis rather than only a hand scaling argument.

The lecture frames this as operating-region optimization:
- An "athlete" design allocates more iron if high peak torque matters and efficiency is secondary.
- A "marathon" design allocates more copper if lower-torque long-duration operation matters.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 304-330.

Actuator Performance Metrics

Lecture 4 refines the actuator primary requirements to four coupled quantities:
- mass
- rated torque
- joint/output inertia
- peak or saturation torque

For off-the-shelf frameless motor selection, the lecture says this often reduces in practice to torque, mass, and inertia because motor constant and saturation torque are coupled inside a motor family. Power and max speed are not valid standalone primary metrics because they can be shifted by winding, driver voltage/current, and gear ratio.

For the motor-physics intuition behind the winding part of this claim, see back-emf-and-winding-turns: changing turns changes both torque per ampere and generated voltage per speed, so rpm and power numbers are partly configuration outcomes.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 5-6.

1. Mass

Total weight of the actuator assembly.

Scaling law:
- Volume $\propto L^3$, magnetic shear area $\propto L^2$, torque arm $\propto L$
- $\tau \propto$ shear stress $\times$ area $\times r \propto L^2 \times L = L^3$? No — torque scales roughly as $L^2$ relative to mass scaling of $L^3$
- $10\times$ size → $1000\times$ mass, roughly $100\times$ torque → torque/mass ratio worsens with scale

Simply making a motor bigger gives diminishing returns.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-102.

2. Rated Torque and Peak Torque

Lecture 4 separates two torque limits:
- Rated torque: continuous or steady-state torque, limited mainly by heat dissipation under a defined thermal condition.
- Peak / saturation torque: short-duration torque limited by magnetic saturation when heating is ignored or treated as transient.

Rated torque is tied to copper heat:
- Copper heat loss: $P{cu} = I^2R$
- Steady-state thermal equilibrium: $P{cu} = hA\Delta T$
- Heat generation $\propto L^3$ (volume), heat dissipation $\propto L^2$ (surface area)
- Larger motors are harder to cool — max torque is thermally constrained

Peak torque is tied to the nonlinear magnetic limit. Beyond saturation, additional current no longer produces proportional torque.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 104-114; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 107-109.

3. Joint Inertia (Output Inertia)

The rotational inertia felt at the output shaft.

$$J{out} = N^2 \times Jm + J{gear}$$

Where:
- $J{out}$ = output-side or joint-side inertia of the actuator's internal rotating parts.
- $N$ = reduction ratio: the motor turns $N$ times for one output/joint turn.
- $Jm$ = motor rotor inertia about the motor shaft, not total motor mass.
- $J{gear}$ = gearbox/reducer rotating inertia as seen at the output side.
- Gear ratio $10\times$ -> reflected inertia $100\times$ increase
- Power is maintained, torque increases, but backdrivability degrades severely
- This is the fundamental reason QDD (Quasi-Direct Drive) avoids high gear ratios

The formula is usually about the actuator's internal inertia, not the external robot link/load inertia. A larger system model may add the link/load inertia separately.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 116-138.

Reference Frame Clarification

"Output inertia," "joint inertia," and "total reflected rotor inertia" are often used inconsistently. In this lecture's main design argument, the important quantity is the inertia felt at the joint/output, especially motor rotor inertia reflected through the reducer:

$$J{rotor \rightarrow out} \approx N^2 Jm$$

The inverse statement is also true when looking from the motor side: a load inertia behind a reducer appears smaller to the motor by roughly $\frac{1}{N^2}$. These are not contradictory; they use opposite reference frames.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 118-124.

Key Insight

> Using power or max angular velocity as performance metrics is a misconception. Electric motors (unlike combustion engines) have no theoretical power limit — you can always adjust the operating region via gear ratio. The three metrics above are what actually matter.

Lecture 4 sharpens this point: velocity/power is a secondary requirement for actuator selection, not a primary physical trade-off like mass, rated torque, peak torque, and inertia. It still must be checked against winding and driver voltage/current limits, especially when using off-the-shelf motors.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 5.

headingPath: Why "Bigger Motor + Bigger Gearbox" Is Not Free
chunks.text:
Why "Bigger Motor + Bigger Gearbox" Is Not Free

Two scaling traps explain why actuator design becomes a Pareto trade-off instead of a simple "make it stronger" problem.

headingPath: 1. Motor Scale: Mass Grows Very Fast
chunks.text:
1. Motor Scale: Mass Grows Very Fast

If every linear dimension of a motor scales by $L$, its volume and mass scale roughly as:

$$Mass \propto Volume \propto L^3$$

So a motor that is $10\times$ larger in every direction has roughly:

$$10^3 = 1000\times$$

more mass.

Many useful physical limits, such as magnetic shear area or heat-dissipation area, scale more like area:

$$Area \propto L^2$$

So a $10\times$ length scale gives only about:

$$10^2 = 100\times$$

more area.

The practical intuition: a bigger motor becomes heavy very quickly, while some of the useful electromagnetic or thermal capability does not grow as cheaply. Scaling up is therefore a trade-off, not a free improvement.

> QUESTION
> The exact torque scaling depends on which dimensions are scaled and which constraints are held fixed. The safe takeaway from Lecture 1 is not "torque is always exactly $L^2$," but "mass grows aggressively with size, so motor scaling has a real mass penalty."
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-102.

headingPath: 2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically
chunks.text:
2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically

A reducer with ratio $N$ means the motor spins $N$ times for one output/joint rotation.

For a $10:1$ reduction:
- motor rotates 10 turns
- joint rotates 1 turn
- output torque is about $10\times$ motor torque

But reflected motor inertia at the joint is:

$$J{rotor \rightarrow out} \approx N^2Jm$$

This comes from kinetic energy:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2$$

If the joint speed is $\omega$, the motor rotor speed is $N\omega$:

$$Em = \frac{1}{2}Jm(N\omega)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(N^2Jm)\omega^2$$

From the joint's point of view, the motor rotor behaves like it has $N^2Jm$ inertia.

Examples:
- $10:1$ reduction → $10\times$ torque but $100\times$ reflected rotor inertia
- $100:1$ reduction → $100\times$ torque but $10{,}000\times$ reflected rotor inertia

This is why high reduction can make a legged robot feel heavy, non-backdrivable, inefficient during impact, and easier to damage. See legged-robot-actuation and transmission-transparency.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 118-138.

The slide deck makes this concrete with a power-density trap:
- Small motor: mass = 38g, power = 1166W
- HOUND-style stator+rotor example: mass = 1042g ($27\times$), power = 2700W ($2.3\times$)

headingPath: 2. Gear Ratio: Reflected Inertia Grows Quadratically
chunks.text:
Figure: power density can make the small high-speed motor look better, but the joint torque/inertia requirement changes the conclusion. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 13.

If power were the metric, the small motor would look categorically better. The lecture's point is that once the joint torque requirement is imposed, the small motor needs much higher reduction, and the $N^2$ reflected inertia can dominate.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 12-13.

Seok et al. make the same argument more formally: EM motors can achieve high power density at high speed and relatively low torque, but high-force robot joints need delivered torque. If higher voltage/speed requires higher gear reduction, actuator impedance rises and delivered force control worsens. The paper therefore recommends torque density over power density as the clearer robotics actuator metric.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, pages 1-3.

Thought Experiment

Two motors, same max torque target:
- Motor 1: mass = 8 ($2\times2\times2$ cube), direct drive → $J{out} = mr^2 = 8 \times 4 = 32$
- Motor 2: mass = 1, needs 8:1 gear ratio → $J{out} = 1 \times 8^2 = 64$

Motor 1 is heavier but has half the output inertia. Bigger motor + lower gear ratio wins on inertia.

Note: this is an illustrative calculation using the $N^2$ reflected-inertia relation, not a copied motor spec.

Source: related lecture example in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22.

Application Dependence

The same Pareto front can imply different choices:
- Legged robots: reflected inertia is critical because impacts dominate, so heavier high-torque actuators can be justified to keep joint inertia low.
- Manipulators: static load, precision, and force control can dominate more than repeated impact survival, so higher reduction and lower motor mass may remain acceptable in many designs.

Motor Constant

Motor constant $Km$ measures how much motor torque is available per square-root copper loss. It is a motor-level metric: it combines torque production through the torque constant $Kt$ with resistive heating through winding resistance $Rs$.

The related actuator-level normalized metric is now split out to actuator-constant.

Torque Constant $Kt$

$Kt$ is the torque constant: the proportionality between motor current and produced torque under a stated current convention.

$$
\tau = Kt I
$$

For a one-axis brushed-DC-style model, $I$ is just the motor current. For a sinusoidal SPMSM under field-oriented control, the clean ideal relation is:

$$
\taum \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

Under the q-axis peak-current convention, this means:

$$
Kt \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif
$$

where $p$ is pole-pair count, $\phif$ is permanent-magnet flux linkage, and $iq$ is q-axis current. The coefficient changes if the current is line RMS, phase peak, terminal current, or a different Park/Clarke convention. That is why this page treats $Kt$ as convention-dependent and points detailed conversion work to motor-constant-unit-conversions.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 46-74; linear SPMSM approximation caveat in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

Definition of $Km$

Copper loss under the matching resistance/current convention is:

$$
P{cu} = I^2Rs
$$

Combining this with $\tau = KtI$ gives:

$$
I = \sqrt{\frac{P{cu}}{Rs}}
$$

$$
\tau = Kt\sqrt{\frac{P{cu}}{Rs}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Km = \frac{\tau}{\sqrt{P{cu}}} = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}
$$

The common unit is $\mathrm{N\,m}/\sqrt{\mathrm{W}}$ when torque is in $\mathrm{N\,m}$ and copper loss is in watts.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, line 166.

Physical Meaning

Read $Km$ as torque per square-root heat generation:
- Higher $Kt$ means the motor creates more torque for the same current.
- Lower $Rs$ means the same current causes less copper loss.
- Higher $Km$ means the motor can create more continuous torque for the same allowed copper heating.

If the cooling condition allows a continuous copper loss of $P{cu,max}$, then the rated-torque estimate is:

$$
\tau{rated} \approx Km\sqrt{P{cu,max}}
$$

This is why Lecture 4 describes motor constant as tied to rated torque: rated torque is the torque that can be held while the thermal condition remains steady. Peak torque belongs to saturation and is handled separately by actuator-constant.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.

Why $Kt$ Alone Is Not Enough

$Kt$ answers "how much torque per ampere?" $Km$ answers "how much torque per heat?"

That distinction matters because winding choices can change $Kt$ and $Rs$ together. A high-turn winding raises $Kt$, but it also raises resistance. A low-turn winding lowers $Kt$, but it also lowers resistance. For fixed geometry and fixed copper volume, the ideal motor constant stays approximately invariant:

$$
\tau{rotor} \propto N
$$

$$
R \propto \frac{L}{A} \propto N^2
$$

$$
KM = \frac{\tau{rotor}}{\sqrt{I^2R}}
$$

So winding is mainly a driver matching decision: high-turn/wye/series versions suit higher voltage and lower current, while low-turn/delta/parallel versions suit lower voltage and higher current.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-168.

Measuring $Kt$ from Back EMF

Lecture 3A answers a homework question from Lecture 2: if a torque sensor is unavailable, estimate torque constant from the back-EMF constant. The reason this works is energy consistency: in matching SI definitions, the same permanent-magnet flux linkage appears in torque production and generated voltage.

Spin the motor, measure an open-circuit line-to-line back-EMF waveform with an oscilloscope, and use the waveform frequency to get electrical angular speed. After applying the correct line/phase and transform convention, the measured voltage amplitude identifies the $\omegae\phif$ term. Then $\phif$ gives $Kt$.

For the unit-conversion details, see motor-constant-unit-conversions. The method is simple; the numerical coefficient is not, because phase vs line-line, peak vs RMS, mechanical vs electrical speed, and q-axis vs line current conventions all matter.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 46-74.

Estimating $Kt$ Before FEM

Lecture 5 extends the same EMF/flux-linkage idea into a geometry-level estimate for surface-magnet motors:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g}BrNRl
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

This is upstream of FEM. It explains how geometry, magnet grade, air gap, and winding factor enter $Kt$ before saturation, leakage, fringing, cogging, and detailed magnetic-circuit effects are added. To turn this into $Km$, the designer still needs the matching winding resistance $Rs$. To turn it into actuator-level quality, the designer also needs saturation torque, mass, and output inertia; see actuator-constant.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 20 and 32; design motivation in sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 468-483.

Gap Radius Scaling

Seok et al. write motor constant as:

$$
KM^2 = \frac{Kt^2}{R} = \frac{\tau^2}{I^2 R}
$$

This is the squared form of the same heat-normalized torque metric. Since $\tau = Kt I$ and copper loss is $P{cu} = I^2R$, the motor constant can be read as:

$$
KM = \frac{\tau}{\sqrt{P{cu}}} = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{R}}
$$

Squaring it makes the design meaning explicit: $KM^2$ measures how much torque squared the motor produces per watt of winding copper loss. A motor does not improve this metric merely by having a larger $Kt$; it must also avoid paying for that torque constant with proportionally larger winding resistance.

Under their electromagnetic-similarity model:

Figure: gap radius scaling for torque density, torque/inertia, and $\frac{Kt^2}{R}$. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Fig. 3.

$$
KM^2 \propto \frac{n l0 B^2 r{gap}^2 A}{\rho}
$$

where $n$ is the number of wires in cross section, $l0$ is axial length, $B$ is magnetic field strength, $r{gap}$ is motor gap radius, $A$ is wire cross-sectional area, and $\rho$ is wire resistivity.

The proportional form comes from combining conductor force, torque arm, and copper resistance. A current-carrying wire segment in the air-gap field produces a tangential force that scales like:

$$
F{wire} \sim B I l0
$$

That force acts at the gap radius, so one active wire segment contributes:

$$
\tau{wire} \sim B I l0 r{gap}
$$

With $n$ effective active wire segments:

$$
\tau \sim n B I l0 r{gap}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Kt = \frac{\tau}{I} \sim n B l0 r{gap}
$$

The winding resistance follows the usual conductor relation:

$$
R \sim \rho \frac{L}{A}
$$

If the active conductor length scales as $L \sim n l0$, then:

$$
R \sim \rho \frac{n l0}{A}
$$

Combining the two estimates gives:

$$
KM^2 = \frac{Kt^2}{R}
\sim
\frac{(n B l0 r{gap})^2}{\rho n l0 / A}
=
\frac{n l0 B^2 r{gap}^2 A}{\rho}
$$

The important reading of $n$ is "total active conductor cross-sections around the gap," not "more turns forced into one slot." If the wire gauge is fixed, each wire cross-section $A$ is fixed. If the radial slot/copper thickness and fill factor are held similar while the air-gap radius grows, the available copper cross-section around the circumference scales like:

$$
A{cu,total}
\sim
\eta{fill}(2\pi r{gap})t{slot}
$$

where $\eta{fill}$ is winding fill factor and $t{slot}$ is the radial thickness available for copper. Then:

$$
n \sim \frac{A{cu,total}}{A} \propto r{gap}
$$

So for fixed wire gauge, similar magnetic loading, and fixed axial length:

$$
KM^2 \propto n r{gap}^2 \propto r{gap}^3
$$

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 3, Fig. 3 and accompanying motor-constant paragraph.

The paper's empirical motor comparison found the same qualitative trend: torque-production efficiency rises strongly with gap radius, although real manufacturing details shift the exponent.

What $Km$ Does Not Say

$Km$ is a compact motor metric, but it is not the whole actuator:
- It does not include core saturation or peak torque; use actuator-constant for the normalized metric that includes $\tau{sat}$.
- It does not include reducer ratio, gearbox friction, reflected inertia, or output bearing design.
- It does not replace thermal modeling; it only converts an allowed copper-loss budget into torque.
- It is only comparable when current, voltage, and resistance conventions match.

Web References
- MIT Biomimetic Robotics Lab, "Actuator Design for High Force Proprioceptive Control in Fast Legged Locomotion," https://biomimetics.mit.edu/publications/56708036-8946-4bf4-af21-a6e66a04942f/, accessed 2026-05-29, found via Explore web search. External publication page for the Seok et al. paper.
- maxon Support, "Motor constants," https://support.maxongroup.com/hc/en-us/articles/360005873794-Motor-constants, accessed 2026-05-29, found via Explore web search. General external reference for how motor constants depend on geometry, winding, resistance, and magnetic loading.

Definition

Pareto optimal = a state where improving one performance metric necessarily requires sacrificing another.

Pareto front = the set of all Pareto-optimal solutions — the physical limit boundary.

$$
\mathcal{P}=\left\{x^\star \in \mathcal{X}\;\middle|\;\nexists x \in \mathcal{X} :\Bigl(\forall i,\; fi(x) \ge fi(x^\star)\Bigr)\land\Bigl(\exists j,\; fj(x) > fj(x^\star)\Bigr)\right\}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 51-61; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 8; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-61.

Application to Actuator Design
- There is no universally "best" actuator — like MTB vs road bike, it depends on purpose
- The primary actuator metrics include mass, rated torque, peak torque, and joint inertia
- Design goal: reach the Pareto front by eliminating dominated designs, then choose the right point for your application
- Two actuators with the same actuator constant $C$ sit on the same Pareto front

The Lecture 1 slides use two bicycle comparisons to separate two ideas: dominated designs have a clear "goodness" gap, while two specialized designs on the same front (e.g., MTB vs road bike) require an application-dependent choice rather than a universal ranking.

Figure: dominated vs application-dependent design points on a Pareto front. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 8.

Lecture 4 turns this into a workflow distinction: requirement selection chooses where the actuator should sit on the current Pareto front, while actuator optimal design tries to push the front outward through better motor topology, materials, reducer design, manufacturing, or assembly quality. Mixing those two steps leads to optimizing the wrong actuator for the wrong robot.

Why It Matters

Naive thinking: "just make it bigger/stronger." Reality: scaling up a motor increases mass faster than torque ($L^3$ vs $L^2$), and adding gear ratio increases inertia quadratically ($N^2$). Every improvement has a cost. Understanding the Pareto front prevents chasing impossible designs.

Actuator Requirement Selection

Actuator requirement selection means choosing what the actuator must be good at before optimizing the hardware. Lecture 4 separates this from actuator optimal design itself.

The core rule:

Choose the target point on the current Pareto front first; push the Pareto front later.

Requirement Selection vs Hardware Improvement

Requirement selection answers:
- What robot are we building?
- What payload, speed, and task envelope matter?
- Where should this actuator sit among the trade-offs?

Hardware improvement answers:
- Can better materials improve the front?
- Can better reducer manufacturing improve friction/backlash?
- Can better motor topology raise the achievable performance?

These are different problems. The lecture's analogy is a bicycle: first decide mountain bike vs road bike, then optimize bearings, frame material, and components.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-61.

Design Intentions Before Contributions

The RAIPAL mechanism lecture adds a practical language layer: design intentions are the pre-build choices that make the robot possible, while contributions are the retrospective performance claims made after the robot exists.

This matters because many requirement-selection decisions are not glamorous metrics. Modularity, manufacturability, serviceability, simple dynamics, repeated subcomponents, and clear assembly paths may decide whether the robot becomes controllable hardware at all. The lecture explicitly warns that intentions and contributions are not mutually exclusive; a good hardware engineer circulates between them while samples are limited and expensive.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 37-59.

Primary Requirements

Lecture 4 identifies four primary actuator requirements:

Figure: Lecture 4 primary actuator requirements and corrected normalized relationship. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.
- Mass
- Rated torque
- Joint/output inertia
- Peak or saturation torque

These are primary because they are fundamentally coupled by the motor/actuator physics. At the same design level, improving one usually worsens another.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 5-6.

Secondary Requirements

The lecture lists secondary requirements such as:
- backlash
- volume
- stiffness
- velocity / power

They are still important, but they are not coupled in the same clean fundamental relationship as the primary requirements. They are often limited by:
- manufacturing precision
- assembly tolerances
- reducer tooth/profile design
- driver voltage/current capability
- cost and available components

Backlash is a useful example. It matters a lot for robot positioning, but an ideal zero-tolerance manufacturing world could reduce it without violating a Maxwell-equation-style actuator metric. In practice, cost, thermal expansion, wear, and manufacturing quality set the limit.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 81-89; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-315.

Corrected Actuator Metric

Lecture 4 supersedes the earlier lecture notes and gives the current normalized actuator constant relationship as:

$$
C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.

where:
- $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}$ is the motor constant
- $\tau{sat}$ is the torque limit from core saturation
- $M$ is copper mass
- $I$ is joint/output inertia

This formula is why the lecture says the four primary requirements are coupled.

For off-the-shelf frameless motor selection, $Km$ and $\tau{sat}$ are usually already coupled inside a motor family. In that practical case, the designer mainly chooses among torque, mass, and inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 95-117.

Iteration

Requirement selection is not a one-pass calculation. If a robot becomes too heavy or cannot meet the task:
- step back in the distal-to-proximal chain
- lower payload or total-mass goals
- choose better motors/materials/reducer topology
- improve manufacturing quality if the issue is friction/backlash rather than the primary motor trade-off

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.

Torque-Mass-Inertia Selection

Torque-mass-inertia (T-M-I) selection is the Lecture 4 workflow for turning robot-level requirements into actuator-level specs.

The lecture's practical ordering is:

1. torque
2. mass
3. inertia

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155.

Distal-to-Proximal Workflow

For a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator, Lecture 4 proposes choosing actuator specs from the end effector back toward the base.

Figure: example distal-to-proximal requirement procedure for a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.

The rough sequence is:

1. define kinematic parameters such as link lengths and joint ranges
2. set rated and peak payload
3. define wrist actuator T-M-I
4. define forearm mass
5. define elbow actuator T-M-I
6. define upper-arm mass
7. define shoulder actuator T-M-I

After each distal component is chosen, its mass becomes part of the load for the next proximal actuator.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 7.

RAIPAL Concept Comparison Example

The RAIPAL mechanism lecture applies the same T-M-I logic before detailed CAD. Its FAST / SIMPLE / LINKED concept table compares each topology using total mass, supporting mass per joint, supporting center-of-mass distance, worst-case holding torque, projected inertia, and worst-case payload.

Figure: RAIPAL compares three early arm concepts using mass, inertia, holding torque, and payload estimates before committing to detailed mechanism CAD. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8.

The slide reports FAST at 8.5 kg with projected inertia <0.30,0.48,0.24> kgm^2 and 7.8 kg worst-case payload, SIMPLE at 7.7 kg with <0.42,0.52,0.10> kgm^2 and 6.9 kg payload, and LINKED at 7.9 kg with <0.33,0.43,0.11> kgm^2 and 7.2 kg payload. The transcript frames this as a rough design-space reduction step: external arm case studies rarely expose exact mass and inertia data, so the comparison used approximate same-assumption modeling rather than exact reverse engineering.

Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 113-126; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 197-217.

Torque From Payload

Torque requirements come from payload and child-link mass. The lecture uses the standard Jacobian transpose force mapping relation:

$$
\boldsymbol\tau = J^T\mathbf F
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10.

Figure: rated/peak actuator torque is set from payload, child-link mass, and the configurations considered. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10.

The subtle question is which configuration set to design for:
- Fixed-base high-precision manipulator: often design for worst configuration inside the workspace.
- Mobile anthropomorphic manipulator: can design around favorable configurations because the body/base can move to make the task easier.

Rated torque is the torque that can be sustained thermally. Peak torque is the short-duration transient torque. The lecture gives a rough heuristic that peak torque is often about $300-400\%$ of rated torque, but the actual ratio depends on motor saturation, thermal path, and vendor definition.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10; rated/peak definitions in sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 107-109.

Mass Selection

For the static QDD manipulator case, Lecture 4 assumes off-the-shelf frameless motors and static rather than highly dynamic behavior.

The practical rules are:
- lighter motors are better once torque is satisfied
- maximize reduction ratio within the chosen QDD/friction limit
- check joint velocity under the given battery voltage
- account for full actuator mass, not only motor mass

The reason full actuator mass matters is that each proximal joint must lift all distal motors, reducers, housings, links, and payload.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 11.

headingPath: Reducer Mass Is Mostly Output-Torque Driven
chunks.text:
Reducer Mass Is Mostly Output-Torque Driven

The lecture argues that reducer mass is dominated by the output stage that must withstand peak joint torque. A $100:1$ reducer and a $10:1$ reducer can be similar in mass if both must output the same peak torque, because the high-output-torque stage dominates the structure.

This lets a first-pass workflow separate:
- motor mass selection from off-the-shelf frameless motor choice
- reducer mass estimation from peak output torque

It is an approximation, but it keeps the early design problem tractable.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-229; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 11.

Inertia and Reduction Ratio

Once the frameless motor is chosen, rotor inertia $Jr$ is fixed. The output-reflected inertia is:

$$
J{out} = N^2Jr
$$

where $N$ is reduction ratio.

For static applications, inertia is often secondary, but ignoring it completely produces fragile or sluggish robots. Excessive output inertia makes impacts harder to absorb and can break gear teeth instead of letting the joint move.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 12.

Dynamic 1-DOF Rule

For a 1-DOF system with fixed link inertia $J{link}$ and rotor inertia $Jr$, output acceleration is maximized when:

Figure: in a 1-DOF system, maximum acceleration occurs when reflected rotor inertia equals link inertia. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 13.

$$
N^2Jr = J{link}
$$

This gives a critical reduction ratio:

$$
N{crit} = \sqrt{\frac{J{link}}{Jr}}
$$

Below this ratio, both static performance and dynamic acceleration can be worse. Above this ratio, static torque improves, but dynamic acceleration starts to trade off.

For multi-DOF manipulators, the lecture presents this as intuition rather than a solved full-system rule. The multi-joint version depends on configuration-dependent inertia and is left as future work.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 13.

Legged Robot Actuation

Why legged robot motors are heavy: they use large, high-torque motors with minimal gear reduction. This is not a design flaw — it's the optimal solution.

Reference: Seok et al. (2012) + KAIST DRCD lecture

Core Constraint: Contact Dynamics

Legged robots experience frequent, impulsive ground contact. This makes low passive impedance (reflected inertia + friction + damping) the dominant design requirement — more important than raw output power. Seok et al. frame this as maximizing transmission-transparency for high-force proprioceptive control.

The transcript contrasts this with manipulators: arms are often dominated by static load, precision, and force control, while legs are dominated by repeated impacts. This is why leg actuators moved toward QDD earlier and more aggressively than many manipulator joints.

1. Control Performance
- High reflected inertia ($N^2 J{motor}$) kills force-control bandwidth
- Contact force tracking degrades
- Backdrivability/transparency destroyed
- Force estimation from motor current becomes less reliable unless transmission impedance is modeled/compensated

2. Efficiency
- Touchdown is roughly a quasi-inelastic impact, where impact energy scales with:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2
$$
- Larger reflected $J$ → more energy lost per impact
- $\tau{out} \approx N\tau{motor}$, but $J{ref} \approx N^2J{motor}$ — inertia grows faster than torque

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 116-138; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slide 22; sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II.

3. Durability
- Large reflected inertia + low backdrivability → impact torque transients concentrate on gears
- High-ratio or poorly backdrivable harmonic/planetary geartrains can suffer accelerated fatigue, wear, backlash growth, and failure under repeated leg impacts
- This is not a ban on all planetary gearing: a simple low-ratio single-stage planetary can still fit QDD, while multi-stage or high-ratio planetary reducers add the inertia, friction, backlash, and part-count penalties that QDD tries to avoid

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 218-230; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 279-475.

headingPath: Design Direction: High-Torque Motor + Minimum Gearing
chunks.text:
Design Direction: High-Torque Motor + Minimum Gearing
- Maximize gap radius (rgap) to increase motor torque density
- Use the lowest possible gear ratio (ideally direct drive or single-stage)
- Accept heavier motor mass as the trade-off

This is the QDD (Quasi-Direct Drive) philosophy: sacrifice some mass efficiency to gain transparency, backdrivability, and robustness.

Important Nuance

The lecture argues against the oversimplified reading "QDD = low gear ratio is always theoretically best." In the ideal model, pizza and kimbap motor shapes can have the same output torque and reflected inertia if mass and technology level are fixed. The low-reduction choice becomes attractive because real gearboxes add friction, inertia, mass, backlash, and modeling difficulty.

Put differently: low reduction is often a robust engineering shortcut to low passive impedance. A high-reduction actuator can still be viable if the transmission is exceptionally efficient and backdrivable.

This distinction is especially important for reducer selection. Harmonic drives usually sit awkwardly with QDD-style legs because high reduction, preload/friction, and flexspline fatigue work against force transparency. Planetary drives split by implementation: the MIT Cheetah example below uses a low-ratio one-stage planetary, while a multi-stage/high-ratio planetary geartrain would drift away from the QDD goal.

Lecture 4 gives the cleaner vocabulary: backdrivability should usually mean friction/reverse-drive behavior, while reflected inertia should be tracked separately. Legged robots need both low friction and low reflected inertia because contact is impulsive.

headingPath: Seok et al. 2012 MIT Cheetah Example
chunks.text:
Seok et al. 2012 MIT Cheetah Example

The paper referenced by Lecture 1 applies the design argument to the MIT Cheetah front leg:

Figure: MIT Cheetah front leg layout with drive components near the shoulder to reduce distal leg inertia. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Fig. 1.
- Selected the largest motor radius that fit a 5 inch space constraint
- Used a minimal 1:5.8 gear ratio instead of more than 1:100 ratios common in many EM legged-robot actuators
- Designed for roughly 100 Nm desired peak joint torque, with a 21 Nm motor peak torque
- Used one-stage planetary gearing with four planet gears
- Located hip/knee motors and gear trains coaxially at the hip, with the knee driven by a steel linkage
- Reduced distal inertia using foam-core composite leg links and a tendon-reinforced foot

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Section III and Figs. 1, 4, and 5.

The force-control experiment showed that after friction compensation, proprioceptive force estimates from motor current tracked force-sensor measurements during impact with mean absolute error ratios of 0.041 and 0.049 for 3406 N/m and 5038 N/m virtual stiffness tests.

Pizza vs Kimbap in Practice

The third design principle shows that ideal scaling makes pizza and kimbap motors equivalent in output inertia. But in reality:
- Kimbap + high reduction: gearbox adds mass, inertia, friction → breaks ideal scaling
- Pizza + low reduction: simpler gearbox, fewer loss sources → easier to achieve good control

> Practical conclusion: pizza motor (flat, wide) + minimum gearing is the dominant architecture for legged robots.

The transcript adds a useful reversal: kimbap/long motors are not inherently inferior. Because longer motors waste less copper in end turns, they can be electrically more efficient. Their problem in legged robots is usually the high-quality high-reduction transmission required to preserve QDD-like behavior.

One-Line Summary

> Legged robots are contact-driven systems where high reduction creates reflected inertia ($N^2J$) + gearbox penalties that simultaneously destroy efficiency, force control, and durability. High-torque motor + minimum gearing to secure low passive impedance is the key.

More precise version: high-torque motor + minimum gearing is the conservative default for low passive impedance; high-ratio designs may compete only when the transmission is unusually efficient, low-friction, and impact-tolerant.

Resistive Braking

Resistive braking is a motor safety mode where the moving motor is electrically loaded so mechanical kinetic energy is converted into heat in the winding, driver path, or braking resistor. In the Lecture 5 homework prelude, the example is HOUND2 using braking behavior to remove leg energy quickly for safety.

Figure: resistive-braking q-axis current plot and HOUND2 demonstration image. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 12.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 46-54; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 12.

How to Read It

When a permanent-magnet motor is spinning, its back EMF can drive current if a circuit path is provided. If that path is a short, resistor, or controlled dissipative path, the current produces torque opposing motion. This is braking, not free position holding: the available braking torque depends on speed, resistance, winding inductance, driver state, and thermal limits.

The homework plot shows q-axis current becoming negative over a range of electrical speed, which is the expected sign for braking when the rotor is moving in the positive direction under the lecture's convention.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 12.

Design Cautions

Resistive braking is useful because it can work even when the controller is not actively commanding precise torque, but it is not automatically gentle. The braking current and heat must stay inside motor and driver limits, and a hard short can create abrupt deceleration. In legged robots, that trade-off may still be preferable when the alternative is an uncontrolled collision or self-damaging motion.

In the same homework bundle, the motor operating region problem asks how much controllable torque remains at speed, while resistive braking asks how to dissipate energy safely once stopping becomes the priority.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 46-54.
