Motor Current Control

Motor current control is the inner loop that makes measured d-q currents track reference currents. In an SPMSM, this is the practical way to command torque because:

$$
\tau \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

Lecture context: this page belongs to Lecture 3B, Juminchan's motor-driver/current-control seminar.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

The controller chooses d-q voltage commands, then space-vector-pwm and the motor-driver-inverter apply them to the motor.

In textbook terms, this page is the inner control layer inside vector control: the outer torque or speed logic chooses d-q current references, and the current regulator must make the torque-producing current track quickly enough that an AC motor behaves like a separately excited DC motor. chapter-06-current-regulators is the main textbook basis for the synchronous-frame PI, feedforward, and anti-windup structure used below.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-35; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-17.

Figure: d-q PI current loop with feedforward, voltage saturation, anti-windup, inverse Park transform, SVPWM, sensing, Clarke/Park transform, and encoder feedback. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 24.

First-Order Plant

Start from the d-q voltage equations. For a simple first pass, ignore angular-velocity terms:

$$
Vd \approx Rs id + Ld\frac{did}{dt}
$$

$$
Vq \approx Rs iq + Lq\frac{diq}{dt}
$$

Each axis then behaves like a first-order electrical plant:

$$
Gd(s)=\frac{id}{Vd}=\frac{1}{Ld s+Rs}
$$

$$
Gq(s)=\frac{iq}{Vq}=\frac{1}{Lq s+Rs}
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 20.

Because the plant is first order, Lecture 3B uses PI control rather than PID. A derivative term usually makes jitter/noise worse in this digital current-loop setting.

PI Gains by Pole-Zero Cancellation

Use:

$$
C(s)=KP+\frac{KI}{s}
$$

Pole-zero cancellation chooses:

$$
\frac{KI}{KP}=\frac{Rs}{L{dq}}
$$

With desired current-loop bandwidth $\omega{des}$:

$$
KP = L{dq}\omega{des}
$$

$$
KI = Rs\omega{des}
$$

The resulting closed-loop response is:

$$
Gs(s)=\frac{i{dq}}{i{dq,ref}}=\frac{\omega{des}}{s+\omega{des}}
$$

The lecture gives a practical bandwidth range of roughly $0.05-0.2$ times the control frequency.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 21.

The DC motor textbook gives the same pole-zero cancellation idea in scalar form. With back-EMF feedforward, a DC armature current loop can be reduced to a first-order target by choosing:

$$
K{pc}=La\omega{cc}
$$

$$
K{ic}=Ra\omega{cc}
$$

This is not a different rule; it is the one-axis version of choosing proportional gain from inductance and desired bandwidth, and integral gain from resistance and desired bandwidth.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 31-33.

Chapter 6 applies the same pole-zero cancellation rule directly to synchronous-frame AC current regulation. For PMSM current loops, the textbook gives the axis-specific starting point:

$$
K{pd}=Ld\omegac,\quad K{pq}=Lq\omegac
$$

$$
Ki=Rs\omegac
$$

For an SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so this collapses back toward the lecture's single $L{dq}$ rule. For an IPMSM, the d-axis and q-axis gains should start from their different inductances. This is still only an initial continuous-time design; switching frequency, current sampling, delay, filtering, voltage saturation, and motor-driver-nonidealities decide how aggressive the real bandwidth can be.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-13.

Feedforward Terms

The terms ignored in the first-order simplification are not gone. They are speed-dependent coupling and back EMF:

$$
Vd \approx Rs id + Ld\frac{did}{dt} - \omegae Lq iq
$$

$$
Vq \approx Rs iq + Lq\frac{diq}{dt} + \omegae Ld id + \omegae\phif
$$

Since $\omegae$, $id$, $iq$, $Ld$, $Lq$, and $\phif$ can be estimated or measured, these terms can be added as feedforward:

$$
V{d,ff} = -\omegae Lq iq
$$

$$
V{q,ff} = \omegae Ld id + \omegae\phif
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 23.

Lecture 3B recommends starting without feedforward when debugging. A strong PI loop can often handle the disturbance, while incorrect feedforward makes tuning harder.

The textbook version makes the same trade explicit. Feedforward can simplify the motor seen by the PI loop back toward an $R$-$L$ plant, but accurate compensation needs useful estimates of inductance, magnet flux linkage, current, and rotor speed. If those estimates are poor, feedforward becomes a structured voltage error rather than a free improvement.

Figure: textbook synchronous-frame PI current regulator with feedforward and anti-windup around a PWM inverter. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, page 16, Fig. 6.14.
Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 15-17.

Voltage Saturation and Anti-Windup

The inverter cannot command unlimited d-q voltage. Under the lecture's SVPWM convention:

$$
\sqrt{Vd^2+Vq^2} \leq \frac{V{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Practical controllers usually apply a smaller limit to leave modulation margin.

When the voltage command saturates, the PI integrator can keep accumulating error even though the actuator cannot apply the requested voltage. Anti-windup prevents this integrator runaway. Lecture 3B mentions backward Euler integration and back-calculation anti-windup.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 24-25.

The textbook describes the same failure mode as integral windup: once the voltage command is limited, the integrator can keep accumulating past error and the loop behaves effectively open-loop until the accumulated value unwinds. It lists back-calculation, conditional integration, and limited integration, with back-calculation shown as the common dynamic anti-windup method. For the DC current controller, it gives the usual back-calculation gain choice:

$$
Ka=\frac{1}{K{pc}}
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 35-36.

Chapter 7 adds a PWM-specific warning: if the command enters overmodulation, the inverter no longer acts like a linear voltage actuator. Dynamic overmodulation may be used during transients, but steady overmodulation increases harmonics and degrades the clean d-q current-regulator assumption.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 60-67.

Rotor Angle and Speed Feedback

D-q current control only works if the controller uses the right electrical angle. For PMSM vector control, the d-axis is aligned with rotor magnet flux, so startup needs absolute rotor position or an incremental encoder plus an initial-position routine. Chapter 9 then separates three feedback cases:
- a resolver or absolute encoder can give startup position directly
- an incremental encoder gives pulse-derived speed and relative position, with M, T, and M/T speed estimates trading low-speed resolution against timing complexity
- sensorless observers avoid sensor hardware but become weak at low speed when back EMF is small

This is why current-loop debugging should not treat the encoder angle, speed estimate, and inverter voltage error as separate afterthoughts. They define the rotating frame and feedforward terms that the PI loop assumes.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 5-15; see also chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control.

Practical Tuning

The lecture's tuning advice is intentionally pragmatic:
- exact $Rs$, $Ld$, $Lq$, and $\phif$ are hard to know
- start with small gains
- inspect current step response
- adjust gains gradually toward the desired bandwidth and overshoot
- add feedforward later, after the feedback loop behaves
- remember that the real loop is discrete-time, delayed, noisy, and voltage-limited

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 26.

Motor Current Sensing

Motor current sensing measures the actual phase currents that motor-current-control tries to regulate. Because torque in an SPMSM is controlled mainly through current, a motor driver is only as good as its current estimate.

Lecture context: this page belongs to Lecture 3B, Juminchan's motor-driver/current-control seminar.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

Shunt Resistor Measurement

Lecture 3B focuses on shunt-resistor sensing:

$$
V{shunt} = iR{shunt}
$$

A shunt resistor is a precise, very low resistance inserted in the current path. Typical values in the lecture are about:

$$
1-10\ \mathrm{m\Omega}
$$

Because the voltage drop is small, the signal is amplified with an op-amp and then measured by the MCU ADC.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 15.

Low-Side, In-Line, and High-Side Sensing

Current can be sensed in several positions:

| Location | Main advantage | Main drawback |
|---|---|---|
| Low-side shunt | cheap, ground-referenced, practical | only measurable when the low-side FET is on |
| In-line shunt | can measure phase current directly at any time | needs high common-mode rejection, more expensive |
| High-side shunt | bus-side measurement option | also has common-mode voltage problems |
| Hall sensor | lower insertion heating at high current | lower precision or higher cost depending on device |

The low-side shunt is popular because one side of the measurement is near ground. In-line and high-side shunts require the amplifier to reject a large common-mode voltage while measuring a small differential voltage.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-17.

Timing Window

With triple-shunt low-side sensing, the ADC cannot sample whenever it wants. The relevant low-side FET must be on, and the samples should be synchronized.

Figure: low-side shunt sensing depends on switch state and ADC timing. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 17.

This is one reason SVPWM should leave phase-voltage margin. If the command saturates so hard that a low-side window disappears, low-side current sensing can fail.

Using three shunts gives redundancy. In a balanced three-wire motor:

$$
ia+ib+ic=0
$$

So two phase currents are mathematically enough, but a third measurement can help reject bias and detect measurement errors.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 16-17.

PWM-Synchronized Sampling

Chapter 7 gives the textbook version of the timing rule. A PWM-driven phase current contains switching ripple as well as the fundamental current that produces torque and flux. In symmetrical SVPWM, sampling at the midpoint of zero-vector intervals can recover the fundamental component more cleanly, so practical drives synchronize ADC sampling to the carrier peaks.

The chapter also ties sampling directly to controller bandwidth: sampling twice per switching period, often called double sampling, gives a maximum attainable synchronous-frame current-regulator bandwidth of about one-tenth of the switching frequency. Delays in sensors, filters, ADC conversion, and computation reduce the usable bandwidth further.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 74-75; see also chapter-07-pwm-inverters.

Noise and Scaling Problems

Lecture 3B calls out several practical failure modes:
- op-amps amplify both signal and noise
- analog low-pass filtering should usually be placed after the amplifier output, not in the motor current path
- digital filtering reduces noise but adds delay
- ADC samples must be synchronized
- Clarke/Park transform convention changes scaling
- op-amp gain and ADC range must be calibrated
- ground bounce can move the measurement reference
- PCB placement can inject switching noise into the sensing path

Comparing the driver estimate against an oscilloscope current probe is useful even when the probe value is not used in the final controller.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 18; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 217-223.

Motor Driver and Three-Phase Inverter

A motor driver for a PMSM/SPMSM is the power electronics and control system that turns current commands into switched phase voltages. Its job is to make the phase currents that create flux linkage and torque.

Lecture context: this page belongs to Lecture 3B, Juminchan's separate Motor Driver / Current Control seminar, not the Lecture 3A homework-review block.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

For the ideal SPMSM case:

$$
\tau \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

So the control chain is:

$$
v \rightarrow i \rightarrow \lambda \rightarrow \tau
$$

This is why a driver is usually treated as a current controller, not just as a voltage source.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 8.

Why an Inverter Is Needed

The power source is usually a DC battery or DC supply. The motor needs controlled three-phase voltages. A real robot driver normally does not create smooth analog phase voltages directly because that would be expensive and inefficient.

Instead, it uses PWM/SVPWM: switch the available DC bus quickly, then rely on motor inductance to smooth the current response. If an oscilloscope is connected to the phase voltage, it sees pulses; the motor current is smoother because the winding behaves like a large inductor.

Chapter 7 of Electric Motor Control frames this as a voltage-source inverter (VSI): a DC-link voltage and capacitor feed inverter legs whose terminals are switched between DC rails. The chapter contrasts this with current-source inverters, but the robot-drive topology on this page is the VSI case. For the source-level inverter/PWM route, see chapter-07-pwm-inverters.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 2-17.

Half-Bridge

A half-bridge has a high-side switch and a low-side switch. In the simplified model:

| High-side FET | Low-side FET | Output node |
|---:|---:|---|
| 0 | 0 | floating |
| 1 | 0 | $V{dc}$ |
| 0 | 1 | $0$ |
| 1 | 1 | short circuit |

The last case is forbidden because it directly shorts the DC bus through both switches. Real gate drivers insert dead time to avoid that shoot-through case.

Three-Phase Inverter

A three-phase inverter uses three half-bridges, one for each terminal. With one legal high/low state per phase, there are eight useful switch states.

Figure: three half-bridges create eight useful switch states, treated as space vectors. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 8.

The states $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$ are zero-voltage vectors because all three phase terminals move together. The six remaining states are active voltage vectors.

The motor neutral point is not ground. In a wye-equivalent model with a floating neutral, the neutral voltage depends on the average of the terminal voltages. That is why an active state such as $(1,0,0)$ does not simply mean phase A receives $V{dc}$ relative to neutral.

In Chapter 7 notation, those six active vectors all have magnitude $\frac{2}{3}V{dc}$ in the stationary d-q plane, and adjacent vectors are 60 electrical degrees apart. That vector view is what makes space-vector-pwm a timing problem: choose the two adjacent active states and the two zero states so the average voltage over one PWM period matches the requested vector.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-41.

High-Side Gate Drive

The low-side MOSFET is comparatively easy to turn on because its source is near ground. The high-side MOSFET is harder: when the phase node is near $V{dc}$, the high-side gate must be driven above that source voltage.

Two common approaches are:
- Bootstrap circuit: uses a diode and capacitor to temporarily provide a gate voltage above the switch node. It is simple but needs recharge time, so it cannot hold a high-side device on indefinitely in all operating patterns.
- Charge pump: actively maintains a boosted gate-drive supply, so modern gate-driver chips often prefer it.

This is why an MCU GPIO pin does not directly switch the inverter MOSFETs. A gate driver sits between logic-level commands and power MOSFET gates.

DC H-Bridge Contrast

The same switching idea appears in a simpler DC motor drive as an H-bridge. Instead of three half-bridges for three motor terminals, a DC H-bridge uses two half-bridges around one armature winding. Diagonal switch pairs can apply positive or negative voltage, which enables four-quadrant motoring and braking.

The textbook distinguishes bipolar and unipolar H-bridge PWM. Bipolar switching alternates the motor terminal voltage between $+V{dc}$ and $-V{dc}$, while unipolar switching modulates the two bridge legs separately so the winding sees smaller voltage steps. The same hardware lesson carries into three-phase inverters: the motor inductance filters switched voltage into current, but the switching pattern changes current ripple and torque ripple.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 45-47.

Motor Driver Nonidealities

A real inverter does not apply the exact voltage requested by the controller:

$$
V{ref} \neq V{real}
$$

Lecture 3B treats this as the central practical warning. The current loop can hide many voltage errors, but they still matter for tuning, low-speed behavior, heating, torque ripple, and sensorless control.

Textbook Chapter 7 gives the inverter-side source for these effects, especially dead time, switching delay, and current-direction-dependent voltage error. Chapter 9 explains why the same voltage errors are especially damaging to model-based sensorless control, where the controller infers flux or rotor angle from measured current and an estimated applied voltage. See chapter-07-pwm-inverters and chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control for the source summaries.

Lecture context: this page belongs to Lecture 3B, Juminchan's motor-driver/current-control seminar.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

Figure: dead time, MOSFET delay, and body-diode effects make the actual voltage differ from the reference voltage. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 33.

Dead Time

MOSFETs take finite time to turn on and off. If the high-side and low-side devices in one half-bridge are on at the same time, the DC bus is shorted.

Gate drivers avoid this by inserting dead time: a short interval where both switches are off during commutation.

Dead time protects the hardware, but it changes the actual pulse width. The same absolute dead time is a larger fraction of a small voltage command, so its effect is most visible at low voltage.

Chapter 7 makes the current-direction dependence explicit. During dead time, motor current freewheels through a diode, so the actual terminal voltage depends on whether current is positive or negative. Accumulated over a half cycle, the average error magnitude is:

$$
V{err}=t{dead}V{dc}f{sw}
$$

This causes fundamental voltage loss, low-order current harmonics, torque pulsation, additional loss, and flux-angle distortion. The textbook emphasizes that the dead-time effect is especially significant at low speed because the desired motor voltage is small.

Figure: inverter leg voltage error caused by dead time for positive and negative current directions. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 69, Fig. 7.80.
Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 68-71.

Body Diode

An NMOS device includes an intrinsic body diode. During dead time, motor current still needs a path because inductor current cannot stop instantly. The body diode provides a freewheel path, but it introduces a diode voltage drop.

That voltage drop is part of why the real phase voltage differs from the commanded voltage during switching transitions.

Dead-Time Compensation

If inserted dead time is known, the controller can compensate by adding a feedforward correction to the pulse width. The sign depends on current direction.

Lecture 3B notes two practical points:
- compensation is most useful at low voltage, where dead time is a large part of the command
- very small currents can be dominated by current-sensor jitter, so compensation may be disabled below a threshold

The textbook version gives the same warning in another form: compensation depends on detecting current polarity correctly, especially near current zero crossings where noise and switching ripple can corrupt the sign decision.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 72-76.

MOSFET Delay and Gate Charge

MOSFET turn-on and turn-off delay vary by part and condition. Gate charge also means the gate voltage cannot move instantly. These delays are usually smaller than intentionally inserted dead time, but they still add voltage error.

Many current-controlled drivers tolerate these errors because feedback corrects current. Sensorless voltage-model methods are more sensitive because they need a better estimate of $V{real}$.

Chapter 9 explains the same sensitivity from the observer side. Back-EMF or voltage-model sensorless control estimates flux or rotor position from voltage, current, and motor parameters. At low speed, back EMF is small, so inverter voltage drops and dead-time errors can dominate the signal the observer is trying to use.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 12-15.

Ringing and Parasitics

Fast switching interacts with parasitic capacitance and inductance. The result can be MOSFET ringing, EMI, and torque/current ripple.

Common mitigation methods include:
- increasing gate resistance
- limiting gate-driver current
- tuning smart-gate-driver settings
- improving PCB layout and current-return paths

The trade-off is that slower switching can reduce ringing but increases transition time and switching loss.

Delay and Noise

Nonidealities are not limited to MOSFETs. Lecture 3B also flags:
- ADC sampling delay
- digital control-period delay
- sensor noise
- low-pass-filter delay
- op-amp noise and offset
- ground bounce from high-current or high-voltage switching paths

Every filter that reduces noise adds delay. Every delay reduces phase margin. That is why the lecture repeatedly frames real motor-driver work as a compromise between ideal control diagrams and hardware behavior.

Space Vector PWM

Space vector PWM (SVPWM) is a modulation method for a three-phase inverter. It approximates a desired voltage vector by spending controlled fractions of each switching period in nearby inverter switch states.

In Lecture 3B, Juminchan's motor-driver seminar, SVPWM is the practical bridge between a d-q voltage command from motor-current-control and real MOSFET switching in motor-driver-inverter.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 12.

For the textbook source route, use chapter-07-pwm-inverters. Chapter 7 grounds the same lecture picture in voltage-source inverter switching states, modulation index, common-mode offset injection, overmodulation, dead time, and PWM-synchronized current measurement.

Time-Averaged Voltage

Within one switching period $Ts$, SVPWM mixes active vectors and zero vectors. In one sector, the idea is:

$$
\mathbf V{ref}
=
\frac{1}{Ts}
\left(
T1\mathbf V1
+T2\mathbf V2
+T{0a}\mathbf V0
+T{0b}\mathbf V7
\right)
$$

where $\mathbf V1$ and $\mathbf V2$ are adjacent active vectors and $\mathbf V0$, $\mathbf V7$ are zero-voltage vectors.

The zero-vector time does not create net phase voltage, but it fills the switching period. Symmetric SVPWM splits this zero time so the pulses are distributed more evenly.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 12.

The textbook expresses the same volt-second balance as:

$$
\mathbf V^Ts = \mathbf VnT1 + \mathbf V{n+1}T2
$$

$$
T0=Ts-(T1+T2)
$$

The two zero states $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$ do not apply effective load voltage, while the six active vectors have magnitude $\frac{2}{3}V{dc}$ and are spaced by 60 electrical degrees in the stationary d-q plane.

Figure: output voltage vectors for a three-phase voltage-source inverter in the stationary d-q plane. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 37, Fig. 7.38.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-41.

Why Use Both Zero Vectors

The seminar emphasizes ripple. Current in the motor winding rises and falls according to the applied voltage pulse pattern. If the active-vector time is bunched together, current ripple increases. If the zero-vector time is split symmetrically around the active vectors, current ripple is reduced.

Lower current ripple means lower torque ripple.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 12.

Voltage Hexagon and Normal Modulation

The active space vectors form a voltage hexagon. For smooth non-overmodulated operation, the commanded voltage vector should stay inside the hexagon's inscribed circle.

Figure: non-overmodulated SVPWM uses the inscribed circle inside the space-vector hexagon. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 13.

Under the lecture's convention, the phase-voltage magnitude limit used by the controller is:

$$
\sqrt{Vd^2 + Vq^2} \leq \frac{V{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

The active vector magnitude is shown as $\frac{2}{3}V{dc}$ because the motor neutral is floating, not tied to ground.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 13 and 25.

Modulation Index and DC-Link Use

Chapter 7 defines modulation index as the ratio between the inverter phase-voltage fundamental peak and half the DC-link voltage:

$$
MI=\frac{V{1,peak}}{V{dc}/2}
$$

Sinusoidal PWM is linear up to $MI=1$, which is 78.5% of the six-step fundamental. Symmetrical SVPWM reaches the same linear voltage increase as third-harmonic injection, about 15.5% more than SPWM, and the normal linear boundary corresponds to 90.7% of six-step operation. That is the textbook basis for the lecture's practical voltage-margin warning.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 25-41.

Common-Mode Offset View

SVPWM can also be implemented as carrier PWM plus a common-mode offset voltage. Adding the same offset to all three phase references changes pole-voltage duty cycles, but it cancels from line-to-line voltage in a floating-neutral three-phase motor. This is the common framework that connects SPWM, third-harmonic injection, symmetrical SVPWM, and discontinuous PWM.

Discontinuous PWM intentionally clamps one inverter leg to a DC rail for part of the electrical cycle. Its goal is usually lower switching loss; the trade is changed ripple and common-mode behavior.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 50-59.

Overmodulation and Margin

Overmodulation uses voltage vectors outside the normal inscribed-circle region. Lecture 3B warns that it can make the motor vibrate or behave poorly, so practical drives usually leave a margin instead of commanding the full theoretical boundary.

The seminar's rule of thumb is to leave about 1-5% phase-voltage margin.

Chapter 7 separates two reasons a controller may enter overmodulation. Dynamic overmodulation handles transient current-loop demands, such as a speed-command or load-torque change. Steady-state overmodulation intentionally uses more inverter voltage near six-step operation, which can extend high-speed operation but breaks the linear voltage-command assumption and increases harmonics.

That margin has two consequences:
- it reduces the practical motor operating region
- it preserves sampling windows for low-side current sensing

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 13 and 16.

Textbook cross-check: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 60-67.

Electrical Angle

Electrical angle measures position in the motor's magnetic/electrical cycle, not just the physical shaft angle.

Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Hall-effect commutation placement discussion and Fig. 7.

Mechanical angle is the actual rotor rotation: one shaft revolution is 360 mechanical degrees.

Electrical angle counts how far the rotor magnetic field has advanced relative to the stator phase pattern. A motor with $p$ pole pairs goes through $p$ electrical cycles per one mechanical revolution:

$$
\theta{electrical} = p\theta{mechanical}
$$

Equivalently, with pole count $P$:

$$
\thetae=\frac{P}{2}\thetam
$$

$$
\theta{mechanical} = \frac{\theta{electrical}}{p}
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 10.

Examples:
- 2-pole motor = 1 pole pair: 30 electrical degrees = 30 mechanical degrees.
- 8-pole motor = 4 pole pairs: 30 electrical degrees = 7.5 mechanical degrees.
- 8-pole motor = 4 pole pairs: one full 360-degree shaft revolution = 1440 electrical degrees.

This matters for Hall sensor placement because commutation depends on magnetic/electrical phase, not only physical package angle. Two motors may need the same Hall-sensor spacing in electrical degrees but different mechanical spacing if their pole counts differ.

Related
- bldc-electronic-commutation
- hall-effect-position-sensing

Magnetic Torque and Reluctance Torque

Electric motors can produce torque through permanent-magnet field alignment, magnetic reluctance alignment, or both.

Figure: magnetic torque aligns a magnet with an electromagnet; reluctance torque aligns an iron rotor with the lower-reluctance flux path. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 4.

Magnetic Torque

Magnetic torque comes from interaction between the stator's current-generated magnetic field and permanent magnets on the rotor.

In the lecture's simple picture:
- the stator winding acts like an electromagnet
- the rotor permanent magnet has a preferred magnetic direction
- current makes the stator field pull the rotor magnet into alignment

This is the dominant ideal torque mechanism in SPMSM motors used in many robot actuators.

Reluctance Torque

Reluctance torque comes from a rotor moving toward the orientation that gives magnetic flux the easier path.

The lecture's intuition is:
- iron is a much easier flux path than air/vacuum
- a rotor with direction-dependent iron/air paths has different reluctance depending on angle
- applying stator current makes the rotor rotate toward the lower-reluctance orientation

So reluctance torque is not "magnet pulls magnet"; it is the motor using a geometry-dependent magnetic-resistance difference.

The textbook formalizes this with magnetic reluctance $\mathcal R$. For a coil with $N$ turns:

$$
L=\frac{\lambda}{i}=\frac{N^2}{\mathcal R}
$$

So geometry that changes the air-gap reluctance also changes inductance. In a rotating machine, torque can come from the rotor moving toward the angle where the magnetic system has lower reluctance and higher inductance for the applied current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 10 and 23-25.

headingPath: Why Magnets Can Be Ignored in the Reluctance Argument
chunks.text:
Why Magnets Can Be Ignored in the Reluctance Argument

For the reluctance-torque intuition, the lecture treats permanent magnets as vacuum-like obstacles rather than as extra iron.

The reason is local and qualitative: in a good permanent magnet, the magnetic domains are already aligned. Additional stator field does not align much more material in the same way it does in soft iron. For deciding whether a flux path has easy iron or difficult non-iron regions, the magnet region can be grouped with high-reluctance regions.

This does not mean a permanent magnet is physically the same as vacuum. It means the magnet is not the part that creates the useful incremental saliency for reluctance torque in this simplified argument.

Motor-Type Spectrum

Figure: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM arranged from reluctance-torque-dominant to magnet-torque-dominant. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 3.

The slide places motor types on a rough spectrum:
- SynRM: pure reluctance motor; no permanent magnet
- PM-assisted SynRM / IPMSM / inset SPMSM: mixed magnet and reluctance torque
- SPMSM: mostly pure magnet torque in the ideal symmetric model

For an ideal SPMSM, if you ignore the surface magnets for the reluctance calculation, the remaining rotor iron looks nearly rotationally symmetric. There is no strong angle-dependent flux path, so ideal reluctance torque is near zero.

For an IPMSM, the internal magnet cavities and iron bridges create anisotropic flux paths. That saliency lets the motor use reluctance torque in addition to magnet torque.

Chapter 4 presents the same point in PMSM modeling language: for an IPMSM, $Ld$ and $Lq$ differ, so the torque equation contains both a permanent-magnet term and a reluctance/saliency term. For an ideal SPMSM, $Ld \approx Lq$, so the reluctance term largely cancels.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 48-49.

Control Consequences

In vector-control terms, saliency gives IPMSM one extra design lever. Chapter 5 shows that when $Lq>Ld$, negative d-axis current can make reluctance torque add to magnet torque, so maximum-torque-per-ampere control is not simply $id=0$ as it is for the ideal SPMSM.

Chapter 8 adds the high-speed consequence. In a PMSM, magnet flux cannot be reduced directly, so flux weakening uses negative d-axis current to oppose the magnet flux. That makes larger d-axis inductance useful: it gives each ampere of negative d-axis current more authority over effective flux linkage. The combined insight is captured in pmsm-topology-control-consequences: IPMSM saliency helps both low-speed MTPA and high-speed flux weakening, while SPMSM keeps simpler low-speed control but usually has a weaker high-speed flux-weakening lever.

This is why IPMSM is attractive in applications that need wide constant-power speed range. It does not automatically mean IPMSM is the right robot actuator choice; the robotics question also depends on manufacturability, rotor stress, torque ripple, saliency maps, sensor/control complexity, thermal behavior, and the joint speed range.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 38-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; see chapter-05-vector-control and chapter-08-high-speed-operation.

> QUESTION
> Lecture 2 asks why robotics usually uses SPMSM rather than IPMSM, even though IPMSM can combine magnet torque and reluctance torque. Chapter 8 explains why IPMSM is favored for high-speed operation, but it still does not fully answer the robotics-vs-EV topology trade-off. Revisit this when a source covers robot actuator motor topology selection directly.

Three-Phase Rotating Magnetic Field

A rotating magnetic field is the air-gap field produced when the stator phase currents are coordinated so their combined magnetic axis moves around the stator.

The stator iron and coils do not physically rotate. The controller changes the phase currents, so the resultant N/S field direction moves electrically around the air gap. A permanent-magnet rotor produces torque because it tries to align with that moving field.

For a brushless permanent-magnet robot motor, the energy path is usually:

text
DC battery or DC bus
 -> three-phase inverter
 -> phase currents in fixed stator windings
 -> rotating stator magnetic field
 -> permanent-magnet rotor follows synchronously

This is the field-level reason why BLDC/PMSM naming is confusing. The product can be powered from a DC bus, but the motor action depends on time-varying phase currents and a moving stator field.

Sector-Stepped and Smooth Versions

The same intuition can be drawn at two resolutions:

text
Six-step / block commutation:
 the effective field direction jumps from sector to sector

Sinusoidal current:
 three phase currents are shifted by 120 electrical degrees,
 so the resultant field rotates more smoothly

Both are ways of making the rotor see a magnetic field that moves ahead of it. The difference is the waveform and control method, not whether the rotor is surface-magnet or interior-magnet. See pmsm-control-waveform-language for the control-label distinction.

Synchronous PM Motor Reading

A PMSM is "synchronous" because the rotor magnetic axis follows the rotating stator field at the same average electrical speed. The exact relationship between mechanical shaft angle and magnetic/electrical angle depends on pole-pair count; see electrical-angle.

For ideal SPMSM modeling, the rotor d-axis is aligned with permanent-magnet flux, and the q-axis is the torque-producing direction. That rotor-coordinate view is what later becomes the d-q model in spmsm-dq-voltage-equations and current control in motor-current-control.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 36-42; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2.

The example is induction-motor oriented, but the rotating stator-field animation is useful background for PMSM/BLDC study.

BLDC, PMSM, and SPMSM Terminology

Use this page only as the naming map. It answers what each label names, which labels are topology labels, and how to read loose BLDC language in robot-actuator notes.

The compact rule is:
- PMSM names the permanent-magnet synchronous machine family.
- SPMSM and IPMSM name rotor topologies inside that family.
- BLDC is a loose product/control label, not a clean physical topology class.

In this wiki, if a source says "BLDC motor" but the hardware is a brushless permanent-magnet actuator with an inverter drive, the safe default is to read it as an SPMSM unless the source is explicitly discussing six-step commutation, trapezoidal back EMF, Hall-sector control, or product naming.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 36-42; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - transcript.md, lines 486-494.

Term Map

| Term | Names what | Does not specify | Safe reading in this wiki |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| BLDC | A brushless permanent-magnet motor label used in products, drive notes, and beginner explanations | Rotor magnet placement, exact torque mechanism, exact phase-current waveform | Usually casual language for a surface-PM brushless motor plus electronic commutation; do not infer topology from the label alone |
| PMSM | The permanent-magnet synchronous machine family | Surface vs interior magnets | Umbrella term for SPMSM and IPMSM |
| SPMSM | Surface-mounted rotor magnets | Drive waveform | Common robot-actuator topology; often the hardware behind casual "BLDC" naming |
| IPMSM | Interior rotor magnets | Drive waveform | Interior magnets plus saliency; can produce both magnet torque and reluctance torque |

Strict topology view:

text
PMSM
├── SPMSM (surface-mounted rotor magnets)
└── IPMSM (interior rotor magnets)

BLDC is omitted from the strict tree because it mixes product history, back-EMF assumptions, and commutation language. It can be useful in a driver discussion, but it is not the clean label to use for rotor geometry.

Outrunner and inrunner language is a different axis again. It describes whether the outer bell or the inner shaft is the rotating part, not whether the motor is BLDC or PMSM, and not whether the magnet topology is SPMSM or IPMSM. Use outrunner-vs-inrunner-bldc-motors when the source is really comparing outer-rotor versus inner-rotor packaging, especially in drone propeller selection.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner BLDC Motor vs Inner Rotor BLDC Motor.md, mechanical-structure comparison; sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, structural-differences section.

Textbook Taxonomy Check

The Electric Motor Control textbook shows why the naming is confusing. In its classification figure, "Brushless DC" appears near the DC/AC boundary while the permanent-magnet synchronous branch appears under AC motors; the same figure also annotates the permanent-magnet synchronous branch with "BLAC motor." This supports the wiki rule: BLDC, BLAC, and PMSM language often mixes waveform/control naming with physical topology.

Figure: motor classification tree showing brushless DC near the DC/AC boundary and permanent-magnet synchronous machines under AC motors. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2, Fig. 1.1.

Chapter 4 then treats SPMSM, IPMSM, PM-assisted SynRM, and SynRM as rotor/topology categories on a spectrum from magnet torque to reluctance torque. That is the topology language to prefer when discussing d-q torque, saliency, or actuator selection.

Figure: SynRM, PM-assisted SynRM, IPMSM, inset SPMSM, and SPMSM arranged from reluctance-torque-dominant to magnet-torque-dominant. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, slide 3.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 2; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 12-14.

Why BLDC Feels Like DC

From the user's power-terminal viewpoint, a BLDC actuator often looks like a DC product:

text
DC battery or DC bus
 -> inverter / motor driver
 -> switched three-phase phase currents
 -> rotating stator field
 -> permanent-magnet rotor follows

That does not mean the motor windings simply receive constant DC. The DC bus is the energy source before the inverter; the phase currents and stator field are actively switched or modulated by the driver. See three-phase-rotating-magnetic-field for the field intuition and pmsm-control-waveform-language for six-step, sinusoidal, and FOC naming.

The clean mental model is:

text
BLDC: how the product or drive is often described
PMSM: what kind of synchronous PM machine it is
SPMSM/IPMSM: where the rotor magnets are placed

Practical Reading Rules
- Use SPMSM/IPMSM when the source is about rotor geometry, torque generation, d-q models, saliency, or actuator selection.
- Use BLDC when the source is deliberately talking about six-step or block commutation, trapezoidal back EMF, Hall-sector feedback, or casual product naming.
- Use outrunner/inrunner when the source is about rotor packaging, motor shape, torque radius, rotor inertia, cooling path, or propeller/ducted-fan fit.
- Do not infer waveform from topology or topology from waveform label alone. The same physical motor can be driven with six-step/block commutation or sinusoidal/FOC control if the driver and feedback support it.
- When the source moves from naming into MTPA, negative d-axis current, or wide constant-power speed range, switch to pmsm-topology-control-consequences instead of keeping that discussion on this taxonomy page.

> WARNING
> "BLDC is DC" and "PMSM is AC" are useful shortcuts, but they are not clean taxonomy. A brushless robot motor still needs an inverter/driver, rotor-position information or estimation, and commutation or current-control logic.

Unified Lecture Reading

The lectures are not making different claims; they are layering the same naming correction at different abstraction levels.

| Lecture | Role in the unified picture |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | Actuator-level intuition: the robot motor people casually call BLDC is better read as SPMSM because the driver needs rotor position to commutate current and make torque in the desired direction. |
| 2 | Formal taxonomy and model: SPMSM is the surface-magnet topology used in robotics, IPMSM adds saliency and reluctance torque, and SynRM has no magnets. |
| 4 | Design-language correction: the same motor is more accurately SPMSM, while BLDC has become an overloaded industry/paper label that can hide the actual topology. |

Put together, this wiki should read BLDC as a loose control/product label and SPMSM as the physical rotor topology. Use BLDC only when the source is explicitly talking about six-step commutation, trapezoidal back EMF, Hall-sector sensing, or casual naming.

DC Motor Control

DC motors are a useful minimal model for understanding motor-operating-region, back-EMF, current control, and PWM drives before moving to three-phase PMSM control. The same structure appears repeatedly in robot actuator notes: voltage creates current, current creates torque, motion creates back-EMF, and the driver must respect voltage and current limits.

Dynamic Model

The textbook models a DC motor drive with four coupled equations:

$$
Va = Ra ia + La\frac{d ia}{dt} + ea
$$

$$
ea = ke\phif\omegam
$$

$$
Te = kT\phif ia
$$

$$
Te = J\frac{d\omegam}{dt} + B\omegam + TL
$$

Here $Va$ is armature voltage, $ia$ is armature current, $Ra$ and $La$ are armature resistance and inductance, $ea$ is back-EMF, $\phif$ is field flux, $\omegam$ is mechanical angular speed, $J$ is inertia, $B$ is viscous friction, and $TL$ is load torque. In the textbook's SI convention, $kT=ke$.

Figure: equivalent circuit for the armature equation and moving-conductor back-EMF. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 5, Figs. 2.5-2.6.

This is why a DC motor feels like the scalar version of a PMSM q-axis model. Back-EMF subtracts from the voltage available for current, and current is the torque-producing quantity.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 4-6.

Speed-Torque Curve

At steady state, set $dia/dt=0$ and $d\omegam/dt=0$. Neglecting viscous friction, the textbook gives:

$$
\omegam = \frac{Va}{k\phif} - \frac{Ra}{(k\phif)^2}TL
$$

The slope is negative: as load torque increases, the armature current and resistive voltage drop increase, leaving less voltage margin for back-EMF and speed. This is the DC-motor version of the high-speed torque limit in motor-operating-region.

Speed Control Regions

Below base speed, a DC motor normally uses armature voltage control. The field flux is held constant, and speed changes roughly linearly with armature voltage. Torque is controlled by armature current, so the same current can produce the same torque across this region.

Above base speed, the terminal voltage has reached its limit. A wound-field DC motor can reduce field flux to keep back-EMF manageable and extend speed. This is field-flux control or field weakening. Torque per ampere falls as flux is reduced, so the high-speed region is approximately a constant-power region rather than a constant-torque region. A permanent-magnet DC motor cannot use this field-winding method.

Figure: DC motor constant-torque and constant-power capability curve. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 11, Fig. 2.11.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 7-11.

Cascaded Control Structure

A motor control system commonly nests loops from fastest to slowest:
- current loop inside the drive
- speed loop around current
- position loop around speed

The current loop must have the widest bandwidth because torque depends directly on current. The speed and position loops can be slower because mechanical states cannot change as quickly as electrical current.

For a DC current loop with back-EMF feedforward and pole-zero cancellation, the textbook gives:

$$
K{pc}=La\omega{cc}
$$

$$
K{ic}=Ra\omega{cc}
$$

where $\omega{cc}$ is the desired current-control bandwidth. The gains depend on the motor's armature inductance and resistance, so the same numeric controller gains do not transfer cleanly between motors.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 19 and 31-34.

Bandwidth and Anti-Windup

The current-loop bandwidth is limited by switching frequency and current sampling. The textbook rule of thumb is up to about one-tenth of switching frequency when current is sampled twice per switching period, and lower when sampled once per period. It also recommends keeping the bandwidth below about one twenty-fifth of the sampling frequency in the once-per-period case.

Integrator windup appears when a PI controller keeps integrating error while the voltage command is already saturated. Back-calculation anti-windup feeds the difference between the pre-limit and post-limit voltage command back into the integrator path. The textbook gives the common anti-windup gain choice:

$$
Ka = \frac{1}{K{pc}}
$$

This is the DC-motor version of the same saturation problem handled in motor-current-control for PMSM drives.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 34-36.

H-Bridge PWM

A four-quadrant chopper, or H-bridge, lets a DC motor operate in forward/reverse motoring and braking. The textbook describes two PWM schemes:
- Bipolar switching: diagonal switch pairs alternate, so the motor voltage jumps between $+V{dc}$ and $-V{dc}$. This gives smooth average-voltage control but larger current ripple.
- Unipolar switching: the two bridge legs are modulated separately, so the motor sees $0$ and $+V{dc}$ or $0$ and $-V{dc}$. This reduces current and torque ripple, but the average-voltage sign transition can be less smooth.

Figure: H-bridge circuit for four-quadrant DC motor operation. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 45, Fig. 2.47.

Figure: unipolar H-bridge switching waveforms and current ripple comparison. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 47, Figs. 2.50-2.51.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-2---Control-of-direct-current-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 45-47.

Induction Motor Basics

An induction motor is an AC motor whose stator is energized directly but whose rotor current is produced by electromagnetic induction. It is less central than PMSM/SPMSM in the robot-actuator lecture path, but it is important background for rotating magnetic fields, slip, equivalent circuits, and reference-frame modeling.

Rotating Magnetic Field

Balanced three-phase stator currents in windings separated by $120^\circ$ electrical produce a single rotating air-gap magnetomotive-force vector. For ideal sinusoidal phase currents, the resultant mmf has constant amplitude and rotates at the electrical angular frequency of the currents.

For a motor with $P$ poles and stator frequency $f$, the synchronous speed is:

$$
ns = \frac{120f}{P}\quad\text{r/min}
$$

The electrical and mechanical angles satisfy:

$$
\thetae = \frac{P}{2}\thetam
$$

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 6-10.

Why Slip Is Required

The rotating field induces voltage in the rotor conductors. Since the rotor bars are shorted in a squirrel-cage rotor, that induced voltage drives rotor current. Rotor current in the stator's rotating magnetic field creates torque.

If the rotor reached the same speed as the rotating field, the rotor conductors would no longer see a changing field. No rotor EMF would be induced, rotor current would vanish, and torque would disappear. That is why an induction motor under load runs slightly below synchronous speed.

Slip is usually defined as:

$$
s = \frac{ns-nm}{ns}
$$

where $nm$ is the rotor mechanical speed.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 5-6 and 15-17.

Equivalent-Circuit Intuition

The induction motor equivalent circuit separates:
- stator copper resistance and leakage inductance
- magnetizing branch that produces air-gap flux
- rotor resistance and leakage terms reflected to the stator side
- a slip-dependent rotor resistance term

Only the mutual or magnetizing flux crosses the air gap and contributes to energy conversion. Leakage flux links only its own winding and does not directly produce torque.

Because the machine has an air gap, its magnetizing inductance is much lower than a transformer's. The textbook states that transformer magnetizing current is typically about 1-5% of rated current, while induction-motor magnetizing current is about 30-50% of rated current.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 11-12.

Power Flow and Efficiency

Air-gap power $P{ag}$ is the power transferred from the stator field into the rotor circuit. The textbook splits that power into rotor copper loss and mechanical output:

$$
Pr = sP{ag}
$$

$$
P{mech}=(1-s)P{ag}
$$

So lower slip means less rotor copper loss and more mechanical output power. This is why low-slip operation is usually more efficient.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 20-26.

Speed Control and Operating Regions

The most fundamental way to change induction-motor speed is to change synchronous speed by changing stator frequency. Below base speed, the drive increases voltage roughly in proportion to frequency so the air-gap flux stays near constant. This is constant $V/f$ control, and it gives a constant-torque region.

Above base speed, the terminal voltage has reached its limit, so flux must weaken as frequency rises. The motor then enters a field-weakening range split into a constant-power region and a breakdown-torque-limited region.

Figure: induction motor torque capability divided into constant-torque, constant-power, and breakdown-torque regions. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, page 45, Fig. 3.47.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-3---Alternating-current-motors--Synchronous-mot2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 39-47.

Chapter 8 refines the high-speed picture for vector-controlled induction motors. Under rotor-flux orientation, torque is proportional to $id iq$, so field weakening becomes a constrained current-command problem: choose flux-producing d-axis current and torque-producing q-axis current inside both the current circle and the speed-dependent voltage ellipse. The practical result is more specific than "reduce flux above base speed": the optimal current split depends on speed, voltage limit, current limit, leakage inductance, and torque demand.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 5-16.

d-q Model Connection

Chapter 4 uses the induction motor to motivate reference-frame transformation. In abc variables, mutual inductances between stator and rotor windings vary with rotor angle. Transforming to d-q-n variables makes the mutual-inductance relationships constant in the chosen reference frame and magnetically decouples the d and q axes.

For a squirrel-cage rotor, the rotor voltages are zero because the rotor bars are shorted, but the rotor currents and flux linkages still appear in the d-q dynamic model.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-4---Modeling-of-alternating-current-motors-and-2017Electric-Motor-.pdf, pages 36-40.

Vector-Control Connection

Chapter 5 turns the induction d-q model into field-oriented control. The usual choice is rotor-flux orientation: align the d-axis with rotor flux, make q-axis rotor flux zero, use d-axis stator current as the flux channel, and use q-axis stator current as the torque channel. That makes an induction motor behave more like a separately excited DC motor from the controller's point of view.

Indirect vector control computes synchronous angle from rotor speed plus slip speed. With constant rotor flux, the slip frequency depends on the current ratio:

$$
\omega{sl}=\frac{1}{Tr}\frac{iq^e}{id^e}
$$

where $Tr=Lr/Rr$ is the rotor time constant. This is powerful but sensitive to detuning: rotor resistance changes with temperature, so the slip calculation can misalign the d-q frame from the true rotor flux. Direct vector control estimates rotor flux instead, trading slip-equation sensitivity for flux-estimator quality.

Figure: induction motor vector-control system with speed controller, flux controller, current controller, coordinate transformation, and flux estimator. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, page 32, Fig. 5.22.
Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 6-34; see chapter-05-vector-control.

Motor and Actuator Structure

Basic terminology needed for actuator-design-principles and legged-robot-actuation.

Motor vs Actuator

Motor = the energy-conversion core. In this lecture, it mainly means the electromagnetic machine made from stator + rotor that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.

Actuator = the usable motion unit. For robot joints, this usually includes:
- motor
- reducer / gearbox
- motor driver or controller electronics
- sensing such as encoder or rotor-position sensing
- housing and mechanical support

The lecture prefers keeping these terms separate because the third design principle is about the whole actuator, not just the bare motor.

Gearbox / reducer = a mechanical transmission between the motor shaft and output joint. It usually reduces speed and multiplies torque: a $10:1$ gearbox makes the motor rotate about 10 times for one output rotation and ideally gives about $10\times$ output torque before losses. Real gearboxes also add inertia, friction, backlash/compliance, mass, and efficiency loss.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - transcript.md, motor/actuator terminology discussion; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-239.

Core Motor Parts

Stator = fixed part. It usually contains the iron core/teeth and copper windings. Current in the windings creates the magnetic field.

Rotor = rotating part. In BLDC/SPMSM-style motors, it often carries permanent magnets and back iron, and it connects mechanically to the shaft.

Figure: basic BLDC motor system elements: controller, driver circuit, windings, Hall-effect sensor ICs, and power input. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 2.

For the naming distinction, see bldc-pmsm-spmsm. For how the driver replaces mechanical brushes/commutators, see bldc-electronic-commutation.

Air gap = the small gap between stator and rotor. Magnetic force is transmitted across this gap. A larger gap increases magnetic reluctance, weakens the air-gap flux, and reduces torque for the same excitation.

Winding = copper wire wound on the stator. Turns, wire diameter, series/parallel choice, and wye/delta connection change voltage/current matching, but the ideal torque capability is constrained by geometry/materials. See actuator-design-principles and bldc-electronic-commutation for how current is switched through the phases.

End turn = the part of the winding outside the active slot region that bends around to the next slot. It adds wire length, resistance, heat, mass, and packaging cost without directly producing useful torque. Long, narrow "kimbap" motors reduce the relative penalty of end turns.

Housing = external mechanical structure. It holds the stator, protects the motor, supports bearings and assembly interfaces, and often provides a heat path to the environment.

Outrunner vs Inrunner Packaging

Outrunner and inrunner are rotor-packaging terms, not separate motor-control families.

In an outrunner, the outer bell or casing rotates with the permanent magnets, while the stator windings stay fixed near the center. This gives a larger magnetic radius, so the layout tends toward high low-speed torque, larger diameter, shorter axial length, and higher rotor inertia.

In an inrunner, the central rotor and shaft rotate inside a stationary outer stator and housing. This tends toward a narrower, longer package with lower rotor inertia, higher maximum RPM, and a more direct heat path from stator to housing.

For drone propulsion, this packaging distinction matters because direct-drive multirotors usually want large, low-RPM propellers and therefore favor outrunners. Inrunners fit better when the load is a ducted fan, small high-speed propeller, geared propulsion path, or sealed/narrow package.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner BLDC Motor vs Inner Rotor BLDC Motor.md, mechanical-structure comparison; sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, structural differences and applicable scenarios.

Textbook Drive-System View

The Electric Motor Control textbook frames the motor as one part of a larger electromechanical drive system: power source, power electronic converter, digital controller, voltage/current/position sensing, motor, and mechanical load. That matches the lecture convention that a robot actuator is not "just a motor"; the usable joint behavior depends on electronics, sensing, thermal limits, transmission, and load dynamics.

Figure: motor-drive-system block diagram tying source, converter, controller, sensors, motor, and load together. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, page 35, Fig. 1.32.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-1---Fundamentals-of-electric-motors2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 3 and 35.

Design Parameters from Slides

The slide appendix groups the hardware knobs as:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer/gearbox type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

This grouping is useful because it maps directly onto the lecture's three principles: geometry sets the electromagnetic capability, reducer choice sets the actuator-level torque/speed/inertia trade-off, and winding is mainly driver matching.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

Motor Driver

The motor driver is the power electronics/control unit that supplies timed voltage and current to the motor phases.

For a three-phase motor, the driver must:
- regulate voltage/current
- switch phase currents with the correct timing
- use rotor position or commutation logic to produce torque in the desired direction

Lecture 3B makes this more concrete: a DC bus is switched by a three-phase inverter, SVPWM turns d-q voltage commands into timed switch states, current sensing closes the feedback loop, and current control makes the q-axis current that creates torque. This is also why winding is a driver-matching problem. A low-turn, thick-wire or delta/parallel configuration is suitable for high-current drivers; a high-turn, thin-wire or wye/series configuration is suitable for high-voltage drivers.

BLDC/SPMSM-style motors need rotor-position information for commutation. This can come from Hall effect sensors, encoders, or sensorless estimation.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 8 and 12-18.

Outrunner vs Inrunner BLDC Motors

Outrunner and inrunner are mechanical layout terms for brushless permanent-magnet motors. They describe where the rotor sits and which housing part rotates; they do not, by themselves, decide whether a source should be read as BLDC, PMSM, SPMSM, or IPMSM language.

In this wiki, read the terms as:
- Outrunner / outer-rotor motor: the outer bell or casing rotates. Permanent magnets are mounted on the inside of that rotating bell, and the wound stator is fixed near the center.
- Inrunner / inner-rotor motor: the central rotor and shaft rotate. The stator and outer housing stay fixed around the rotor.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner BLDC Motor vs Inner Rotor BLDC Motor.md, mechanical-structure comparison; sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, structural-differences section.

> WARNING
> The drone clipping's early I.What are Drone Outrunner and Inrunner Brushless Motors? subsections appear to swap the detailed structure descriptions for outrunner and inrunner motors. Its opening summary and later structural section use the conventional mapping. This page follows the conventional mapping above.
> Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, Section I and Section II.

headingPath: Why the Layout Changes the Motor Personality
chunks.text:
Why the Layout Changes the Motor Personality

The useful intuition is torque radius, inertia, and heat path.

| Dimension | Outrunner | Inrunner |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Rotating part | Outer bell/can rotates with the magnets | Inner rotor/shaft rotates |
| Shape tendency | Larger diameter, shorter axial length | Smaller diameter, longer axial length |
| Torque tendency | Higher low-speed torque for a given class because the magnetic force acts at a larger radius | Lower direct-drive torque for the same diameter class, but good high-speed power density |
| Speed tendency | Lower maximum RPM, often lower $Kv$ product variants | Higher maximum RPM, often higher $Kv$ product variants |
| Inertia tendency | More rotating mass at larger radius, so higher rotor inertia | Rotating mass closer to shaft, so lower rotor inertia |
| Heat and structure | Rotating bell can self-ventilate, but stator heat may have a longer path out | Stationary housing can be tied to heatsinks, ducts, or sealed structures more directly |
| Mechanical exposure | Rotating outer bell and magnets are exposed to impact/deformation risk | Fixed housing better protects the rotor in high-speed or sealed packages |

The outrunner torque advantage is not magic. It is the same radius intuition used elsewhere in actuator design: for tangential air-gap force $Ft$, torque scales as

$$
\tau = Ft r
$$

headingPath: Why the Layout Changes the Motor Personality
chunks.text: A larger effective magnetic radius gives more torque for the same tangential force, while putting mass farther from the shaft also increases rotor inertia. For real selection, this geometry has to be checked with motor-constant, analytical-torque-constant-spmsm, winding, thermal limit, bearing speed, ESC current, battery voltage, and propeller or load choice.

Drone Selection Rule

Most direct-drive multirotor drones use outrunners because the propeller wants the same thing the motor layout is good at: relatively large diameter, lower RPM, high low-speed torque, and fast thrust changes without a gearbox. This is why camera drones, FPV quads, cinelifters, inspection drones, agricultural drones, and many long-endurance multirotors commonly use outer-rotor motors.

Inrunners make sense when the propulsor or airframe is different:
- EDF / ducted fan: small impellers need very high RPM, which fits an inrunner better.
- High-speed fixed-wing or small-prop layouts: narrow packaging and high RPM can matter more than direct-drive low-speed torque.
- Geared large-prop systems: a gearbox or belt can convert inrunner speed into propeller torque when packaging demands a narrow motor.
- Sealed or integrated thermal structures: the stationary outer housing can be easier to connect to ducts, heatsinks, or protected shells.

So the drone question is not "which motor type is better?" It is "what propulsor and package am I driving?" For an open multirotor propeller, default to outrunner. For ducted fans, narrow fuselages, very high-speed impellers, or sealed thermal integration, evaluate inrunner.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, applicable-scenarios section.

headingPath: Vendor Example: Same Diameter Does Not Mean Same Torque
chunks.text:
Vendor Example: Same Diameter Does Not Mean Same Torque

The Doncen clipping compares one outer-rotor MX33 motor with one inner-rotor BL42 motor at 24 V and similar no-load speed. In that specific product comparison, the outer-rotor example is shorter and has much higher listed torque:

| Parameter | MX33 outer rotor | BL4259 inner rotor |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| No-load speed | 4800 rpm | 5000 rpm |
| Rated speed | 3400 rpm | 4031 rpm |
| Rated torque | 0.18 N m | 0.035 N m |
| Stall torque | 0.40 N m | 0.14 N m |

Treat this as an example of the layout trend, not a universal law. Product family, winding, magnet grade, cooling, rating method, and thermal limit can all change the comparison.

Source: sources/web-clippings/Outrunner BLDC Motor vs Inner Rotor BLDC Motor.md, MX33 vs BL42 comparison.

What Not to Infer
- Do not infer control waveform from outrunner/inrunner. Either layout can be sold under casual BLDC language and may be driven by six-step, sinusoidal, or FOC electronics depending on the motor and ESC.
- Do not equate low $Kv$ with outrunner as a topology law. $Kv$ also changes with turns, winding connection, magnet flux, and voltage/current matching; see back-emf-and-winding-turns and motor-winding-configuration.
- Do not choose inrunner only because "high speed is better." Propeller efficiency often prefers larger diameter and lower RPM, which is why direct-drive multirotors favor outrunners.
- Do not choose outrunner only because "more torque is better." High rotor inertia, exposed rotating bells, bearing loads, dynamic balance, and sustained thermal operation can dominate in high-speed or enclosed systems.

BLDC Electronic Commutation

Electronic commutation means switching stator winding currents with electronics instead of using mechanical brushes and a commutator.

Brush-Type DC Motor vs BLDC

In a brush-type DC motor:
- brushes make sliding electrical contact
- a mechanical commutator switches which rotor coils receive current
- brush wear, dust, friction, electrical noise, and maintenance are real costs

In a BLDC motor:
- the windings are typically on the stator
- the rotor usually carries permanent magnets
- a motor driver switches phase currents electronically
- rotor position sensing or estimation tells the driver when to switch

Figure: BLDC rotor magnets and sensor IC arrangement. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 1.

Honeywell describes BLDC motors as brush-type motors "turned inside out": power goes to stationary windings, while the permanent-magnet rotor rotates.

Six-Step Intuition

In a common three-phase six-step BLDC drive:
- two phases are energized at a time
- one phase is left off/floating
- the active phase pair creates a stator magnetic field
- the driver changes the active pair as the rotor moves, using rotor-position feedback or estimation
- the rotor magnets keep moving toward the next stator field orientation

Beginner analogy: the controller keeps creating the next magnetic "target" slightly ahead of the rotor, so the rotor keeps chasing it.

What the Driver Needs

The motor driver needs rotor position or rotor sector information to energize the correct winding. It can get that from:
- Hall effect sensors
- encoders
- sensorless estimation from electrical signals

Figure: simplified three-phase BLDC driver circuit using six power transistors and Hall-effect sensor IC feedback. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 4.

This is the control-facing reason why motor-actuator-structure says BLDC/SPMSM-style motors need a driver and commutation logic. For the broader distinction between six-step, trapezoidal, sinusoidal, and FOC language, see pmsm-control-waveform-language.

Relation to Winding

Electronic commutation determines when current goes through each phase. Winding design determines the motor's voltage/current matching and electromagnetic constraints. See bldc-pmsm-spmsm for the naming distinction between BLDC/PMSM/SPMSM.
