Winding Function and Winding Factor

The winding function is a compact way to turn a physical slot/conductor layout into a flux-linkage calculation. Lecture 5 uses it to move from conductor-level motional EMF to the phase torque result for an SPMSM.

Conductor Density

The conductor density function $n(\phi)$ counts conductors per angular interval. If $d\phi$ is a small angular slice at angular position $\phi$, then:

$$
\text{conductors in } d\phi = n(\phi)d\phi
$$

In the idealized slot model, $n(\phi)$ can contain impulse-like positive and negative conductor groups. This is inconvenient for flux linkage, so the lecture introduces the winding function $N(\phi)$ as the more useful integrated representation.

Figure: conductor density and winding function for the simplified SPMSM cross-section. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Flux-Linkage Overlap

For the simplified radial air-gap model, the phase flux linkage can be written as an overlap integral:

$$
\lambda(\theta)
=
R l \int0^{2\pi}
Br(\phi-\theta)N(\phi)\,d\phi
$$

Only the radial component $Br$ is kept because the simplified model is counting flux crossing the air-gap surface and linking axial conductors. As the rotor moves, the magnet-field waveform shifts relative to the winding function. Larger aligned overlap gives larger flux linkage.

Figure: radial magnet field waveform shifted against the winding function. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 29.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 281-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 29-30.

Winding Factor

The winding factor $kw$ is the ratio between the useful fundamental EMF/torque contribution of a real winding and the ideal concentrated full-pitch winding. A winding factor of 1 means the conductor EMF contributions add without cancellation in the modeled fundamental. A lower winding factor means some of the conductor contributions partially cancel.

Lecture 5 first derives the ideal case, then inserts $kw$ into the final torque-constant formula:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 335-353 and 382-386; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 20 and 32.

headingPath: Pole Count Is Not a Free Torque Multiplier
chunks.text:
Pole Count Is Not a Free Torque Multiplier

The lecture warns against saying "more pole pairs directly increase torque constant." In the ideal formula, pole-pair count drops out when the total effective conductor count and linkage are fixed. More poles can still improve a real motor by allowing shorter end turns, better practical winding choices, thinner magnetic paths, and lower mass, but that is a packaging and winding-realization advantage rather than a direct independent multiplier in the ideal $Kt$ formula.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 388-424.

Related
- analytical-torque-constant-spmsm
- motional-emf-and-flux-linkage
- motor-winding-configuration
- electrical-angle

Jacobian Inverse Kinematics

Jacobian inverse kinematics uses the local velocity model

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q}
$$

to choose joint motion that moves the end effector toward a target.

If $J$ is square and full rank, the resolved-rate form is

$$
\dot{q}=J^{-1}(q)\dot{x}.
$$

For nonsquare or rank-deficient cases, implementations usually use the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse:

$$
\dot{q}=J^+(q)\dot{x}.
$$

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, Jacobian Inversion Method and pseudo-inverse sections.

Velocity-Controlled Actuators

For joints that accept velocity commands:

1. compute current end-effector pose from forward kinematics
2. compute task error from current pose to goal pose
3. convert the task error into a desired task velocity, often with proportional gain
4. evaluate $J(q)$ at the current joint positions
5. compute $\dot{q}=J^+\dot{x}$
6. command the joint velocities, then repeat until the task error is small

The Jacobian must be recomputed as the robot moves because it depends on $q$.

Position-Controlled Actuators

For joints that accept position commands, use the same linearization as a small displacement step:

$$
\Delta x\approx J(q)\Delta q.
$$

Then

$$
\Delta q=J^+(q)\Delta x.
$$

This is an iterative method, not a one-shot global inverse. The displacement step must be kept small enough that the local Jacobian approximation remains useful.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, position-controlled actuator method section.

Singularities And Limits

Near singular configurations, a finite task-space motion can require very large joint velocities or position increments. The pseudo-inverse helps with nonsquare matrices and rank loss, but it does not remove actuator limits, joint limits, collision risk, or numerical sensitivity.

Practical controllers should still limit joint velocity, limit step size, monitor singular values or manipulability, and define what happens when the requested end-effector motion is not achievable.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, singularity and pseudo-inverse discussion; sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 5 and 7.

Related
- robot-jacobian
- jacobian-singularities
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- space-and-body-jacobians

Jacobian Singularities

A Jacobian singularity is a configuration where the Jacobian loses rank. Physically, some end-effector velocity directions cannot be produced by any finite joint velocity command.

For a square Jacobian, singularity is often checked by

$$
\det J(q)=0.
$$

For a nonsquare Jacobian, use rank or singular values instead of determinant.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 5; sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, Problems with Jacobian Method section.

Two-Link Arm

For the two-link planar arm in robot-jacobian, the determinant simplifies to a term proportional to

$$
L1L2\sin\theta2.
$$

Assuming nonzero link lengths, the arm is singular when

$$
\sin\theta2=0,
$$

so $\theta2=0$ or $\theta2=\pi$ in the usual planar two-link convention. These are the fully stretched or fully folded collinear postures.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 5; sources/web-clippings/craig-robotics-chapter-05-jacobian.md, Singularities section.

headingPath: Why Near-Singular Can Be Worse In Control
chunks.text:
Why Near-Singular Can Be Worse In Control

At the exact singularity, the lost direction is obvious: the linearized map cannot produce motion there. Near the singularity, the controller may still try to invert the Jacobian. A modest desired end-effector motion can then require very large joint velocities or very large joint-position increments.

That is the practical control risk: actuator velocity limits, torque limits, and numerical noise all appear before the math reaches an exact determinant of zero.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, singularity discussion near Jacobian inversion.

Design Reading

Singularities are not only software problems. A manipulator that must produce force or velocity in a specific direction should be checked over the task workspace. A flattened manipulability ellipsoid is an early warning that useful motion directions are disappearing.

Related
- robot-jacobian
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- jacobian-inverse-kinematics
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping

Jacobian Transpose Force Mapping

The Jacobian transpose maps an end-effector wrench into the joint torques needed to hold or resist it:

$$
\tau=J^T(q)F.
$$

The clean derivation is virtual work or power consistency. Since

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q},
$$

the task-space power and joint-space power must match:

$$
F^T\dot{x}=\tau^T\dot{q}.
$$

Substituting $\dot{x}=J\dot{q}$ and requiring the equality for any $\dot{q}$ gives $\tau=J^TF$.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 8.

Frame And Sign Discipline

The formula is only meaningful when $J$, $F$, and $\tau$ use compatible coordinates and sign conventions. If $J$ maps joint rates to a task velocity expressed in a particular frame, then the force or wrench must be expressed in the dual coordinates for that same task representation. Changing the Jacobian frame without changing the wrench frame breaks the power equality.

This is one reason space and body Jacobians need explicit frame labels.

Force Capability

Given joint-torque limits, the same equation can be read backward. If $J^T$ is invertible, the end-effector force that can be counteracted is

$$
F=(J^T(q))^{-1}\tau.
$$

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 9.

Figure: joint torque limits map through $(J^T)^{-1}$ into a configuration-dependent end-effector force set. Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 9.

Hardware Use

Lecture 4 uses the same relation for payload torque sizing. For a chosen payload force and manipulator configuration, $J^TF$ tells the designer which joint torques are required before selecting actuator mass, rated torque, peak torque, and reflected inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 10.

In transparent leg or arm hardware, the relation also runs in the proprioceptive direction: if motor current estimates joint torque accurately and the transmission is low-friction, the robot can estimate contact force through the Jacobian. The estimate is only as good as the torque model, friction compensation, and kinematic model.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II and force-control model discussion.

Related
- robot-jacobian
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- transmission-transparency
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- space-and-body-jacobians

Manipulability Ellipsoid

The manipulability ellipsoid visualizes how a bounded set of joint velocities maps into end-effector velocities through the Jacobian.

If joint velocities satisfy

$$
\dot{q}^T\dot{q}\le 1,
$$

then the possible end-effector velocities are

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q}.
$$

For a two-dimensional task, the unit disk in joint-velocity space becomes an ellipse in end-effector-velocity space. For a spatial task, the same idea becomes an ellipsoid.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 7.

Figure: a unit disk of joint velocities maps through $J(q)$ into a configuration-dependent end-effector velocity ellipsoid. Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 7.

How To Read It

Long axes mean directions where the end effector can move quickly for a small joint-rate effort. Short axes mean directions where the same joint-rate effort produces little motion.

A very flat ellipsoid means the manipulator is close to a singular configuration. In that posture, some directions are almost unavailable, and moving in those directions requires large joint velocities.

Velocity Limits Versus Torque Limits

The velocity ellipsoid starts from a joint-velocity limit. A related force-capability plot starts from joint-torque limits and uses the reciprocal force relation

$$
\tau=J^T(q)F.
$$

When $J^T$ is invertible, allowable joint torques can be mapped into the end-effector forces the robot can counteract. A posture that is good for velocity in one direction may be weak for force in a related direction, so both views matter during actuator and task-workspace design.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 8-9.

Related
- robot-jacobian
- jacobian-singularities
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping
- torque-mass-inertia-selection

Robot Jacobian

The robot Jacobian is the local velocity map from joint motion to end-effector motion:

$$
\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q}
$$

Here $q$ is the joint-position vector, $\dot{q}$ is the joint-velocity vector, and $\dot{x}$ is the end-effector velocity or twist in the chosen task coordinates. The Jacobian is configuration dependent: the same joint velocity can move the end effector in different directions depending on the current posture.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 2; sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, opening Jacobian definition.

Columns As Motion Directions

If the Jacobian is written as columns,

$$
J(q)=
\begin{bmatrix}
J1(q) & J2(q) & \cdots & Jn(q)
\end{bmatrix},
$$

then the end-effector velocity is a linear combination of those columns:

$$
\dot{x}=J1(q)\dot{q}1+J2(q)\dot{q}2+\cdots+Jn(q)\dot{q}n.
$$

Each column is the instantaneous end-effector velocity produced by unit velocity at one joint while the other joint velocities are zero. This column view is the most useful way to reason about reachable velocity directions and singular configurations.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 4-5; sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, column interpretation section.

Two-Link Planar Example

For a two-link planar arm,

$$
\begin{aligned}
x1 &= L1\cos\theta1+L2\cos(\theta1+\theta2) \\
x2 &= L1\sin\theta1+L2\sin(\theta1+\theta2).
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating the forward kinematics gives

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{x}1 \\
\dot{x}2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-L1\sin\theta1-L2\sin(\theta1+\theta2) & -L2\sin(\theta1+\theta2) \\
L1\cos\theta1+L2\cos(\theta1+\theta2) & L2\cos(\theta1+\theta2)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{\theta}1 \\
\dot{\theta}2
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 3-4; sources/web-clippings/craig-robotics-chapter-05-jacobian.md, Jacobian section.

Figure: a square of allowable joint velocities maps through the Jacobian into a parallelogram of end-effector velocities. Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 6.

Linear And Angular Parts

For a spatial manipulator, the task velocity usually has six components:

$$
V =
\begin{bmatrix}
v \\
\omega
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
Jv(q) \\
J\omega(q)
\end{bmatrix}
\dot{q}.
$$

The upper block $Jv$ maps joint rates to linear velocity. The lower block $J\omega$ maps joint rates to angular velocity. A common geometric construction uses forward kinematics to express every joint axis in the base frame, then stacks the linear and angular contributions in joint order.

Source: sources/web-clippings/ros-robotics-jacobian.md, Jv and Jw derivation sections.

Why It Matters For Hardware

The Jacobian is not just a kinematics formula. It is the bridge between:
- joint-rate limits and end-effector velocity capability, through $\dot{x}=J\dot{q}$
- joint torque and end-effector force, through $\tau=J^T F$
- near-singular postures and loss of useful motion directions
- local inverse-kinematics steps, through $J^{-1}$ or $J^+$

This is why the same concept appears in actuator sizing, proprioceptive force control, manipulator workspace analysis, and motion-control algorithms.

Related
- jacobian-singularities
- manipulability-ellipsoid
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping
- space-and-body-jacobians
- jacobian-inverse-kinematics
- torque-mass-inertia-selection

Space And Body Jacobians

For spatial robots, the Jacobian maps joint rates to a six-dimensional twist. The twist must be expressed in a frame. Two common choices are:
- space Jacobian $Js(q)$: end-effector twist expressed in the fixed space frame
- body Jacobian $Jb(q)$: end-effector twist expressed in the end-effector body frame

The two Jacobians describe the same physical motion, but in different coordinates.

Product-Of-Exponentials Setup

With product-of-exponentials forward kinematics,

$$
T(q)=e^{[S1]\theta1}e^{[S2]\theta2}\cdots e^{[Sn]\thetan}M,
$$

the space-frame twist satisfies

$$
Vs=Js(q)\dot{q}.
$$

The first space-Jacobian column is the first space screw:

$$
J{s1}=S1.
$$

For $i\ge 2$,

$$
J{si}=\operatorname{Ad}{e^{[S1]\theta1}\cdots e^{[S{i-1}]\theta{i-1}}}(Si).
$$

This says: move the screw axis of joint $i$ into the current fixed-frame coordinates using the transforms caused by earlier joints.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 10-12.

Body Jacobian

The same forward kinematics can also be written with body screw axes:

$$
T(q)=Me^{[B1]\theta1}\cdots e^{[Bn]\thetan}.
$$

The body-frame twist satisfies

$$
Vb=Jb(q)\dot{q}.
$$

The last body-Jacobian column is

$$
J{bn}=Bn.
$$

For $i<n$,

$$
J{bi}=\operatorname{Ad}{e^{-[Bn]\thetan}\cdots e^{-[B{i+1}]\theta{i+1}}}(Bi).
$$

The body Jacobian is often convenient when the task error or tool velocity is naturally expressed in the end-effector frame.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 20-22.

Relationship

Let $T{sb}$ be the end-effector frame pose relative to the space frame, and let $T{bs}=T{sb}^{-1}$. The twists relate through the adjoint map:

$$
Vs=\operatorname{Ad}{T{sb}}Vb.
$$

Therefore,

$$
Jb(q)=\operatorname{Ad}{T{bs}}Js(q),
$$

and

$$
Js(q)=\operatorname{Ad}{T{sb}}Jb(q).
$$

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 23; sources/web-clippings/craig-robotics-chapter-05-jacobian.md, Changing a Jacobian's frame of reference section.

Practical Rule

Always name the Jacobian frame in code, notes, and derivations. A correct $J^TF$ calculation, resolved-rate IK step, or wrench limit plot depends on pairing a Jacobian with a velocity or wrench in matching coordinates.

Related
- robot-jacobian
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping
- jacobian-inverse-kinematics
- manipulability-ellipsoid

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors

Hall effect sensors detect magnetic field and turn rotor magnetic position into electrical signals for the motor controller/driver.

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
Why BLDC Motors Need Position Information
chunks.text:
Why BLDC Motors Need Position Information

A BLDC driver must know which stator phases to energize. If it switches too early or too late, torque ripple, inefficient operation, poor startup, or incorrect commutation can result.

Hall sensors provide a simple way to answer:

> Where is the permanent-magnet rotor right now?

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Hall Effect Basics

The Hall effect: when a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor is exposed to a magnetic field, a voltage appears perpendicular to the current and field direction.

In practical BLDC sensor ICs, that effect is packaged as a digital position sensor. When a rotor magnet pole passes near the sensor, the sensor output changes state.

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
How It Works in a BLDC Motor
chunks.text:
How It Works in a BLDC Motor

Typical flow:

1. Permanent magnets on the rotor pass near Hall sensor ICs.
2. Sensor outputs change based on magnetic polarity/position.
3. The logic/controller interprets the sensor pattern as rotor sector.
4. The driver switches the power transistors.
5. Current flows through the correct stator windings.
6. The stator field pulls the rotor forward.

Figure: three Hall-effect sensor outputs over electrical angle in a BLDC motor example. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 3.

Honeywell's application note describes three Hall-effect sensor IC outputs being used as shaft-position encoder signals for a logic circuit and a three-phase driver.

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Sensor Placement

The Hall sensor ICs are usually mounted on the stationary side of the motor assembly, such as the stator, housing, or a fixed PCB near the shaft. They are not normally embedded in the rotating rotor; the rotor or rotor-shaft magnet moves past the fixed sensors.

Hall sensors can be placed:
- inside the motor
- at the end of the motor shaft
- around a ring magnet attached to the rotor shaft

Figure: typical sensor IC locations: inside the motor, at the shaft end, or around a rotor-shaft ring magnet. Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf, Fig. 5.

Exact angular spacing depends on pole count, sensor type, and commutation scheme. Beginner sources often describe three Hall sensors spaced 120 electrical degrees apart; Honeywell's example discusses a specific eight-pole motor/sensor arrangement where three sensor ICs are placed 30 electrical degrees apart.

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Advantages
- simple rotor-sector feedback
- reliable commutation
- speed and direction sensing
- solid-state, no mechanical wear
- useful startup behavior compared with purely sensorless commutation

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Limitations
- added sensor/wiring/package complexity
- magnetic interference sensitivity
- temperature effects
- finite response time at high speed
- lower position resolution than high-resolution encoders

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Contrast With Vector-Control Feedback

Hall sensors are usually enough for six-step BLDC commutation because they identify rotor sectors. High-performance PMSM vector control needs a finer electrical angle so the controller can align d-q axes with rotor flux. Chapter 9 distinguishes that feedback layer from Hall-sector commutation:
- resolvers provide robust absolute rotor position and are useful for PMSM startup
- incremental encoders provide relative A/B/Z pulse information, so PMSM startup also needs index/homing or initial-position detection
- sensorless control removes the position sensor but depends on motor model, back EMF, saliency, or injected signals

Figure: induction motor and PMSM drive systems using encoder or resolver position feedback. Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, page 10, Fig. 9.11.

The practical comparison is not "sensor" versus "sensorless" in general. It is a control-performance trade: sensored vector control gives the best low-speed/startup behavior, while model-based sensorless control is weakest where back EMF is small. The source-level route is chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

chunks.pageTitle: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Hall Effect Position Sensing in BLDC Motors

Robot Actuator Reducer Selection

Reducer selection chooses the transmission topology after the actuator's torque, mass, and inertia targets are roughly known.

Lecture 4 focuses on robot actuator reducers:

Figure: common robot actuator reducer types and reducer-level primary/secondary requirements. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 15.
- planetary drive
- cycloidal drive
- harmonic drive

Reducer Requirements

Primary reducer requirements:
- Torque density: maximum transmissible torque per reducer mass
- Efficiency: output power divided by input power

Lecture 4 treats these as fundamentally traded off.

Secondary reducer requirements:
- backlash
- manufacturability
- packaging
- wiring path
- cost and precision

Backlash matters more in robot positioning than in many vehicle transmissions, but the lecture treats it as secondary because manufacturing precision, preload, thermal expansion, and wear dominate it.

headingPath: Why Not Just Use Serial Spur Gears?
chunks.text:
Why Not Just Use Serial Spur Gears?

A chain of external spur gears is simple, but it is poor for compact robot joints:
- input and output are not coaxial by default
- each gear mesh creates radial loads on shafts and bearings
- bearing radial load creates friction
- external gear contact is generally less efficient than internal gear contact
- backlash accumulates across stages
- packaging and wiring become awkward

Planetary, cycloidal, and harmonic reducers are attractive because they package reduction coaxially and can distribute load around the axis.

Single-Stage Planetary Drive

Pros:
- high efficiency
- easy to design
- compact and familiar

Cons:
- limited reduction ratio, typically below about $10:1$
- limited torque density compared with high-contact-ratio reducers
- sun gear cannot be made arbitrarily small because of tooth count, undercut, and packaging/wiring limits

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, reducer-type notes around planetary drive selection; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 15.

Two-Stage Planetary Drive

Pros:
- high efficiency compared with 3K compound planetary designs

Cons:
- still limited in practical reduction range
- many parts
- low torque density because multiple stages and bearings add mass

The output-side stage dominates mass because it carries the larger torque.

3K Compound Planetary Drive

Pros:
- wide reduction-ratio range
- higher torque density than a two-stage planetary drive at comparable reduction

Cons:
- lower efficiency than a two-stage planetary drive at similar design quality
- sensitive to detailed gear-tooth efficiency and backlash requirements

The lecture notes that high-quality designs, such as bilateral-drive-gear-style reducers, can be highly backdrivable, but measured performance depends strongly on backlash tolerance and manufacturing quality.

Cycloidal Drive

Pros:
- high ideal efficiency if power is transmitted through rolling contacts
- potentially good torque density

Cons:
- requires very high manufacturing precision
- sensitive to tolerances and overconstraint
- difficult to build well in a lab without specialized manufacturing experience

The lecture's practical warning is strong: do not casually decide to make a cycloidal drive unless the team has the required machining and design capability.

Harmonic Drive

Pros:
- very high torque density
- low backlash is possible
- many teeth share load

Cons:
- low efficiency compared with QDD-friendly reducers
- difficult flexspline design and manufacturing
- friction/preload can hurt reinforcement-learning or proprioceptive-control style robots

Harmonic drives were excellent for CNC-style position-controlled robot arms because high-gain position control could treat friction as a disturbance. Lecture 4 argues they fit less naturally with QDD-style robots that try to minimize friction and infer torque from motor current.

Related
- actuator-requirement-selection
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- backdrivability
- transmission-transparency
- legged-robot-actuation

Backdrivability

Backdrivability is the ability to drive an actuator backward from the output side. In robot actuator discussions, the word is useful but ambiguous.

Lecture 4 explicitly separates three meanings:

Figure: Lecture 4 separates backdrivability into backward efficiency, Coulomb friction/reverse-drive starting torque, and reflected inertia. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Meaning 1: Backward Efficiency

Forward efficiency measures how much input power becomes output power. Backward efficiency measures how well output-side mechanical power transmits back toward the motor.

These can differ. The lecture uses screw/bottle-cap intuition: turning the cap can move it axially, but pushing axially may not spin it backward. A mechanism can have nonzero forward efficiency and nearly zero backward efficiency.

Meaning 2: Reverse-Drive Starting Torque

Reverse-drive starting torque is the output torque needed to start motion from rest. This is close to a Coulomb/static-friction idea: the output shaft may stay stuck until the applied external torque exceeds the static-friction threshold.

It depends on preload, tooth contact, bearings, seals, lubrication, and assembly. It is not the same as backward efficiency under motion, although both are friction-related.

For QDD/proprioceptive control, this meaning is often the most important: if friction torque is small enough, the motor current can be used as a good proxy for output torque.

Meaning 3: Reflected Inertia

Sometimes people include output-reflected rotor inertia in "backdrivability." Lecture 4 recommends keeping it separate.

Reflected inertia is:

$$
J{out}=N^2Jr
$$

It affects impact response and dynamic motion, but it is not friction. This wiki uses backdrivability mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior and discusses inertia separately.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239 and 317-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

QDD Interpretation

The lecture clarifies a common misconception. QDD does not fundamentally mean "the reduction ratio must be small." It means the actuator should preserve the control idea of direct drive: output torque can be inferred without a dedicated torque sensor because friction and unmodeled transmission effects are small.

Low reduction ratio is a common way to achieve that, but not the only theoretical way. A high-ratio reducer with excellent low-friction design could still behave like a QDD actuator. In practice, high ratio makes this harder, so designers often impose a realistic reduction-ratio limit.

Related
- coulomb-static-friction
- transmission-transparency
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- robot-actuator-reducer-selection
- legged-robot-actuation
- actuator-performance-metrics

Coulomb and Static Friction

In backdrivability discussions, the Coulomb/static-friction idea means that a joint can resist motion even before inertia becomes the main issue. A small external torque may not move the output shaft at all; the joint only starts moving after the applied torque exceeds a friction threshold.

For a robot joint pushed from the output side:
- a weak push produces no motion
- a stronger push may still be held by static friction
- above a threshold, the joint breaks free and starts moving
- after motion begins, friction continues to oppose the direction of travel

That threshold is closely related to reverse-drive starting torque or breakaway torque.

Static Friction

Static friction is the friction available while the joint is still at rest. It does not have one fixed direction by itself; it reacts against the attempted motion up to a maximum value.

A simple output-side model is:

$$
|\tau{\text{external}}| < \tau{\text{static,max}}
$$

If this is true, the joint can remain stuck. To start reverse-driving the joint:

$$
|\tau{\text{external}}| > \tau{\text{static,max}}
$$

This is why a reducer can feel "sticky": nothing happens at first, then it suddenly starts moving once the static-friction threshold is exceeded.

Coulomb Friction

Coulomb friction is the simple model often used after sliding or rotation has begun. It treats friction torque as roughly constant in magnitude and opposite the direction of motion:

$$
\tau{\text{friction}} \approx -\tauc \, \operatorname{sign}(\dot{q})
$$

Here $q$ is joint position, $\dot{q}$ is joint velocity, and $\operatorname{sign}(\dot{q})$ keeps only the direction of the velocity:

$$
\operatorname{sign}(\dot{q}) =
\begin{cases}
+1 & \dot{q} > 0 \\
0 & \dot{q} = 0 \\
-1 & \dot{q} < 0
\end{cases}
$$

So if the joint moves in the positive direction, Coulomb friction acts in the negative direction. If the joint moves in the negative direction, Coulomb friction acts in the positive direction. The important simplification is that the friction magnitude is about $\tauc$; it is not mainly proportional to speed.

headingPath: Not Viscous Friction, Not Reflected Inertia
chunks.text:
Not Viscous Friction, Not Reflected Inertia

Coulomb/static friction can feel similar to other forms of passive impedance, but the causes differ:
- Static friction: the joint will not start moving until a threshold torque is exceeded.
- Coulomb friction: once moving, the joint continues to feel a roughly constant opposing torque.
- Viscous friction: the opposing torque grows with speed, often modeled as $\tau{\text{viscous}}=-b\dot{q}$.
- Reflected inertia: the joint can move, but it resists acceleration because motor or transmission inertia is reflected to the output.

This distinction matters because backdrivability is often used loosely. For actuator design, it is clearer to separate friction/reverse-drive behavior from reflected inertia.

Why It Matters for QDD

In QDD and proprioceptive force control, low Coulomb/static friction helps motor current remain a useful proxy for output torque. If internal friction is large, part of the motor torque is spent overcoming the reducer instead of producing external contact force. The controller then sees a less direct relationship between motor current and output torque.

Lecture 4 uses backdrivability terminology to separate backward efficiency, Coulomb-friction/reverse-drive starting torque, and reflected inertia. In that framing, the Coulomb/static-friction idea explains why a joint may need a noticeable output torque before it starts moving backward at all.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239 and 317-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Web References
- Engineering LibreTexts, "Dry Friction," https://eng.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/MechanicalEngineering/EngineeringStatics%3AOpenandInteractive%28BakerandHaynes%29/09%3AFriction/9.01%3AFriction/9.1.01%3ADryFriction, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.
- "An improved Coulomb friction model for servo mechanisms," https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12788085/, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.
- Springs Fastener, "Breakaway Torque," https://www.springsfastener.com/resources/glossary/breakaway-torque, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.
- maxon Support, "Gears: Self-locking or back-drivability," https://support.maxongroup.com/hc/en-us/articles/5881942527132-Gears-Self-locking-or-back-drivability, accessed 2026-05-28, found via Explore web search.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control

Transmission transparency is the degree to which forces generated by the actuator are faithfully transmitted to the end effector, and external forces at the end effector are faithfully "felt" by the actuator.

It is closely related to backdrivability and low passive impedance.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
What Reduces Transparency

Transmission transparency is degraded by:
- reflected rotor inertia
- gear-train inertia
- friction
- damping
- backlash and compliance
- non-collocated force sensing with unmodeled modes

For legged-robot-actuation, these effects are especially damaging because ground contact is frequent, fast, and impulsive.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
Why It Matters

If the actuator/transmission has low enough mechanical impedance, motor torque can approximate end-effector force through Jacobian transpose force mapping:

$$\tau \approx J^T F{ext} + fc(q,\tau)$$

where $fc$ captures torque-dependent Coulomb friction. This makes proprioceptive force control possible: the robot estimates contact force from internal signals such as motor current and encoder position, without requiring a force sensor at the foot.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, page 8; sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, Sections I-II and force-control model discussion.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
Seok et al. 2012 Result

Seok et al. designed the MIT Cheetah front leg to maximize transparency through:
- large-radius, high-torque motors
- low reduction ratio, 1:5.8
- one-stage planetary gearing
- motors and gear trains located at the hip
- lightweight distal leg structure
- friction characterized and compensated experimentally

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, pages 3-4, MIT Cheetah leg design.

The paper then checks whether that hardware makes motor-current force estimation usable:

Figure: force-displacement calibration and impact force-control results comparing motor-current force estimates to force-sensor measurements. Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 5, Fig. 8.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
Fig. 8 is the paper's direct test of whether motor current can stand in for a foot force sensor. The force sensor is used as the reference measurement; the proposed control signal is the proprioceptive estimate built from motor current, encoder position, the leg Jacobian, and friction compensation.
- Fig. 8(a) plots vertical force against leg displacement while the leg is compressed and released. The loop is hysteresis: at the same displacement, the measured force differs depending on motion direction. In this experiment the loop is interpreted mainly as energy dissipated by transmission friction, and similar loop shapes appear at 7, 213, and 427 mm/s.
- Fig. 8(b) turns that hysteresis into a calibration. Compression and extension need separate linear fits because the friction direction reverses. The reported calibration slopes are about 0.886 in compression and 0.696 in extension, so a single scale factor would hide a direction-dependent force-estimation error.
- Fig. 8(c) compares force over time. The raw motor-current estimate misses the force-sensor curve, while the friction-compensated estimate tracks it much more closely. This is the practical point: current-based force estimation is useful only after the transmission friction is characterized.
- Fig. 8(d) repeats the comparison during impact-like virtual spring tests rather than slow calibration pushes. The compensated estimate follows the force sensor for 3406 N/m and roughly 5038 N/m stiffness settings, with mean absolute error divided by full-scale sensor force of 0.041 and 0.049.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
> WARNING
> The paper is internally inconsistent on the higher virtual stiffness: the abstract and body text report 5038 N/m, while the Fig. 8 labels and caption report 5083 N/m. This page uses 5038 N/m when referencing the reported error sentence, and treats the plot label as 5083 N/m. Sources: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 1; sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 5, Fig. 8.

The figure therefore supports the narrow claim that a low-reduction, low-friction, low-impedance leg can estimate foot force well enough for fast proprioceptive force control. It does not mean motor current is automatically an accurate force sensor on any geared leg; the hardware transparency and friction compensation are part of the measurement chain.

Source: sources/papers/seok-2012-actuator-design-for-high-force-proprioceptive-control.pdf, page 5, force-control experiment text and Fig. 8.

chunks.pageTitle: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
headingPath: Transmission Transparency and Proprioceptive Force Control
chunks.text:
Design Implication

The point of QDD-style hardware is not merely "use a small gear ratio." The point is to reduce passive impedance enough that the actuator behaves transparently for force control and survives impact. Low gear ratio is one practical route; a high-ratio transmission can compete only if it remains efficient, low-friction, backdrivable, and impact-tolerant.

Lecture 4 clarifies the terminology: use "backdrivability" mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior, not as a synonym for reflected inertia. Reflected inertia should be discussed separately as:

$$
J{out}=N^2Jr
$$

Both low friction and low reflected inertia improve transparency, but they are different design problems.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-345; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 16.

Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text:
Actuator Design to Current Control Map

This guide connects the lecture sequence as one design chain. Lecture 3 is split in this map: Lecture 3A belongs to the Lecture 2 modeling/homework review, while Lecture 3B is Juminchan's separate motor-driver/current-control seminar branch. Lecture 5 returns to motor quality and explains how to estimate torque constant analytically before detailed FEM.

mermaid
flowchart LR
 A["Requirements"] --> B["Torque / Mass / Inertia"]
 B --> C["Motor Quality and Winding"]
 C --> C2["Lecture 5: Analytical Kt"]
 C2 --> D["SPMSM Model and MOR"]
 D --> D2["Lecture 3A: Homework / Modeling Review"]
 D2 --> E["Lecture 3B: Inverter and SVPWM"]
 E --> F["Current Sensing"]
 F --> G["d-q Current Control"]
 G --> H["Actuator-Level Revision"]
 H --> I["RAIPAL Case Study: Production / URDF / Demos"]

Use this after the lecture-specific paths when you want the system view rather than one lecture at a time.

headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
Actuator Design to Current Control Map

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text:
1. Choose the Requirement Point

Start with actuator-requirement-selection and pareto-optimality.

The first design decision is not "make every metric better." It is choosing a point on the current Pareto front that matches the robot task. For actuator selection, keep primary requirements separate from secondary preferences so the optimization target does not move every time a design looks attractive.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 51-61; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
2. Translate the Robot Task Into T-M-I
chunks.text:
2. Translate the Robot Task Into T-M-I

Read torque-mass-inertia-selection, then return to actuator-performance-metrics. If the payload torque relation is not familiar, first read jacobian-transpose-force-mapping or the full robot-jacobian-study-path.

The useful mechanical requirements are torque, mass, and output inertia. The Lecture 4 workflow moves distal to proximal: choose the far actuator first, then account for its mass when sizing the upstream joint. After the motor is selected, the reducer ratio becomes the main remaining knob for reflected rotor inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 223-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 10-13.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
3. Pick Motor Quality Before Winding Details
chunks.text:
3. Pick Motor Quality Before Winding Details

Read motor-constant, actuator-constant, actuator-design-principles, and optionally outrunner-vs-inrunner-bldc-motors.

Use motor constant $Km$ and actuator constant $C$ to reason about intrinsic motor and actuator quality before treating winding as a magic performance change. Winding choice mainly matches the same motor geometry to the available voltage/current driver constraints; it does not remove copper loss or thermal limits.

For drone or propeller-driven actuators, add the outrunner/inrunner packaging check here. Direct-drive multirotors usually favor outrunners because large low-RPM propellers need low-speed torque without gearbox losses, while inrunners fit EDF, high-speed small propellers, narrow packages, sealed housings, or geared propulsion paths.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31; sources/web-clippings/Outrunner Brushless Motor vs Inrunner Motor for Drone.md, applicable-scenarios section.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text:
4. Estimate Torque Constant From Geometry

Read analytical-torque-constant-spmsm, motional-emf-and-flux-linkage, and winding-function-and-factor.

Lecture 5 fills in how a designer estimates $Kt$ from cross-section geometry, magnet grade, axial length, and winding pattern before building hardware. Keep the result connected to motor-constant and actuator-constant: analytical $Kt$ is one ingredient in the motor and actuator-quality metrics, not the whole actuator design.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 468-483; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
5. Match the Motor to the Driver
chunks.text:
5. Match the Motor to the Driver

Read motor-winding-configuration, motor-operating-region, pmsm-topology-control-consequences, and motor-constant-unit-conversions.

At this stage, check whether the selected motor and winding can live inside the driver and battery limits. Keep the convention questions explicit: phase vs line-line, peak vs RMS, q-axis current vs line current, and wye/delta equivalent parameters can change the numerical constants used by the controller.

The topology-control consequence belongs here rather than only in the control chapter: if a design is SPMSM-like, low-speed $id=0$ MTPA and limited flux-weakening authority are the default expectations; if it is IPMSM-like, saliency changes both the MTPA map and the high-speed voltage-limited range.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-253; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 5; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 9.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
6. Build the Model Used by the Controller
chunks.text:
6. Build the Model Used by the Controller

Read abc-to-dq-transform-derivation, spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, spmsm-torque-equation, and spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions.

The controller-facing model turns phase variables into rotor-frame d-q variables. Keep the flux-linkage form visible before simplifying to constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$, because saturation and saliency change what the simple SPMSM equations mean.

Use lecture-02-homework-workthrough for the Lecture 3A review questions that close this modeling block.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
7. Branch Into Lecture 3B: Turn Voltage Limits Into Usable Voltage
chunks.text:
7. Branch Into Lecture 3B: Turn Voltage Limits Into Usable Voltage

Read motor-driver-inverter and space-vector-pwm.

This is the start of the Juminchan seminar branch. The inverter applies phase voltage through switching states, not through ideal analog voltage sources. SVPWM mixes active and zero vectors over a switching period, and the real controller keeps voltage margin so sensing and switching behavior remain usable.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-205; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 12-13.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
8. Continue Lecture 3B: Measure Current Before Controlling It
chunks.text:
8. Continue Lecture 3B: Measure Current Before Controlling It

Read motor-current-sensing.

Current control quality depends on the current measurement path: shunt value, amplifier gain, ADC timing, CMRR, ground bounce, and unit scaling. A motor model can be clean while the measured current is still wrong enough to destabilize tuning.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-223; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-18.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
9. Continue Lecture 3B: Close the d-q Current Loop
chunks.text:
9. Continue Lecture 3B: Close the d-q Current Loop

Read motor-current-control, then motor-driver-nonidealities.

The d-q PI loop is where the actuator design becomes an implemented torque source. Bandwidth, feedforward, saturation, anti-windup, dead time, delay, and ringing decide how much of the selected actuator capability is actually reachable in hardware.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 21; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 24-25.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text:
10. Add the Textbook Control Backbone

Use the Electric Motor Control chapters as the control-theory spine behind the lecture chain:
- chapter-05-vector-control links the SPMSM/IPMSM d-q model to vector-control torque commands.
- chapter-06-current-regulators formalizes the current-loop gains and feedforward terms behind the Lecture 3B tuning advice.
- chapter-07-pwm-inverters explains how the inverter actually spends switching time to produce voltage, and why overmodulation/dead time/current sampling constrain the clean control diagram.
- chapter-08-high-speed-operation turns the MOR into current-circle and voltage-ellipse geometry.
- chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control closes the loop on rotor-angle and speed feedback.

Use pmsm-topology-control-consequences as the bridge across Chapters 5 and 8: it is the page that keeps MTPA, negative d-axis current, flux weakening, and SPMSM/IPMSM topology in one control-design interpretation.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text: Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-76; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
11. Feed Hardware Limits Back Into the Design
chunks.text:
11. Feed Hardware Limits Back Into the Design

Return to backdrivability, transmission-transparency, and robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

If the design fails at the current-control stage, the fix may be mechanical, electrical, or both: a different reduction ratio, winding, driver voltage/current rating, thermal path, sensing topology, or reducer type. Treat this as a loop back to requirements, not as a controller-only failure.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 300-345.

chunks.pageTitle: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text:
12. Study the RAIPAL End-to-End Case

Read raipal-mechanism-development, design-intentions-and-contributions, and robot-hardware-iteration.

The RAIPAL special lecture is the end-to-end case study after the abstract design chain. It shows how control transparency and engineering simplicity become mechanism choices, how concept comparison uses T-M-I estimates before detailed CAD, how production drawings and prototypes expose new errors, and why digital interface work is part of hardware completion for RL-oriented systems.

headingPath: Actuator Design to Current Control Map
chunks.text:
Review Checklist
- Can the required joint torque be traced to a payload/task assumption?
- Is the actuator limited by torque, voltage, current, temperature, output inertia, or backdrivability?
- Are $Kt$, $Kv$, $Rs$, and $Ls$ all using compatible conventions?
- Does the operating-region plot use realistic voltage margin?
- Is the current sensor measuring the same current variable used by the control law?
- Is the rotor-angle/speed feedback path good enough for the intended low-speed, startup, and sensorless behavior?
- If the loop saturates, is the next design change in the motor, reducer, driver, battery, sensor, or control tuning?

Lecture 1 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 1: 구동기 설계 개론.

Use this as the entry point if you are new to actuator design and want the concepts in a low-friction order.
For the source-level overview, read lecture-01-actuator-design-introduction first.

0. Start Here: Basic Vocabulary

Read motor-actuator-structure first if any of these terms are unclear:
- motor vs actuator
- stator / rotor
- air gap
- winding
- motor driver
- reducer / gearbox
- end turn
- housing

Goal: understand what physical parts the lecture is talking about before reading the design principles.

Optional BLDC/SPMSM branch:
- bldc-pmsm-spmsm — BLDC/PMSM/SPMSM naming
- outrunner-vs-inrunner-bldc-motors — outer-rotor vs inner-rotor packaging and drone propeller fit
- bldc-electronic-commutation — brushes, commutator, and electronic phase switching
- hall-effect-position-sensing — rotor-position feedback and Hall sensor ICs

1. Understand the Design Frame

Read pareto-optimality.

Core idea: there is no universally "best" motor. There are trade-off fronts. First eliminate dominated designs, then choose the right point for the application.

Key intuition: MTB vs road bike is not a universal ranking problem; it is an application-fit problem.

2. Learn the Three Real Metrics

Read actuator-performance-metrics.

Focus on:
- mass
- max torque
- joint/output inertia
- why power and max speed are misleading standalone metrics
- why "bigger motor + bigger gearbox" is not free

Checkpoint: explain why $10:1$ reduction gives about $10\times$ torque but $100\times$ reflected rotor inertia.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 88-138; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 12-13 and 22.

3. Learn the Three Design Principles

Read actuator-design-principles.

Focus on:
- winding does not change ideal torque capability when geometry/materials are fixed
- winding is mostly driver matching
- fixed mass + fixed max torque largely determines output inertia
- ideal pizza vs kimbap equivalence vs real gearbox/end-turn penalties

Checkpoint: explain why low-turn/thick-wire and high-turn/thin-wire motors can target different voltage/current drivers without changing the ideal motor constant.

headingPath: 4. Connect the Metrics to Legged Robots
chunks.text:
4. Connect the Metrics to Legged Robots

Read legged-robot-actuation.

Focus on:
- why legged robots care about impact and low passive impedance
- why high reduction can damage backdrivability, force control, efficiency, and durability
- why QDD is a practical design direction, not a magical "low gear ratio is always best" rule
- why manipulators and legs can prefer different points on the same Pareto front

Checkpoint: explain why HOUND/MIT-Cheetah-style leg actuators accept heavier motors to reduce reflected inertia and impact losses.

headingPath: 5. Study the Paper Behind the Lecture
chunks.text:
5. Study the Paper Behind the Lecture

Read seok-2012-high-force-proprioceptive-actuation.

Focus on:
- power density vs torque density
- gap radius scaling
- reflected inertia thought experiment
- MIT Cheetah 1:5.8 low-reduction design
- proprioceptive force-control result

Then read transmission-transparency.

Checkpoint: explain why the paper's real target is transmission transparency for force control, not simply a small gear ratio.

headingPath: 6. Study the Intrinsic Actuator Quality Metric
chunks.text:
6. Study the Intrinsic Actuator Quality Metric

Read actuator-constant, then motor-constant for the $Km$ ingredient.

Focus on:
- what $C$ tries to capture
- how $Kt$ and $Rs$ combine into $Km$
- why scaling and gearing do not improve fundamental motor quality
- how better materials/topology/Cu-Iron allocation can improve the real design
- how gap radius relates to $KM^2$ in Seok et al. 2012

Checkpoint: explain the difference between choosing a point on a fixed Pareto front and moving the front itself.

Suggested Review Loop

After reading the pages once, answer these in your own words:
- Why is power not the right motor metric for legged robot actuators?
- What does output/joint inertia mean?
- Why does reflected rotor inertia scale as $N^2$?
- Why can pizza and kimbap motors be ideal-theory equivalent?
- Why does low reduction win often in practice?
- Why can a well-designed high-ratio transmission still be viable?
- What does transmission transparency enable in proprioceptive force control?

If any answer feels fuzzy, return to the linked concept page and update the wiki with a new clarification.

Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:
Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review

This guide is the reading route for the Lecture 2 homework prompts and Lecture 3A, the homework/modeling review at the start of Lecture 3. The long derivations now live in concept pages so they can be reused outside this homework context.

Use this with spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, motor-winding-configuration, motor-operating-region, and motor-constant.

After this review block, Lecture 3 switches to Lecture 3B, Juminchan's separate Motor Driver / Current Control seminar. Use lecture-03-study-path for that branch.

Source: Lecture 3A/3B boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

Figure: target d-q voltage equations from Lecture 2. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:
1. Derive the d-q Voltage Equations

Read abc-to-dq-transform-derivation.

Focus on:
- starting from $\mathbf v{abc}=Rs\mathbf i{abc}+\frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}$
- why only two independent current coordinates remain in a balanced three-wire motor
- why Park/Clarke definitions are convention-dependent
- where $-\omegae\lambdaq$ and $+\omegae\lambdad$ enter
- how the ideal SPMSM assumptions produce the target d-q voltage equations

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:
2. Explain Torque From q-Axis Current

Read spmsm-torque-equation.

Focus on:
- the general flux-linkage torque form
- why ideal SPMSM cancels the $Ls id iq$ terms
- why $\taum \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq$ is a linear-model result
- how saliency adds reluctance torque

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:
3. Relate $Kv$, Back EMF, and $Kt$

Read motor-constant-unit-conversions.

Focus on:
- why matching SI $Ke$ and $Kt$ are numerically equal
- how the common $Kt \approx \frac{9.55}{Kv}$ conversion is derived
- why line-line vs phase, RMS vs peak, and q-axis vs line current conventions change constants
- how the oscilloscope back-EMF method estimates torque constant without a torque sensor

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-235; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:
4. Check Wye/Delta Parameter Conventions

Read wye-delta-parameter-conventions.

Focus on:
- why terminal-only ABC tests do not uniquely reveal ideal internal wye vs delta topology
- converting line-to-line resistance to equivalent phase resistance
- why inductance conversion needs more care than scalar resistance conversion
- why motor-control models only need a consistent equivalent-circuit convention

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
5. Separate Exact Forms From Linear Assumptions
chunks.text:
5. Separate Exact Forms From Linear Assumptions

Read spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions.

Focus on:
- which equations remain valid in flux-linkage form
- where constant $Ls$ and constant $\phif$ enter
- why saturation changes the apparent torque constant
- why the lecture frames high-current nonlinearity as $B$-$H$ saturation, not hysteresis by itself

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 290-297.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:
Homework Checklist

Before considering the homework complete, be able to answer:
- Starting from $\mathbf v{abc}=Rs\mathbf i{abc}+\frac{d\boldsymbol\lambda{abc}}{dt}$, where exactly does $+\omegae\phif$ enter the q-axis equation?
- Which term makes the d-axis equation depend on q-axis current?
- Why does $\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right)=-\frac{1}{2}$ appear in the ideal mutual-inductance argument?
- What convention is used for $id$ and $iq$: amplitude-invariant or power-invariant?
- Is a quoted $Kt$ per phase peak, q-axis peak, line RMS, or controller current?
- Is a quoted $Kv$ from DC bus voltage, phase voltage, or line-line RMS back EMF?
- Can a terminal-only resistance/inductance test distinguish physical wye from equivalent delta?
- Which final equations break first when iron saturates?

Source: checklist distilled from sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2, 4, and 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.

headingPath: Lecture 2 Homework Workthrough and Lecture 3A Review
chunks.text:

- wye-delta-parameter-conventions

Lecture 2 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 2: SPMSM 모델링.

Use this after Lecture 1 if you understand basic actuator parts, winding, and BLDC/SPMSM terminology. The first part of Lecture 3 continues this modeling path as Lecture 3A homework/modeling review; Lecture 3B then branches into Juminchan's motor-driver implementation seminar.

Source: Lecture 3 transition in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

0. Refresh the Prerequisites

Read bldc-pmsm-spmsm and motor-winding-configuration first if any of these are fuzzy:
- why robotics "BLDC" often means SPMSM in stricter language
- surface vs interior permanent magnets
- wye/star vs delta winding
- phase value vs terminal-to-terminal value
- speed constant $Kv$ and torque constant $Kt$

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 7.

1. Read the Source-Level Summary

Read lecture-02-spmsm-modeling.

Goal: get the lecture's whole arc before diving into the formulas.

2. Understand the Torque Mechanisms

Read magnetic-reluctance-torque.

Focus on:
- magnetic torque vs reluctance torque
- why SynRM, IPMSM, and SPMSM sit at different points on the torque-mechanism spectrum
- why ideal SPMSM has little reluctance torque
- why "IPMSM is just too nonlinear to control" is not the full robotics explanation

Then read pmsm-topology-control-consequences as the synthesis page that carries this taxonomy into Chapter 5 MTPA and Chapter 8 high-speed operation. The checkpoint is to explain why the same negative d-axis current can be a reluctance-torque lever in IPMSM MTPA and a flux-weakening lever once voltage becomes limiting.

Textbook extension source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 38-44; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.

3. Learn the Operating-Region View

Read motor-operating-region.

Focus on:
- brushed DC warm-up model
- why back EMF limits torque at high speed
- voltage limit vs current limit
- regeneration and real battery voltage sag/rise
- why a max-torque/max-RPM rectangle is dangerous for sim-to-real

Then read chapter-08-high-speed-operation for the textbook extension: current limits become circles, voltage limits become speed-dependent ellipses, and field weakening is an optimized current-command problem rather than just a note that "$id$ can be negative." Keep pmsm-topology-control-consequences nearby when deciding whether a claim is really about SPMSM, IPMSM, or PMSM control in general.

Checkpoint: explain why a robot policy can be wrong near max RPM even if the simulated actuator has the right max torque and max speed numbers.

Textbook extension source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.

4. Study the SPMSM Model

Read spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.

Focus on:
- why ABC phase variables reduce to two d-q coordinates
- the rotor-frame d-q voltage equations
- cross-coupling terms
- field weakening
- why $Rs$ and $Ls$ need convention checking
- which parts are linear approximations affected by saturation

Checkpoint: point to the exact terms that make the SPMSM model differ from the brushed DC model.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2.

For the control extension, read chapter-05-vector-control. It shows how the same d-q model becomes vector control: rotor-flux or magnet-flux alignment makes one current axis the flux channel and the orthogonal axis the torque channel.

Textbook extension source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-35.

5. Work Through the Homework Questions

Read lecture-02-homework-workthrough as the route through the homework pages.

Use the source slides and transcript for the homework-style prompts:
- derive d-q voltage equations from ABC phase equations with abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- explain q-axis torque production with spmsm-torque-equation
- explain why the flux/inductance terms use trigonometric structure
- relate $Kv$ or back-EMF constant to torque constant with motor-constant-unit-conversions
- decide whether an external ABC-terminal test can distinguish wye vs delta winding with wye-delta-parameter-conventions
- identify where Park/Clarke transform conventions change numerical definitions
- separate equations that remain exact from equations that depend on linear magnetic assumptions with spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2, 4, and 7; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.
