headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path

This is the reading order for Lecture 3B, Juminchan's separate Motor Driver / Current Control seminar.

Use lecture-02-homework-workthrough for Lecture 3A, the Lecture 2 homework/modeling review that comes before the seminar. Use this page only after you already understand the basic SPMSM d-q model and motor-operating-region idea.

Source: seminar boundary in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340; route synthesized from sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 135-223 and 225-299; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 12-25.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
0. Close Lecture 3A if Needed

Read lecture-02-homework-workthrough first if the Lecture 2 homework corrections are still unresolved.

Focus on:
- torque constant estimation from back EMF
- wye/delta equivalent-circuit conventions
- Park/Clarke convention dependence
- exact flux-linkage forms vs linear SPMSM approximations

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-318; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
1. Refresh the Modeling Prerequisites

Read spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, motor-winding-configuration, motor-operating-region, and pmsm-topology-control-consequences first if any of these are fuzzy:
- phase values vs terminal-to-terminal values
- Park/Clarke convention dependence
- why d-q voltage limits shape the MOR
- why q-axis current is torque-producing in ideal SPMSM
- why negative d-axis current means different things in IPMSM MTPA and high-speed PMSM flux weakening

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
2. Read the Source-Level Summary

Read lecture-03-motor-driver-and-homework-review.

Goal: see the Lecture 3A / 3B boundary before studying the seminar-specific concepts.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
3. Learn What the Inverter Is Doing
chunks.text:
3. Learn What the Inverter Is Doing

Read motor-driver-inverter.

Focus on:
- why the driver controls current by applying voltage
- why PWM is used instead of true analog voltage
- half-bridge and three-phase inverter switch states
- zero vectors and active vectors
- why high-side MOSFETs need gate-driver boost circuitry

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
4. Study SVPWM and Voltage Margin

Read space-vector-pwm.

Focus on:
- time-weighted mixing of active and zero vectors
- why symmetric zero-vector placement reduces ripple
- why the usable voltage region is the inscribed circle
- why 1-5% voltage margin is kept in practice
- how that margin shrinks the real MOR

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
5. Understand Current Measurement

Read motor-current-sensing.

Focus on:
- shunt resistor measurement
- low-side vs in-line sensing
- ADC timing windows
- why voltage margin affects sensing
- CMRR, op-amp gain, unit scaling, noise, and ground bounce

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
6. Build the Current Loop

Read motor-current-control.

Focus on:
- first-order $RL$ plant model
- PI gains from pole-zero cancellation
- desired bandwidth
- cross-coupling and back-EMF feedforward
- voltage saturation and anti-windup
- why practical tuning starts small and often omits feedforward at first

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
7. Remember the Hardware Nonidealities

Read motor-driver-nonidealities.

Focus on:
- dead time and body-diode voltage drop
- MOSFET delay and gate charge
- switching ringing
- sensor/filter/control delay
- why current control can tolerate more voltage error than sensorless voltage-model control

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
8. Add the Textbook Reinforcement Branch

After the lecture route, use the textbook chapter summaries to deepen each layer without changing the lecture order:
- chapter-05-vector-control for why AC vector control tries to reproduce the DC-motor separation of flux and torque current
- chapter-06-current-regulators for synchronous-frame PI gains, feedforward, and anti-windup as the formal current-loop basis
- chapter-07-pwm-inverters for SVPWM voltage vectors, modulation index, overmodulation, dead time, and PWM-synchronized current measurement
- chapter-08-high-speed-operation for the voltage/current limit geometry behind field weakening
- chapter-09-speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control for resolver/encoder feedback, M/T speed estimation, and sensorless-control limits

Use pmsm-topology-control-consequences to connect the Chapter 5 PMSM MTPA material to the Chapter 8 flux-weakening material without folding that topology discussion into the motor-driver implementation pages.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text: Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-5---Vector-control-of-alternating-current-m2017Electric-Motor-Cont.pdf, pages 33-35; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-6---Current-regulators-of-alternating-curren2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 11-17; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-7---Pulse-width-modulation-inverters2017Electric-Motor-Control.pdf, pages 37-76; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23; sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-9---Speed-estimation-and-sensorless-control-of-a2017Electric-Motor.pdf, pages 10-15.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
headingPath: Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar Study Path
chunks.text:
9. Homework Branch

For the Lecture 3 homework, return to spmsm-dq-voltage-equations, motor-constant, and motor-operating-region. This homework uses the same SPMSM model, but it should not blur the wiki boundary: Lecture 3A is the earlier homework review, and Lecture 3B is the Juminchan motor-driver seminar.

The assigned problems ask you to:
- identify where linear magnetic assumptions enter torque/copper-loss formulas
- plot SPMSM MOR from the d-q voltage equations
- compare $id=0$ operation against optimized $id$ by speed
- derive d-q short-circuit currents and braking torque under resistive braking

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - homework.pdf; supporting model material in sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

Lecture 4 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 4: Actuator Design Process.

Use this after Lecture 1, Lecture 2, the Lecture 3A modeling review, and the motor-driver basics from Lecture 3B if you understand pareto-optimality, actuator-performance-metrics, and spmsm-dq-voltage-equations.
For the full chain across all four lectures, see actuator-design-to-current-control-map.

1. Read the Source-Level Summary

Read lecture-04-actuator-design-process.

Goal: understand the design arc before diving into each requirement.

2. Separate Requirements From Optimization

Read actuator-requirement-selection.

Focus on:
- choosing a point on the current Pareto front
- pushing the Pareto front as a separate activity
- primary vs secondary requirements
- the corrected normalized actuator relationship from Lecture 4

3. Work Through T-M-I

Read torque-mass-inertia-selection.

Focus on:
- distal-to-proximal manipulator design
- torque from payload via $\tau = J^T F$
- static QDD assumptions
- reducer ratio as the remaining inertia knob after motor selection
- the 1-DOF dynamic rule $N^2Jr = J{link}$

4. Clarify Backdrivability

Read backdrivability.

Focus on the difference between:
- backward efficiency
- reverse-drive starting torque / Coulomb friction
- reflected inertia

Use transmission-transparency afterward if you want the proprioceptive force-control motivation.

5. Compare Reducer Types

Read robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

Focus on:
- planetary vs cycloidal vs harmonic drives
- torque density vs efficiency
- why serial spur gears are not enough
- why harmonic drives suit CNC-style position control better than QDD-style low-friction control
- why cycloidal drives are attractive in theory but hard to manufacture well

6. Keep the Open Ends Visible

Lecture 4 is the first half of the design process. Reducer detailed design and structural-component design are deferred to later material.

Lecture 5 Study Path

A recommended reading order for Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant.

> WARNING
> The transcript is incomplete between 22:48 and 54:56. Use the summary and selected slide-derived concept pages as the primary route through the missing middle.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 220-240.

1. Start With the Source Summary

Read lecture-05-analytical-torque-constant.

Goal: separate the Lecture 3 homework-solution prelude from the main Lecture 5 derivation.

2. Close the Homework Threads

Read motor-operating-region, pmsm-topology-control-consequences, and resistive-braking.

Focus on the field-weakening comparison in the MOR solution and the safety interpretation of resistive braking.

Use chapter-08-high-speed-operation as the textbook cross-check for the field-weakening side: it explains why negative d-axis current changes the PMSM voltage boundary and why the feasible region is the intersection of current and voltage limits. Use pmsm-topology-control-consequences to keep that homework result tied to the SPMSM/IPMSM distinction rather than reading field weakening as a generic add-on to the SPMSM torque-constant derivation.

Source: sources/textbooks/electric-motor-control-2017/Chapter-8---High-speed-operation-of-alternating-curr2017Electric-Motor-Con.pdf, pages 17-23.

3. Rebuild the EMF Link

Read motional-emf-and-flux-linkage.

Focus on why $e=Blv$ is an electromotive-force statement, not a guarantee of terminal current, and why EMF can be integrated into flux linkage.

4. Understand the Winding Representation

Read winding-function-and-factor.

Focus on conductor density, winding function, overlap with radial magnet flux, and why winding factor is a correction for imperfect addition of conductor EMF contributions.

5. Derive the Torque Constant

Read analytical-torque-constant-spmsm, then revisit motor-constant and actuator-constant.

Focus on the final relationship:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g}BrNRl
$$

and on the design warning that pole-pair count is not an independent torque-constant multiplier when total effective conductor count is fixed.

6. Keep the Deferred Material Visible

Lecture 5 is Part 1. Magnetic-circuit modeling, real winding choices, saturation torque, and the final actuator-limit argument are deferred.

For the broader chain, return to actuator-design-to-current-control-map.

RAIPAL Mechanism Development Guide

Use this guide when the RAIPAL lecture feels split across the summary, concept pages, and the cross-lecture design map. It is not another source summary. It is a reading route for extracting the hardware-development lessons from Choi Hyung-ho's RAIPAL mechanism lecture.

The most useful posture is to read RAIPAL as the practical end of the actuator-design sequence: the actuators, drivers, mechanical parts, drawings, prototypes, digital model, middleware, and demonstrations all have to become one usable arm platform.

1. Start With the Lecture Frame

Read raipal-mechanism-development first, then design-intentions-and-contributions.

The lecture is explicitly framed as a "bolt-turner" retrospective, not as a clean actuator-optimization derivation. The speaker is explaining how the arm was packaged and made usable from concept through demo, and why hardware work spends more time on practical design intentions than on final contribution language. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 1-4; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 23-59.

Focus questions:
- What decisions exist before there is a robot to evaluate?
- Which decisions are design intentions, and which are later contribution claims?
- Why are limited hardware samples so different from software-style iteration?

2. Translate Intentions Into Constraints

Read actuator-requirement-selection, transmission-transparency, and the RAIPAL example in design-intentions-and-contributions.

RAIPAL's design intentions were control transparency, engineering simplicity, and streamlined symmetry. In practical terms, that meant RL-friendly linear dynamics, small sim-to-real gap, QDD-like transparent actuation, high-bandwidth motor drivers, transparent decoupled mechanisms, repeated actuator families, and unified subcomponents. The project-level payload target was 5 kg nominal and 25 kg peak, while the mechanism still needed at least one meaningful mechanism-level hook for later contribution framing. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 5-6; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 63-103.

Read this section as a constraint translation exercise: "easy to control" becomes "avoid coupled mechanisms"; "easy to digitize" becomes "model mass and inertia well"; "modular" becomes "reuse actuators and electronics where possible."

3. Rebuild the Concept Comparison

Read torque-mass-inertia-selection and actuator-performance-metrics, then inspect the FAST, SIMPLE, and LINKED concept table.

Figure: FAST, SIMPLE, and LINKED concept comparison using mass, inertia, holding-torque, and payload estimates before detailed CAD. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8.

Do not treat the table as reverse-engineered truth about every reference robot. Treat it as a same-assumption comparison that shrinks topology choices before CAD. The LINKED concept is reported at 7.9 kg, projected inertia <0.33,0.43,0.11> kg m^2, and 7.2 kg worst-case payload, but the transcript stresses that case-study data were rough because exact masses and inertias are rarely public. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 8; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 113-126; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 197-217.

4. Follow the Iteration Loop

Read robot-hardware-iteration next.

The lecture's iteration rule is "broad strokes first": make the concept tangible quickly, expect multiple iterations, and use each draft to expose the next important mistake. The concrete CAD example compares v0-1 at minimum safety factor 1.8 and about 15 mm peak-torque deformation against v1-1 at minimum safety factor 3.2 and about 1 mm deformation. The transcript warns that the simplified FEA is still approximate; its value is fast relative evidence, not exact fracture prediction. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 9-12; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 127-193.

Use this section to ask what evidence should stop an iteration. For RAIPAL, the answer moved through strength, manufacturability, bearing load, simpler wrist structure, and finally enough confidence to spend money and time on production.

5. Treat Production as Design Work

Return to the production part of raipal-mechanism-development.

"Production ready" in this lecture does not mean perfect. It means the design is credible enough that the team is willing to spend scarce money and time on it. Drawings are part of the design process because they transfer manufacturing intentions into a common language: critical dimensions, precision requirements, assembly relations, and manufacturability constraints. The transcript notes that drawing work can force another CAD iteration when the intended part cannot be manufactured or specified cleanly. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 13-15; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 1.txt, lines 207-257.

This is the point where the lecture stops being "CAD" and becomes assembly risk management.

6. Close the Hardware-Software Interface

Read the digital-interface section of raipal-mechanism-development, then branch to motor-operating-region, transmission-transparency, and motor-current-control if you want to connect the mechanism back to control.

The robot is not finished when the metal assembly moves. RAIPAL needed URDF conversion for physical simulation and middleware for real-time policy rollout. The slide calls out roughly 5 g mass agreement, precise rotor inertia calculation, communication and real-time rollout support, and crossed-4-bar calculation; the transcript adds that poor rotor-inertia modeling can waste a large fraction of useful actuator capability in sim-to-real transfer. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 16; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 9-30.

Read this as the control-facing closure of the hardware loop: a transparent mechanism still fails the learning workflow if the model, inertia, communication, or timing layer is wrong.

headingPath: 7. Use Demos and Time Data as Reality Checks
chunks.text:
7. Use Demos and Time Data as Reality Checks

Read the demonstrations, time-allocation, and retrospective sections of raipal-mechanism-development.

Demos are presented as requirement-verification sequences for payload, speed, backdrivability, and other behaviors. They do not replace specialized tests for efficiency, backlash, thermal paths, or actuator characterization. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 17; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 31-39.

The time charts are a planning warning. The slide statistics put design iteration at 30.6% of non-delay work, digital interface at 18.5%, production at 16.2%, and testing at 17.3%. A separate chart labels 54.5% of the calendar as delay, which the transcript connects to demos, other robot work, renovation, health, proposals, and coordination bottlenecks. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 25-26; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 42-84.

8. Extract the Retrospective Lesson

Finish by rereading design-intentions-and-contributions and robot-hardware-iteration.

Figure: tangible, analytic, and specialized tools used during RAIPAL iteration. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, page 28.

The retrospective message is not "ignore contributions." It is that good hardware often reveals defensible contribution framing after it works. RAIPAL's contribution was later framed around human-level dynamic range, helped by the forearm reduction choice and dynamic demonstrations. The broader advice is to design first, contribute later, and transfer insights to the next engineer because one graduate hardware cycle often cannot build the improved next robot. Source: sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture slides.pdf, pages 28-33; sources/lectures/raipal-mechanism-development-choi-hyung-ho/RAIPAL Mechanism Development - Choi Hyung-ho lecture transcript part 2.txt, lines 88-188.

Review Questions
- Which RAIPAL mechanism choices directly protect control transparency?
- Which concept-comparison metrics would you compute before detailed CAD?
- What evidence would make you keep iterating a structural part?
- What must a production drawing communicate that a CAD model alone does not?
- Which URDF or middleware errors could hide good actuator hardware from the controller?
- Which demonstrations verify requirements, and which questions need specialized fixtures?
- How would you plan schedule differently after seeing the RAIPAL time-allocation and delay charts?

Related
- raipal-mechanism-development
- design-intentions-and-contributions
- robot-hardware-iteration
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- transmission-transparency
- actuator-design-to-current-control-map

Robot Jacobian Study Path

Use this path when the goal is to connect kinematics formulas to actuator sizing, contact force, and inverse kinematics.

Core Path

1. robot-jacobian - start with $\dot{x}=J(q)\dot{q}$, column interpretation, and the two-link example.
2. jacobian-singularities - understand rank loss, determinant checks for square Jacobians, and why near-singular control becomes fragile.
3. manipulability-ellipsoid - read velocity capability as a shape, not only a matrix.
4. jacobian-transpose-force-mapping - connect task force to joint torque through virtual work.
5. space-and-body-jacobians - make frame conventions explicit for spatial twists and wrench calculations.
6. jacobian-inverse-kinematics - use the Jacobian as a local IK step, with pseudoinverse and singularity caveats.

Hardware Branch

For actuator design, branch from jacobian-transpose-force-mapping into torque-mass-inertia-selection. That is where the same $J^TF$ relation becomes payload torque sizing.

For proprioceptive force control, branch into transmission-transparency. The Jacobian only gives the kinematic mapping; hardware friction, reflected inertia, and torque-estimation quality decide whether the force estimate is usable.

chunks.pageTitle: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors

Source: sources/application-notes/honeywell-app-note-magnetic-position-sensing-bldc-motors.pdf - Honeywell/Sensing and Productivity Solutions application note, "Magnetic Position Sensing in Brushless DC Electric Motors" (March 2016).

chunks.pageTitle: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Core Idea

BLDC motors replace brush/commutator mechanical switching with electronic commutation. Hall-effect sensor ICs detect rotor magnetic position and send angular-position information to logic/driver circuitry, which switches winding polarity in the correct sequence.

chunks.pageTitle: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Key Details
- BLDC motors are described as brush-type motors "turned inside out":
- power is fed to stationary armature/stator windings
- permanent magnet rotor is the rotating member
- position sensors and electronic switching replace brushes and commutator
- Rotor magnets operate Hall-effect sensor ICs.
- Sensor outputs act as shaft-position encoder signals.
- Logic circuitry uses these signals to switch power transistors in an H-bridge/three-phase driver circuit.
- Windings alternate polarity relative to shaft position, producing torque from the interaction of winding field and rotor permanent magnets.
- Hall sensor ICs can be placed inside the motor, at the end of the shaft, or around a ring magnet attached to the rotor shaft.
- Hall-effect sensor ICs can also support RPM and direction sensing.

chunks.pageTitle: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
headingPath: Honeywell App Note: Magnetic Position Sensing in BLDC Motors
chunks.text:
Honeywell Product Notes

The app note discusses bipolar and latching Hall-effect sensor IC families such as SS30, SS31x/SS41x, SS36x/SS46x, VF360NT/VF360ST/VF460S, and related variants.

Promoted benefits include:
- no moving parts
- reduced brush-wear maintenance
- compact packaging such as SOT-23
- low-voltage operation in selected parts
- reverse-voltage protection in selected parts
- operation up to 150 °C in selected parts
- high sensitivity enabling smaller magnets

BLDC Electronic Commutation — ChatGPT Notes

Source: sources/chatgpt-shares/BLDC Electronic Commutation - ChatGPT notes.md - user-provided shared ChatGPT conversation about BLDC commutation and Hall sensors.

Main Questions Covered
- What is an electronic commutation circuit?
- What are DC and AC?
- How can a BLDC controller continuously switch windings?
- What is winding?
- What are BLDC motor sensor ICs?
- What are brushes and commutators?
- How does the controller know rotor position?
- Why does a BLDC rotor usually use permanent magnets rather than electromagnets?
- What is a Hall sensor?
- How do Hall signals map to coil switching?

Useful Explanation Captured

The final ChatGPT explanation frames BLDC six-step commutation as:
- Hall sensor signals identify rotor sector.
- Controller chooses which two of three phases are energized.
- One phase is positive, one negative, and one off in a common six-step scheme.
- The energized stator field is moved ahead of the rotor.
- The rotor permanent magnets keep "chasing" the next stator field position.

This is a useful beginner analogy for bldc-electronic-commutation, but should be treated as a learning note rather than an authoritative source.

For the broader split between six-step, trapezoidal, sinusoidal, and FOC language, use pmsm-control-waveform-language. For the field intuition behind "the rotor chases the stator field," use three-phase-rotating-magnetic-field.

chunks.pageTitle: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
headingPath: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
chunks.text:
ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian

This lecture introduces the Jacobian as the derivative of the forward kinematic map and then develops its main uses: velocity mapping, singularity analysis, manipulability, force mapping, and space/body Jacobians.

chunks.pageTitle: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
headingPath: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
chunks.text:
Main Flow

The lecture starts from forward kinematics $x=f(\theta)$ and differentiates it:

$$
\dot{x}=\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}\dot{\theta}.
$$

The matrix $\partial f/\partial \theta$ is the Jacobian. A two-link planar arm is used to show the concrete matrix form and the interpretation of Jacobian columns as end-effector velocity directions.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 2-5.

chunks.pageTitle: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
headingPath: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
chunks.text:
Velocity, Manipulability, And Singularities

The lecture maps bounded joint velocities through $J(\theta)$ to obtain bounded end-effector velocity sets. A square joint-velocity limit becomes a parallelogram in the two-link example, while a disk becomes a manipulability ellipsoid.

A flat ellipsoid indicates a posture close to singularity: some end-effector directions are unavailable or require large joint velocities. For the two-link example, $\theta2=0$ is explicitly identified as singular.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 5-7.

chunks.pageTitle: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
headingPath: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
chunks.text:
Force Mapping

The lecture derives the joint-torque relation from conservation of power:

$$
\tau=J^T(\theta)F.
$$

It also shows the reciprocal view: with joint-torque limits and invertible $J^T$, one can plot the end-effector forces the robot can counteract using $(J^T)^{-1}\tau$.

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 8-9.

chunks.pageTitle: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
headingPath: ECE470 Lecture 10: Velocity Kinematics And The Jacobian
chunks.text:
PoE Space And Body Jacobians

The second half of the lecture derives the space Jacobian from product-of-exponentials forward kinematics:

$$
T(\theta)=e^{[S1]\theta1}e^{[S2]\theta2}\cdots e^{[Sn]\thetan}M.
$$

Using adjoint maps, each space-Jacobian column is the corresponding screw vector expressed in fixed-frame coordinates after accounting for earlier joints. The lecture then derives the body Jacobian and relates the two by:

$$
Jb(\theta)=\operatorname{Ad}{T{bs}}Js(\theta),
$$

$$
Js(\theta)=\operatorname{Ad}{T{sb}}Jb(\theta).
$$

Source: sources/lectures/ece470/lecture-10-velocity-kinematics-the-jacobian.pdf, pages 10-23.

Lecture 1: Introduction to Actuator Design

Sources: KAIST DRCD Lab Hardware Lecture Notion export, user-provided transcript, slide PDF, and ChatGPT listening notes.
Slide deck: "Actuator Optimal Design — Lecture 1: Pareto Optimality & Design Principles" (2026-03-02, KAIST DRCD Lab, 송태규 박사과정).
Contributors: @최혁, @Yechan Seo, @JUNE, @최민석, @박경서, @이동호

Core Question

What makes a "good" motor, and where are the physical limits of its performance?

The lecture answers this through the lens of pareto-optimality — there is no universally "best" actuator, only optimal trade-off points for a given application.

1. Pareto Optimality in Design
- Design "goodness" requires defined criteria — like MTB vs road bike, the optimal point depends on purpose
- Pareto front = the set of solutions where improving one metric necessarily sacrifices another
- Goal: select the right point on the Pareto front for your application (legged robot, manipulator, etc.)

2. Three Performance Metrics
See actuator-performance-metrics for details.
- Mass — total actuator weight. Torque/mass ratio worsens with scale ($\tau \propto L^2$ but mass $\propto L^3$)
- Max Torque — limited by thermal dissipation (heat generation $\propto L^3$, dissipation $\propto L^2$)
- Joint Inertia — $J{out} = N^2 Jm + J{gear}$. Gear ratio $10\times$ → inertia $100\times$. This is why QDD avoids high gear ratios

Key insight: Power, torque-speed curve area, and max angular velocity are NOT the right standalone metrics for actuator evaluation. Electric motor operating regions can be reshaped with gearing and voltage, but mass, max torque, and joint inertia expose the real Pareto trade-off.

The slide deck gives the concrete comparison: a 38g motor rated at 1166W vs a 1042g stator+rotor rated at 2700W. By raw power density, the small motor looks better, but if it needs huge reduction to meet the joint torque target, the reflected inertia can become worse than a heavier, lower-reduction actuator.

3. Three Design Principles
See actuator-design-principles for details.
1. Max torque is independent of winding — $KM$ depends on motor geometry/materials, not how you wind the coils
2. Winding is a driver-matching problem — parallel/delta/low-turns for high-current drivers; series/wye/high-turns for high-voltage drivers
3. Given fixed mass and max torque, joint inertia is determined — pizza motor (flat, wide) vs kimbap motor (long, narrow) yield same theoretical inertia at the output

The transcript also distinguishes motor and actuator: a motor is the energy-conversion core (stator + rotor), while an actuator is the usable motion unit, typically motor + reducer + driver/control electronics + housing/sensing. See motor-actuator-structure.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166 and 250-264; sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 16, 20, and 22.

4. Legged Robot Application
See legged-robot-actuation for details.
- Ground contact is frequent and impulsive → low passive impedance is critical
- High gear ratios can destroy backdrivability, efficiency, and durability through reflected inertia, gearbox inertia, and friction
- Dominant practical direction: high-torque motor + minimum gearing (low reduction)
- Kimbap motors can have better electrical efficiency because long axial length reduces end-turn waste, but pizza motors win in many legged robots because low-reduction gearboxes are simpler and preserve backdrivability
- The lecture explicitly warns against over-reading QDD as "low gear ratio is always theoretically best." In the ideal frictionless model, pizza and kimbap can be equivalent; low reduction is a conservative practical choice because real gearboxes add friction and are hard to model/design well

5. Actuator Constant C
See actuator-constant for the corrected actuator-level metric and motor-constant for $Km$.
- Current wiki formula: $C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}$
- Higher C = fundamentally better actuator technology level. Same C = same Pareto front
- C is invariant to simple dimensional scaling and gear ratio changes
- To actually improve C: better magnets, better core materials, better topology, or FEA-driven Cu/Iron allocation for the intended operating region

Source: earlier Lecture 1 version in sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론.md, lines 146-166 and 284; corrected relationship tracked in sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117.

6. Cu vs Iron Trade-off
- Cu (copper): creates magnetic potential (like a battery)
- Iron: provides flux path (like a wire), but saturates (nonlinear → requires FEA)
- More iron → higher saturation limit → higher max torque
- More copper → higher efficiency → longer/farther operation

7. Terminology Clarifications from Listening Notes
- Stator: fixed part that carries windings and creates magnetic field
- Rotor: rotating part connected to the shaft; often carries magnets in BLDC/SPMSM-style motors
- Air gap: small gap between stator and rotor where magnetic shear force is produced; larger gap increases magnetic reluctance and weakens torque
- End turn: winding segment outside the active slot region; it adds resistance/heat but does not directly produce torque
- Motor driver: electronics that supply timed voltage/current to the phases; winding choice should be matched to this driver's voltage/current envelope

8. Appendix Design Parameters
The slide appendix groups actuator design knobs into three axes:
- Stator-rotor dimensions: gap radius, thickness, length
- Reducer: reducer type, reduction ratio
- Winding: delta/wye, serial/parallel, coil diameter/turns

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-01/Lecture 1. Actuator 설계 개론 - slides.pdf, slides 28-31.

Lecture 2: SPMSM Modeling

Sources: KAIST DRCD Lab Hardware Lecture Notion note, slide PDFs, and transcript.
Slide deck: "Actuator Optimal Design — Lecture 2: SPMSM Modeling" (2026-03-09, KAIST DRCD Lab, 송태규 박사과정).

Core Question

How do we model the motor type used in robotics, and why does the model matter for operating limits and sim-to-real?

The lecture builds from motor taxonomy to motor operating regions and then to SPMSM d-q voltage equations.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lecture structure; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2 and 7.

1. Robotics "BLDC" is Usually SPMSM

The lecture reinforces the terminology nuance from bldc-pmsm-spmsm: in robotics, the motors casually called BLDC are often better described as SPMSMs: surface permanent magnet synchronous motors.

The taxonomy used in the slides:
- SynRM: synchronous reluctance motor, no permanent magnets, pure reluctance torque
- IPMSM: interior permanent magnet synchronous motor, magnet torque plus reluctance torque from rotor saliency
- SPMSM: surface permanent magnet synchronous motor, mostly magnet torque in the ideal symmetric model

See magnetic-reluctance-torque for the torque-mechanism distinction.

2. Magnetic Torque vs Reluctance Torque

Magnetic torque comes from the stator field interacting with permanent magnets. Reluctance torque comes from the rotor moving toward the direction of lower magnetic reluctance.

For the lecture's qualitative model:
- A permanent magnet can be treated like a vacuum-like region for the incremental reluctance-torque argument because its magnetic domains are already aligned.
- Iron matters because its domains can align with the applied field, so flux prefers paths through iron.
- An IPMSM rotor can create path-dependent reluctance because the internal magnet/iron geometry is anisotropic.
- An ideal SPMSM rotor is symmetric after ignoring the surface magnets for reluctance, so it does not produce meaningful reluctance torque.

The lecture asks why robotics usually avoids IPMSM even though IPMSM can produce more maximum torque by combining magnet and reluctance torque. The Q&A rules out one simple guess: it is not merely because IPMSM torque is impossible to control. Properly modeled IPMSM can have smooth current-to-torque behavior, especially in EV-oriented designs. The durable bridge from this lecture question to Chapter 5 MTPA and Chapter 8 high-speed operation is pmsm-topology-control-consequences.

3. Magnetic Nonlinearity and Torque Constant

Before using linear motor equations, the lecture flags the main approximation: real magnetic materials do not follow a perfectly linear $B$-$H$ curve.
- Hard magnetic materials, such as permanent magnets, retain aligned magnetic domains.
- Soft magnetic materials, such as stator/rotor iron, have narrow hysteresis loops but still saturate.
- The reason torque eventually stops scaling linearly with current is the nonlinear $B$-$H$ curve and saturation, not hysteresis by itself.
- The usual torque constant is effectively the small-signal slope near the origin; high-current operation can deviate.

4. Brushed DC Model and MOR

The brushed DC warm-up model is:

$$
V = iR + Kb\omega + L\frac{di}{dt}
$$

For normal operating-region sketches, the lecture usually neglects the inductive term:

$$
V \approx iR + Kb\omega
$$

Combining this with voltage and current limits gives a MOR in the speed-current or speed-torque plane. The lecture emphasizes:
- The MOR is not a symmetric hexagon made by mirroring a one-quadrant datasheet plot.
- Voltage limits are hard; current limits are semi-hard because they usually represent thermal/core-saturation limits.
- Negative-power regions matter for dynamic robots because braking a moving joint can regenerate energy into the battery.
- Real batteries are not ideal voltage sources, so internal resistance shifts the apparent limit under large discharge or regeneration.
- A URDF-style rectangle using only max torque and max RPM can be badly wrong near high speed, where back EMF consumes the available voltage budget.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, brushed DC/MOR discussion; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 1.pdf, MOR slides.

5. SPMSM d-q Voltage Equations

The SPMSM model uses two electrical degrees of freedom because the motor has three phase terminals but Kirchhoff constraints remove one independent degree. The d-q frame rewrites phase variables into a rotor-aligned two-axis coordinate system.

The rotor-frame SPMSM voltage equations are the central object:

$$
v{ds}^{r} = Rs i{ds}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{ds}^{r}}{dt} - \omegar Ls i{qs}^{r}
$$

$$
v{qs}^{r} = Rs i{qs}^{r} + Ls\frac{d i{qs}^{r}}{dt} + \omegar Ls i{ds}^{r} + \omegar\phif
$$

The cross-coupling terms are the key difference from the brushed DC model. If the coupling is ignored, the model collapses toward the simpler brushed DC operating-region picture. With the coupling kept, the SPMSM MOR becomes curved, and field weakening can extend the high-speed region.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slide 2; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 452-482.

6. Winding and Parameter Conventions

Lecture 2 adds a caution to motor-winding-configuration: when a datasheet reports resistance or inductance, first check whether it is phase, terminal-to-terminal, wye-equivalent, or delta branch value. The d-q equations use phase values $Rs$ and $Ls$ under the chosen convention.

The slides also ask whether a black-box motor's wye/delta winding can be distinguished using only the external ABC terminals. The lecture frames this as homework rather than giving the answer directly.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링.md, lines 227-253; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 4 and 7.

7. Homework / Study Tasks

The homework-oriented slides ask the student to:
- derive the d-q voltage equations from ABC phase equations
- explain why the flux/inductance terms use trigonometric structure
- understand Park/Clarke transformation conventions
- identify which d-q variables are convention-dependent rather than directly measured physical variables
- reason about which equations survive magnetic saturation and which are only linear approximations
- estimate torque constant from back-EMF / speed constant using only an oscilloscope-style measurement setup

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - slides part 2.pdf, slides 2, 4, and 7; sources/lectures/lecture-02/Lecture 2. SPMSM 모델링 - transcript.md, lines 188-217 and 473-482.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar

Sources: Lecture 3 Notion export, transcript, homework-solution slide PDF, motor-driver seminar slide PDF, and homework PDF.

Lecture 3 should be treated as two related but separate blocks, not as one continuous lecture flow.

| Block | Treat as | Main route |
|---|---|---|
| Lecture 3A | Lecture 2 homework/modeling review: torque constant, wye/delta equivalence, waveform assumptions, d-q conventions, and linear magnetic approximations | lecture-02-homework-workthrough |
| Lecture 3B | Juminchan's separate motor-driver/current-control seminar: inverter, SVPWM, current sensing, d-q PI loop, and driver nonidealities | lecture-03-study-path |

The transcript explicitly closes the homework review before introducing Juminchan at about 37:05, and the following section is framed as a motor-driver seminar on PMSM/IPMSM current control.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 316-340.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
Core Question

For Lecture 3B:

How does a torque command become real phase voltage/current in a PMSM driver, and what hardware effects break the ideal model?

The short chain is:

$$
v \rightarrow i \rightarrow \lambda \rightarrow \tau
$$

For an ideal SPMSM, torque is mainly controlled through q-axis current:

$$
\tau \approx \frac{3}{2}p\phif iq
$$

where $p$ is pole-pair count and $\phif$ is permanent-magnet flux linkage. The driver therefore regulates current by commanding motor voltage through an inverter.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 352-355; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
1. Lecture 3A: Lecture 2 Homework Solutions

The first slide deck answers several modeling questions that were left open in Lecture 2.

Torque constant without a torque sensor. The lecture says to measure back EMF instead of torque. Torque constant and back-EMF constant are tied by the motor equations, so an oscilloscope measurement of line-to-line back EMF while spinning the motor can identify $\phif$, then $Kt$. The waveform frequency gives electrical speed; the waveform amplitude, after the chosen phase/line and transform convention is handled, gives the voltage term proportional to $\omegae\phif$.

Wye vs delta from the outside. A black-box stator cannot be distinguished as wye or delta using only the three external ABC terminals. Thevenin-equivalent networks can present the same external behavior. For modeling, use a consistent equivalent-circuit convention, commonly a serial wye-equivalent model, and keep parameter definitions explicit.

Sinusoidal flux is not guaranteed by nature. Three phases are separated by $120^\circ$ electrical, but the waveform does not have to be sinusoidal. Distributed windings and flux-leakage effects can make back EMF close to sinusoidal; trapezoidal BLDC-style machines deliberately differ.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
d-q variables are convention-dependent. The lecture's practical rule is that it is acceptable if the forward and inverse transforms are consistent: $x = \mathrm{Park}(\mathrm{Clarke}(x))$ under the selected convention. Many modeling and firmware mistakes come from mixing conventions.

The d-q voltage equations contain approximations. Ideal SPMSM can use $Ld = Lq$, but saliency, IPMSM geometry, rotor-angle-dependent inductance, and current-dependent saturation can break that equality. The linear flux-linkage relation is the approximation to watch.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 25-33; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 29-74, 123-161, and 290-316; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 1.pdf, slides 4-5 and 12-13.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
2. Lecture 3B: Juminchan Motor Driver Seminar

The motor-driver material after the transition is Juminchan's separate practical seminar. It should be connected to the actuator-design and SPMSM-modeling sequence as a driver/current-control branch, while keeping Lecture 3A as the homework-review closure for Lecture 2.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 19-21 and 135-139; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - transcript.md, lines 324-340.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
3. Motor Driver as Current Controller

The motor driver is not just a power amplifier. It is a current-control system that uses voltage commands to force measured phase currents to track desired values.

See motor-driver-inverter for the inverter view and motor-current-control for the d-q PI-loop view.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 171-181; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slide 4.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
4. Inverter and PWM

Real drivers usually do not synthesize smooth analog phase voltages. They switch MOSFETs rapidly and use PWM. The motor winding is a large inductor, so the applied voltage pulses produce smoother current than the voltage waveform itself.

A three-phase inverter is built from three half-bridges, or six switches. Its legal switch states form eight space vectors:
- two zero-voltage vectors, $(0,0,0)$ and $(1,1,1)$
- six active voltage vectors

High-side MOSFETs require gate-drive circuitry because their source can sit near $V{dc}$; the gate must still be driven above the source. The lecture contrasts older bootstrap circuits with charge-pump gate drivers.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
5. SVPWM and Voltage Margin

SVPWM combines adjacent active vectors and zero vectors over each switching period to create the desired average voltage vector. Splitting the zero-vector time symmetrically reduces current ripple, and lower current ripple means lower torque ripple.

For normal non-overmodulated SVPWM, the usable voltage region is the inscribed circle inside the space-vector hexagon. The lecture recommends not using the full theoretical boundary; leave roughly 1-5% phase-voltage margin. That margin matters because it:
- avoids overmodulation vibration
- preserves current-sensing windows for low-side shunts
- shrinks the practical motor operating region

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
6. Current Sensing

The lecture focuses on triple-shunt low-side current sensing because it is low-cost and practical.

The signal path is:

$$
i \rightarrow V{shunt}=iR{shunt} \rightarrow \text{op-amp} \rightarrow \text{ADC}
$$

Important implementation details:
- shunt resistors are usually around $1-10\ \mathrm{m\Omega}$
- low-side shunts have a convenient ground reference
- high-side or in-line shunts need better common-mode rejection
- in-line sensing is electrically cleaner but more expensive
- Hall sensors can be attractive in high-current drivers because shunt heating changes resistance
- low-side sensing only works when the relevant low-side FET is on
- ADC samples must be synchronized and placed inside the valid sensing window

Noise, op-amp gain, low-pass filters, ADC scaling, Clarke/Park convention, and ground bounce can all corrupt the current estimate. See motor-current-sensing.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver.md, lines 185-223; sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 15-18.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
7. Current Control PI Loop

Ignoring speed-dependent terms at first, each d-q current channel looks like a first-order electrical plant:

$$
G(s)=\frac{i}{V}=\frac{1}{Ls+R}
$$

The lecture uses a PI controller with pole-zero cancellation:

$$
KP = L\omega{des}
$$

$$
KI = R\omega{des}
$$

The resulting closed-loop form is a first-order low-pass response:

$$
Gs(s)=\frac{\omega{des}}{s+\omega{des}}
$$

The speed-dependent cross-coupling and back-EMF terms can be added as feedforward. In practice, the lecture recommends tuning from small gains and starting without feedforward, because incorrect feedforward can make debugging harder.

The command voltage must be saturated to the available inverter voltage, and the integrator needs anti-windup. The lecture mentions backward Euler integration and back-calculation anti-windup.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 21 and 24-25.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
8. Real Inverter Nonidealities

The ideal command voltage is not the real motor terminal voltage:

$$
V{ref} \neq V{real}
$$

Lecture 3B flags the main causes:
- sensor noise and low-pass-filter delay
- discrete-time control delay
- MOSFET/gate-driver dead time
- body-diode voltage drop during dead time
- MOSFET turn-on/off delay
- gate charge and switching ringing from parasitic capacitance/inductance
- PCB layout and ground-bounce problems

Current feedback can often tolerate these errors, but voltage-model-based sensorless control is more sensitive because it depends on the actual applied voltage. See motor-driver-nonidealities.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - slides part 2.pdf, slides 26-31.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
Homework Assigned

The Lecture 3 homework asks for:
- derivation of copper loss and torque-constant formulas, including where the linear magnetic approximation enters
- SPMSM MOR plotting from d-q voltage equations, including whether $id=0$ is optimal at all speeds
- resistive braking current and torque when all three phase terminals are shorted

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-03/Lecture 3. Motor Driver - homework.pdf.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
headingPath: Lecture 3: 3A Homework Review + 3B Motor Driver Seminar
chunks.text:
Related

Lecture 3A / modeling review:
- lecture-02-homework-workthrough
- motor-constant
- motor-constant-unit-conversions
- wye-delta-parameter-conventions
- abc-to-dq-transform-derivation
- spmsm-dq-voltage-equations
- spmsm-torque-equation
- spmsm-linear-magnetic-assumptions

Lecture 3B / Juminchan motor-driver seminar:
- motor-driver-inverter
- space-vector-pwm
- motor-current-sensing
- motor-current-control
- motor-driver-nonidealities
- motor-operating-region

Lecture 4: Actuator Design Process

Sources: Lecture 4 Notion export, transcript, and slide PDF.

Lecture 4 shifts from motor modeling to a practical actuator design workflow. It covers the first half of the design process: requirements, torque-mass-inertia selection, and reducer type selection. Reducer detailed design and structural-component design are deferred.

Core Question

Given a robot concept, how do we choose actuator requirements before designing or selecting hardware?

The lecture's process is:

1. define requirements
2. define torque-mass-inertia (T-M-I)
3. select reducer type
4. design reducer
5. design structural components

Figure: Lecture 4 actuator design process. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 2.

1. Define Requirements Before Optimization

Requirements must be chosen before actuator optimization. Lecture 4 distinguishes two different activities:
- choosing where to sit on the current Pareto front
- pushing the Pareto front outward by better topology, materials, manufacturing, or reducer quality

The lecture uses the mountain-bike analogy: decide whether the product is an MTB or road bike before optimizing materials and bearings. For robot actuators, this means deciding what kind of robot and task envelope you want before trying to improve hardware quality.

See actuator-requirement-selection.

2. Primary vs Secondary Requirements

Lecture 4 states that actuator primary requirements are:
- mass
- rated torque
- joint/output inertia
- peak or saturation torque

These are fundamentally coupled. Improving one generally worsens at least one other at the same design/manufacturing level.

Secondary requirements include:
- backlash
- volume
- stiffness
- velocity / power

The lecture calls these secondary because their limits are practical rather than fundamental in the same way: manufacturing precision, driver voltage/current capability, assembly quality, and cost can improve them without the same clean Maxwell/F=ma coupling.

3. Corrected Normalized Actuator Metric

Lecture 4 explicitly says the earlier normalized actuator metric was corrected again. The slide gives the current relationship as:

$$
C \approx \frac{Km^2\tau{sat}^2}{M I^2}
$$

where $Km = \frac{Kt}{\sqrt{Rs}}$, $\tau{sat}$ is torque limited by core saturation, $M$ is copper mass, and $I$ is joint inertia.

For off-the-shelf frameless motors, $Km$ and $\tau{sat}$ are usually coupled by the motor family/design. That reduces practical selection to torque, mass, and inertia, with one relationship constraining them. See actuator-constant, motor-constant, and actuator-performance-metrics.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 65-117; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slide 6.

4. T-M-I Selection Workflow

For a QDD-based anthropomorphic manipulator, the lecture gives a distal-to-proximal workflow:

1. define kinematic parameters
2. set rated and peak payload
3. define wrist actuator T-M-I
4. define forearm mass
5. define elbow actuator T-M-I
6. define upper-arm mass
7. define shoulder actuator T-M-I

If the system becomes too heavy or fails the goal, iterate backward. If iteration cannot meet the target, lower the goal or push the Pareto front with better motor, material, or reducer design.

Torque is set from payload and child-link mass using Jacobian transpose force mapping:

$$
\tau = J^T F
$$

For fixed-base industrial manipulators, the design often considers worst configuration. For mobile anthropomorphic manipulators, the lecture argues it is acceptable to consider favorable configurations because the base/body can move to make the task mechanically reasonable.

See torque-mass-inertia-selection.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 150-155 and 223-229; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 7 and 10-11.

5. Static vs Dynamic Design

For static QDD manipulator design with off-the-shelf frameless motors:
- lighter motors are better
- maximize reduction ratio within the QDD/backdrivability limit
- check joint velocity under the given battery voltage
- reducer mass depends mainly on peak joint/output torque, not motor torque

For dynamic design, the metric is not maximum speed but maximum angular acceleration. In a 1-DOF system, acceleration is maximized when reflected rotor inertia equals link inertia:

$$
N^2Jr = J{link}
$$

Below that critical ratio, both static torque and dynamic acceleration can be worse. Above it, static performance improves while dynamic performance trades off.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process.md, lines 231-239; sources/lectures/lecture-04/Lecture 4. Actuator Optimal Design - Actuator Design Process - slides.pdf, slides 12-13.

6. Reducer Type Selection

Lecture 4 covers common robot reducer families:
- single-stage planetary drive
- two-stage planetary drive
- 3K compound planetary drive
- cycloidal drive
- harmonic drive

Reducer primary requirements are torque density and efficiency; these have a fundamental trade-off. Backlash is important in robotics, but the lecture treats it as secondary because precision/manufacturing and preload decisions dominate it.

See robot-actuator-reducer-selection.

7. Backdrivability Is Ambiguous

The lecture warns that "backdrivability" is used loosely. It can mean:
- backward efficiency
- reverse-drive starting torque / Coulomb friction
- reflected joint inertia

For this wiki, use backdrivability mainly for friction/reverse-drive behavior, and discuss reflected inertia separately.

Related
- actuator-requirement-selection
- torque-mass-inertia-selection
- jacobian-transpose-force-mapping
- robot-actuator-reducer-selection
- backdrivability
- actuator-constant
- motor-constant
- actuator-performance-metrics
- transmission-transparency

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant

This source bundle contains a Lecture 3 homework-solution prelude and Lecture 5, "Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant (Part 1)." The source files are organized under sources/lectures/lecture-05/; their contents remain unchanged.

Sources: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md and sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf.

> WARNING
> The transcript has a large gap from 22:48 to 54:56, caused by recording trouble. The pages covering magnetic co-energy, flux linkage setup, and part of the winding-function derivation are therefore summarized from the slide deck and the later resumed transcript rather than from continuous spoken explanation.
> Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 220-240.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
Homework Solution Prelude

The first twelve slides are Lecture 3 homework solutions. The durable items for the wiki are:
- The motor operating region (MOR) homework uses the SPMSM d-q voltage equation, ignores heating and magnetic saturation, and compares the $id=0$ boundary against a field-weakening-current (FWC) boundary.
- The example parameters are $V{DC}=100.8\,\mathrm{V}$, 30 poles, terminal-to-terminal $R{tt}=0.42\,\Omega$, $L{tt}=1.58\,\mathrm{mH}$, and $Kt=0.5881\,\mathrm{Nm/A}$.
- The plotted solution shows that $id=0$ is not the best high-speed strategy; choosing negative d-axis current extends the feasible q-axis current region at high electrical speed.

Figure: MOR homework prompt and parameters. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 5.

Figure: $id=0$ boundary compared with the field-weakening-current boundary. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 9.

Problem 3 introduces resistive braking as a safety behavior: shorting or otherwise resistively loading the motor can remove kinetic energy quickly and create braking current. The lecture frames this as a HOUND2 safety behavior for stopping a leg before self-collision or damage.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
Figure: resistive-braking q-axis current plot and HOUND2 demonstration image. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 12.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 46-54; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 5-12.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
Lecture Goal

Lecture 5 asks why an analytical torque-constant model matters. Physical testing can measure $Kt$ after fabrication, and finite-element analysis can estimate it numerically, but a design workflow needs a low-dimensional hand model before testing every candidate geometry. The lecture's target is to estimate SPMSM torque constant from cross-section geometry, axial length, magnet grade, and winding pattern before manufacturing or full FEM.

The slide-level result is:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

where $kw$ is winding factor, $t$ is magnet thickness, $g$ is effective air gap, $Br$ is magnet remanence, $2N$ is turns per phase, $R$ is air-gap radius, and $l$ is axial length.

Figure: analytical torque-constant target formula and variable definitions. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 20.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 52-72 and 86-90; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 13 and 20.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
Motional EMF and Voltage Are Different
chunks.text:
Motional EMF and Voltage Are Different

The lecture revisits $e=Blv$ from a moving-conductor viewpoint. The important distinction is that $e$ is electromotive force, not ordinary terminal voltage. In the lecture's examples, a rod moving through a uniform magnetic field can have an induced EMF between its ends, but current does not necessarily flow unless the physical circuit provides a return path. The rail example is assigned as a way to separate EMF, voltage, resistance, and the chosen stationary reference frame.

This matters because the later motional-EMF derivation treats each conductor segment in the motor as a small generator, then accumulates those contributions into phase flux linkage and torque.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 95-191.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
From EMF to Flux Linkage

After the transcript gap, the lecture resumes by saying the derivation had just computed EMF, while the desired quantity was flux linkage. The bridge is that EMF is the time derivative of flux linkage, so integrating the motional-EMF result gives the flux-linkage form.

For the simplified radial-field model, the relevant overlap is:

$$
\lambda(\theta)
=
R l \int0^{2\pi}
Br(\phi-\theta)N(\phi)\,d\phi
$$

Only the radial component of the air-gap flux density is retained in this model. The winding function $N(\phi)$ acts like the angular window that counts how much conductor linkage sees each part of the rotor magnet field.

Figure: conductor density and winding function for the simplified SPMSM cross-section. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 26.

Figure: radial magnet field waveform overlapped with the winding function. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 29.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 271-316; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 26-30.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
Winding Factor and Pole-Count Insight

The derivation first uses an ideal concentrated full-pitch winding with winding factor $kw=1$, then introduces $kw$ as the efficiency of a real winding pattern relative to that ideal. The lecture's practical interpretation is that a winding factor below 1 means some conductor EMF contributions partially cancel instead of adding perfectly.

The most important design warning is about pole count: increasing pole-pair count does not directly multiply torque constant if total conductor count and effective linkage are fixed. More poles can still help real motors by enabling shorter end turns, better practical concentrated windings, thinner magnetic paths, and lower mass, but the final ideal formula does not contain pole-pair count as an independent torque-constant multiplier.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 318-424; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slides 31-34.

chunks.pageTitle: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
headingPath: Lecture 5: Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant
chunks.text:
Torque Result

For three balanced phases with $id=0$, the slide result sums the phase torques into:

$$
\tau = 3 Bm R l N iq
$$

and then substitutes the simplified magnet field amplitude $Bm \approx \frac{t}{g}Br$ and real-winding correction $kw$ to obtain:

$$
Kt = 3 kw \frac{t}{g} Br N R l
$$

Figure: three-phase torque summation and the final analytical $Kt$ expression. Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.

The lecture closes by framing this as one half of a larger actuator-limit argument. Mass and inertia can be computed directly from geometry, while torque constant and saturation torque need electromagnetic modeling. Lecture 5 handles the EMF/flux-linkage side; magnetic-circuit modeling and saturation are deferred. In the broader wiki, this geometry-level $Kt$ result feeds motor-constant and then actuator-constant rather than replacing either page.

Source: sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - transcript.md, lines 382-386 and 468-483; sources/lectures/lecture-05/Lecture 5. Analytical Calculation of Torque Constant - slides.pdf, slide 32.
